https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/5/24/armenia-returns-four-border-villages-to-azerbaijan-as-part-of-deal|title = Armenia returns four border villages to Azerbaijan as part of deal |publisher= Al Jazeera |date=24 May 2024 |access-date= 28 May 2024}} *[[Armenia–Azerbaijan peace agreement]] signed on 8 August 2025 | combatant1 = '''{{flag|Artsakh}}''' ('''Nagorno-Karabakh'''; until 2023){{efn|Formal dissolution on 1 January 2024}}{{efn|[[Nagorno-Karabakh Autonomous Oblast]] (NKAO) until 1991.}}

''[[#Diplomatic support|Diplomatic support]]'' | combatant2 = '''{{flag|Azerbaijan}}''' (from 1991)
'''{{flag|Soviet Union}}''' (until 1991){{efn|"Throughout the Soviet period, Moscow supported the Azerbaijani authorities against Armenian secessionists." "Until the dissolution of the USSR, the Soviet authorities sided, in general, with Azerbaijan. [...] Soviet troops sent to the conflict area [...] on numerous occasions, took the side of the Azerbaijani forces to 'punish' the Armenians for raising the NK issue." "Soviet troops have been in Nagorno-Karabakh for 2 1/2 years [...] The troops support armed Azerbaijani militias who have imposed a blockade of the region..."{{cite news |last1=Shogren |first1=Elizabeth |title=Armenians Wage Hunger Strike in Regional Dispute: Soviet Union: Five threaten to starve themselves to death unless Moscow ends military rule in Azerbaijan enclave. |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1990-09-21-mn-730-story.html |work=[[Los Angeles Times]] |date=21 September 1990}} Soviet troops directly intervened during [[Operation Ring]] in April–May 1991 on the Azerbaijani side.{{cite web |last1=Cornell |first1=Svante E. |author-link1=Svante Cornell |title=The Nagorno-Karabakh Conflict |url=https://is.muni.cz/el/fss/jaro2019/POL587/um/Cornell_The_Nagorno-Karabakh_Conflict.pdf |website=Report no. 46, Department of East European Studies |publisher=[[Uppsala University]] |page=26 |date=1999 |quote=Sporadic clashes became frequent by the first months of 1991, with an ever-increasing organization of paramilitary forces on the Armenian side, whereas Azerbaijan still relied on the support of Moscow. [...] In response to this development, a joint Soviet and Azerbaijani military and police operation directed from Moscow was initiated in these areas during the Spring and Summer of 1991.}}{{cite journal |last1=Papazian |first1=Taline |title=State at War, State in War: The Nagorno-Karabakh Conflict and State-Making in Armenia, 1991–1995 |journal=The Journal of Power Institutions in Post-Soviet Societies |date=2008 |issue=8 |page=25 |doi=10.4000/pipss.1623 |url=https://journals.openedition.org/pipss/1623 |quote=... units of the 4th army stationed in Azerbaijan and Azeri OMONs were used in "Operation Ring", to empty a number of Armenian villages in Nagorno-Karabakh in April 1991.|doi-access=free}} It was essentially a "combined Soviet-Azerbaijan operation."{{cite journal |last1=Murphy |first1=David E. |title=Operation 'Ring': The Black Berets in Azerbaijan |journal=[[The Journal of Soviet Military Studies]] |date=1992 |volume=5 |issue=1 |doi=10.1080/13518049208430053 |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/13518049208430053 |page=93 |quote=... Operation 'Ring' as a combined Soviet-Azerbaijan operation to weaken Armenian resistance in the Nagorno-Karabakh enclave.|url-access=subscription }}}} * {{flag|Azerbaijan SSR}} {{flag|Turkey}} (2020) (alleged by Armenia){{cite news |title=Принуждение к конфликту |trans-title=Coercion to conflict |url=https://www.kommersant.ru/doc/4537733 |work=[[Kommersant]] |language=Russian |date=16 October 2020 |access-date=21 April 2021}}{{cite news |last1=Kramer |first1=Andrew E. |title=Armenia and Azerbaijan: What Sparked War and Will Peace Prevail? |url=https://www.nytimes.com/article/armenian-azerbaijan-conflict.html |access-date=4 March 2021 |work=[[The New York Times]]|date=29 January 2021 |quote=Armenia has said that Turkey was directly involved in the fighting in and around Nagorno-Karabakh, and that a Turkish F-16 fighter shot down an Armenian jet. Turkey denied those accusations.}}{{cite news |last1=Tsvetkova |first1=Maria |last2=Auyezov |first2=Olzhas |date=9 November 2020 |title=Analysis: Russia and Turkey keep powder dry in Nagorno-Karabakh conflict |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-armenia-azerbaijan-allies-analysis/analysis-russia-and-turkey-keep-powder-dry-in-nagorno-karabakh-conflict-idUSKBN27P0MN |work=[[Reuters]] |access-date=4 March 2021 |quote=Turkey's support for Azerbaijan has been vital, and Azerbaijan's superior weaponry and battlefield advances have reduced its incentive to reach a lasting peace deal. Ankara denies its troops are involved in fighting but Aliyev has acknowledged some Turkish F-16 fighter jets remained in Azerbaijan after a military drill this summer, and there are reports of Russian and Turkish drones being used by both sides.}}

44 killed (2021–2022)See [[#2021–2022|here]] | notes = | campaignbox = {{Campaignbox Nagorno-Karabakh conflict}} }} The '''Nagorno-Karabakh conflict'''{{efn|Also called the '''Karabakh conflict''',{{cite book |last1=Rezvani |first1=Babak |title=Conflict and Peace in Central Eurasia: Towards Explanations and Understandings |date=2014 |publisher=Brill |isbn=978-9004276369 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=juziBQAAQBAJ&dq=Karabakh+territorial+conflict&pg=PA159 159] |quote=The Karabakh conflict is an ethno-territorial conflict....}} '''Armenia–Azerbaijan conflict''', or '''Armenian–Azerbaijani conflict'''. Usually referred to as the '''Artsakh conflict''' in Armenia{{cite web |title=Armenia, Artsakh Security Councils hold joint session in Yerevan |url=https://www.primeminister.am/en/statements-and-messages/item/2019/12/23/Nikol-Pashinyan-meeting-Security-Council/ |website=primeminister.am |publisher=The Prime Minister of the Republic of Armenia |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200805205942/https://www.primeminister.am/en/statements-and-messages/item/2019/12/23/Nikol-Pashinyan-meeting-Security-Council/ |archive-date=5 August 2020 |date=23 December 2019 |quote=...the peaceful resolution of the Artsakh conflict.}} and the '''Armenia-Azerbaijan Nagorno-Karabakh conflict''' in Azerbaijan.{{cite web |title=Armenia-Azerbaijan Nagorno-Karabakh conflict |url=https://nk.gov.az/en/page/69/ |website=nk.gov.az |publisher=[[Cabinet of Azerbaijan|Cabinet of Ministers of the Republic of Azerbaijan]] |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200817115852/https://nk.gov.az/en/page/69/ |archive-date=17 August 2020}}}} is an [[ethnic conflict|ethnic]] and [[Territorial dispute|territorial]] conflict between [[Armenia]] and [[Azerbaijan]] over the region of [[Nagorno-Karabakh]], inhabited mostly by ethnic Armenians until 2023, and [[Armenian-occupied territories surrounding Nagorno-Karabakh|seven surrounding districts]], inhabited mostly by [[Azerbaijanis]] until their expulsion during the 1990s. The Nagorno-Karabakh region was entirely claimed by and partially controlled by the [[List of states with limited recognition|breakaway]] [[Republic of Artsakh]], but was recognized internationally as part of Azerbaijan. Azerbaijan gradually re-established control over Nagorno-Karabakh region and the seven surrounding districts. Throughout the Soviet period, Armenians in the [[Nagorno-Karabakh Autonomous Oblast]] were heavily discriminated against. The [[Soviet Azerbaijan]]i authorities suppressed Armenian culture and identity in Nagorno-Karabakh, pressured Armenians to leave the region, and encouraged Azerbaijanis to settle within it, although Armenians remained the majority population.{{sfn|Starovoytova|1997|p=24}} Motivated by fears of cultural and physical erasure under [[Anti-Armenian sentiment in Azerbaijan#Suppression of Armenian culture in Nagorno-Karabakh|government policies from Azerbaijan]],{{Cite book |last1=Chorbajian |first1=Levon |title=The Caucasian knot: the history & geopolitics of Nagorno-Karabagh |last2=Donabédian |first2=Patrick |last3=Mutafian |first3=Claude |date=1994 |publisher=Atlantic Highlands, NJ : Zed Books |isbn=978-1-85649-287-4 |series=Politics in contemporary Asia |location=London |page=37}}{{Cite book |last=De Waal |first=Thomas |title=Black garden: Armenia and Azerbaijan through peace and war |date=2004 |publisher=New York Univ. Press |isbn=978-0-8147-1945-9 |edition=1st publ. in paperback |location=New York |pages=10–12}}{{cite book |author=Eduard Abrahamyan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=951ZEQAAQBAJ&pg=PA1964 |title=Small States, Russia and the West Polarity, Constellations and Heterogeneity in the Geopolitics of the Caucasus |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2025 |isbn=978-10-4035016-4 |pages=1964 |quote=The violence prompted the Armenian society of the crumbling Soviet Union of 1990-1991 to act in self-defence, perceiving a 'tangible prospect of looming genocide in Artsakh.'}}, the [[Karabakh movement|Karabakh Movement]] advocated for the reunification ("[[miatsum]]") of the enclave with [[Armenian Soviet Socialist Republic|Soviet Armenia]]. A referendum in 1988 was held to transfer the region to [[Soviet Armenia]], citing [[self-determination]] laws in the [[1977 Constitution of the Soviet Union|Soviet constitution]].{{efn|According to the [[Constitution of the USSR]], if a [[Republics of the Soviet Union|union republic]] voted to leave the [[Soviet Union]], its [[Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republics|autonomous republics]], [[Oblasts of the Soviet Union|autonomous oblasts]], and [[Autonomous okrugs of the Soviet Union|autonomous okrugs]] had the right to hold their own [[referendum]]s to independently decide whether to remain in the USSR or to leave alongside the seceding union republic. They also had the right to raise questions regarding their own state-legal status.{{cite web|url=https://docs.cntd.ru/document/902002993|title=СОЮЗ СОВЕТСКИХ СОЦИАЛИСТИЧЕСКИХ РЕСПУБЛИК. ЗАКОН О порядке решения вопросов, связанных с выходом союзной республики из СССР|language=ru|access-date=13 June 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160912024819/https://docs.cntd.ru/document/902002993|archive-date=12 September 2016}}}} This act was met with a [[Anti-Armenian sentiment in Azerbaijan#During the Karabakh Movement (1988-1992)|series of pogroms against Armenians across Azerbaijan]], before violence committed against both Armenians and Azerbaijanis occurred.{{sfn|Starovoytova|1997|p=vi}} The conflict escalated into a full-scale [[First Nagorno-Karabakh War|war in the early 1990s]] following the [[dissolution of the Soviet Union]]. The war was won by [[Republic of Artsakh|Artsakh]] and Armenia, and led to occupation of regions around Soviet-era Nagorno-Karabakh. Azerbaijan and Turkey responded with a transportation and economic [[Turkish–Azeri blockade of Armenia (1989–ongoing)|blockade of Armenia]] which persists to this day, while [[Republic of Artsakh|Artsakh]] was also blockaded until 2023. There were expulsions of ethnic Armenians from Azerbaijan and ethnic Azerbaijanis from Armenia and the Armenian-controlled areas.{{cite book |editor=M. Hakan Yavuz, Michael Gunter |title=The Nagorno-Karabakh Conflict: Historical and Political Perspectives |publisher=Routledge |year=2022 |page=131 |isbn=978-1000608496}} The [[Bishkek Protocol|ceasefire ending the war]], signed in 1994 in [[Bishkek]], was followed by two decades of relative stability, which significantly deteriorated in the 2010s. A [[2016 Nagorno-Karabakh conflict|four-day escalation]] in April 2016 resulted in hundreds of casualties but only minor changes to the [[Line of Contact (Nagorno-Karabakh)|front line]]. In late 2020, the large-scale [[Second Nagorno-Karabakh War]] resulted in thousands of casualties and a significant Azerbaijani victory. The hostilities subsided with a tripartite [[2020 Nagorno-Karabakh ceasefire agreement|ceasefire agreement]], under which Azerbaijan captured one-third of Nagorno-Karabakh and regained control over the occupied territories surrounding the region.{{Cite web |last=Geybullayeva |first=Arzu |date=19 July 2023 |title=Armenia and Azerbaijan: A blockade that never ended and a peace deal hanging by a thread |url=https://globalvoices.org/2023/07/19/armenia-and-azerbaijan-a-blockade-that-never-ended-and-a-peace-deal-hanging-by-a-thread/ |url-status=live |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20250725010718/https://globalvoices.org/2023/07/19/armenia-and-azerbaijan-a-blockade-that-never-ended-and-a-peace-deal-hanging-by-a-thread/ |archive-date=25 July 2025 |access-date=24 July 2023 |website=Global Voices |language=en |quote=Following the second Karabakh war in 2020, Azerbaijan regained control over much of the previously occupied seven regions. Azerbaijan also captured one-third of Karabakh itself during the war}}{{Cite journal |last=Cheterian |first=Vicken |date=20 October 2022 |title=Technological determinism or strategic advantage? Comparing the two Karabakh Wars between Armenia and Azerbaijan |journal=Journal of Strategic Studies |language=en |volume=47 |issue=2 |pages=214–237 |doi=10.1080/01402390.2022.2127093 |issn=0140-2390 |s2cid=253061240 |quote=The Armenian side also lost territories within the former NKAO, namely the district of Hadrut and the strategic town of Shusha/Shushi, areas that were not even considered for handover to Azerbaijan during the long years of diplomatic negotiations between the two wars. |doi-access=free}} [[Peacekeeping operations in Nagorno-Karabakh#Ceasefire violations|Ceasefire violations in Nagorno-Karabakh]] and on the [[Armenia–Azerbaijan border crisis (2021–present)|Armenian–Azerbaijani border]] continued following the 2020 war. Between 2022 and 2023, Azerbaijan escalated its blockade of Nagorno-Karabakh using a military checkpoint, sabotaging civilian infrastructure,* {{cite web |title=Why Are There No Armenians in Nagorno-Karabakh? |url=https://freedomhouse.org/report/special-report/2024/why-are-there-no-armenians-nagorno-karabakh |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240904222706/https://freedomhouse.org/report/special-report/2024/why-are-there-no-armenians-nagorno-karabakh |archive-date=4 September 2024 |work=[[Freedom House]] |language=en |quote=The blockade was accompanied by the regular cutting of the supply of gas and electricity, as well as disrupting lines of communication, such as telephone landlines, cellular signals, and internet. Azerbaijani state actors regularly shut down the supply of gas to Nagorno-Karabakh during the first four months of the blockade...On January 9, 2023, the high-voltage cable from Armenia to Nagorno-Karabakh, the main source of electricity, was damaged in the territory of the Lachin Corridor. Azerbaijani officials did not allow any repair work on it, and the supply of electricity was never restored.}} * {{cite web |date=1 February 2023 |title=Statement Condemning the Azerbaijani Blockade of the Artsakh (Nagorno-Karabakh) |url=https://genocidescholars.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/IAGS-EB-AB-Statement-on-Azeri-Blockade-of-Artsakh.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20230321152131/https://genocidescholars.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/IAGS-EB-AB-Statement-on-Azeri-Blockade-of-Artsakh.pdf |archive-date=21 March 2023 |website=International Association of Genocide Scholars}} * {{cite web |date=22 June 2023 |title=Ensuring free and safe access through the Lachin Corridor |url=https://pace.coe.int/en/files/32878/html |website=Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe |quote="85. Calculations of gas flow indicate that the gas goes to the village of Pekh, and not beyond that point, suggesting that a valve has been installed. The blockage can be checked with the level of gas pressure."}} * {{cite web |last=Avetisyan |first=Ani |date=17 January 2023 |title=Nagorno-Karabakh reports gas cut for second time since start of blockade |url=https://oc-media.org/nagorno-karabakh-reports-gas-cut-for-second-time-since-start-of-blockade/ |website=OC Media |language=en-US |quote="Daily two-hour power cuts instituted on 9 January were replaced with four-hour daily power cuts on 17 January."}} and targeting agricultural workers.* {{citation |title=Why Are There No Armenians in Nagorno-Karabakh? |work=Freedom House |url=https://freedomhouse.org/report/special-report/2024/why-are-there-no-armenians-nagorno-karabakh |language=en |quote=In addition to creating the blockade, Azerbaijan took deliberate measures to instill a sense of insecurity in Nagorno-Karabakh. The shootings near the line of contact continued, targeting people carrying out agricultural work, which was needed for self-sustenance during the blockade.}} * {{citation |last1=ռ/կ |first1=Ազատություն |title=Armenian PM Reacts To Reported Shooting At Farmers In Karabakh |date=14 November 2022 |work=Ազատություն ռ/կ |url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/32129537.html Jamnews |language=en-US}} The ten-month-long [[Blockade of Nagorno-Karabakh|military siege]] isolated the region from the outside world.* {{cite web |title=Nagorno-Karabakh authorities warn of "critical" situation as Azerbaijan bans Red Cross vehicles {{!}} Eurasianet |url=https://eurasianet.org/nagorno-karabakh-authorities-warn-of-critical-situation-as-azerbaijan-bans-red-cross-vehicles House |pages=28–30 |language=en}} * {{cite news |last1=Badalian |first1=Susan |last2=Sahakian |first2=Nane |date=16 June 2023 |title=Relief Supplies To Karabakh Blocked By Baku for the second consecutive day on Friday, aggravating a humanitarian crisis in the Armenian-populated region effectively cut off from the outside world since December."}} In September 2023, Azerbaijan launched a large-scale [[2023 Azerbaijani offensive in Nagorno-Karabakh|military offensive]],{{Cite news |date=19 September 2023 |title=Azerbaijani forces strike Armenian-controlled Karabakh, raising risk of new Caucasus war |work=Reuters |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/azerbaijan-says-six-its-citizens-were-killed-by-land-mines-karabakh-2023-09-19/}}{{Cite web |date=19 September 2023 |title=Azerbaijan launches attack in Nagorno-Karabakh, announces 'evacuation' of Armenian population operations in Nagorno-Karabakh |url=https://www.france24.com/en/asia-pacific/20230919-azerbaijan-says-it-has-begun-anti-terrorist-operations-in-nagorno-karabakh |access-date=19 September 2023 |publisher=France 24 |language=en}} resulting in the [[Flight of Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians|flight of the entire population]], the dissolution of [[Republic of Artsakh|Artsakh]],{{cite news |date=1 January 2024 |title=Нагорно-Карабахская республика прекратила существование |trans-title=The Nagorno–Karabakh Republic Ceased to Exist |url=https://www.interfax.ru/world/938736 |language=ru}}{{Cite web |date=28 September 2023 |title=Глава Нагорного Карабаха подписал указ о прекращении существования республики |url=https://www.kommersant.ru/doc/6239337 |access-date=28 September 2023 |website=Коммерсантъ |language=ru}} and its incorporation into Azerbaijan. In August 2025, Azerbaijan and Armenia signed a US-brokered [[Armenia–Azerbaijan peace agreement|peace agreement]] in Washington D.C., aiming to end the conflict, reopen transport routes, and normalize relations.{{Cite web |date=2025-08-08 |title=Azerbaijan and Armenia sign peace pledge at White House summit |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c39dzl1lzrgo |access-date=2025-08-24 |website=www.bbc.com |language=en-GB}} ==Background== {{main|History of Nagorno-Karabakh|Armenian–Azerbaijani war (1918–1920)|Armenia–Azerbaijan relations}} Following the breakup of the [[Russian Empire]], the Armenians of Nagorno-Karabakh formed an unrecognised polity known as the [[Karabakh Council]] in 1918. Due to Azerbaijani–British pressure, the Karabakh Council in August 1919 was forced to provisionally recognise the authority of [[Azerbaijan Democratic Republic|Azerbaijan]], pending the [[Paris Peace Conference (1919–1920)|Paris Peace Conference]]'s adjudication of the international borders of the republics within the [[South Caucasus]].{{Cite web |title=Հակամարտության ծագում. 1918–1921 թթ. {{!}} NKR |url=http://www.nkr.am/hy/azerbaijan-karabakh-conflict-history |access-date=21 May 2022 |website=www.nkr.am}} As the peace conference was inconclusive regarding Nagorno-Karabakh, the Azerbaijani governor-general [[Khosrov bey Sultanov]], issued an ultimatum to the Armenians of Karabakh in early 1920, stipulating their acceptance of permanent inclusion into Azerbaijan. Leaders associated with the [[Republic of Mountainous Armenia]] and the [[Dashnak Party]] attempted to organize a rebellion against Azerbaijani rule, which failed and led to the [[Shusha massacre|massacre and displacement]] of [[Shusha]]'s Armenian population.{{Cite book |last=Hovannisian |first=Richard G. |title=The Republic of Armenia: From London to Sèvres, February–August 1920 |date=1996 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |pages=140–52}}{{efn|According to the ''Caucasian Calendar for 1917'', in 1916 Shusha had an Armenian population of 23,396, forming 53.3% of the city's population.{{Cite book |url=https://www.prlib.ru/item/417322 |title=Кавказский календарь на 1917 год |publisher=Tipografiya kantselyarii Ye.I.V. na Kavkaze, kazenny dom |year=1917 |edition=72nd |publication-place=Tiflis |pages=190–197 |language=Russian |trans-title=Caucasian calendar for 1917 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211104233151/https://www.prlib.ru/item/417322 |archive-date=4 November 2021}}}} By 1921, Soviet authorities were in control of Nagorno-Karabakh who decided on the formation of the [[Nagorno-Karabakh Autonomous Oblast]] (NKAO) within [[Soviet Azerbaijan]].{{cite web |url=https://armenian.usc.edu/qa-with-arsene-saparov-no-evidence-that-stalin-gave-karabakh-to-azerbaijan/ |title=Q&A with Arsène Saparov: No Evidence that Stalin 'Gave' Karabakh to Azerbaijan |author= |date=10 December 2018 |website=armenian.usc.edu |quote=Of all the documents I have seen, there is no direct evidence of Stalin doing or saying something in those 12 days in the summer of 1921 that [resulted in this decision on Karabakh]. A lot of people just assume that since Stalin was an evil person, it would be typical of someone evil to take a decision like that.}} In 1964, the Armenians of Karabakh sent a letter to leader of the [[USSR]], [[Nikita Khrushchev]], complaining about Azerbaijan's economic management of the region. They also requested that the NKAO and adjacent regions where the Armenian population is approximately 90% be reincorporated into the [[Armenian SSR]] or integrated into the [[RSFSR]].{{Cite web |date=29 May 2017 |title=Nagorno Karabakh's Petition to Prime Minister Nikita Khrushchev: 19 May, 1964 |url=https://www.aniarc.am/2017/05/29/nagorno-karabakhs-petition-to-prime-minister-nikita-khrushchev-19-may-1964/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221018193549/https://www.aniarc.am/2017/05/29/nagorno-karabakhs-petition-to-prime-minister-nikita-khrushchev-19-may-1964/ |archive-date=18 October 2022 |access-date=16 November 2022 |website=ANI Armenian Research Center |quote=In 1923, within the territorial borders of Azerbaijan, Mountainous Karabakh was given autonomy, and the regions of Shamkhar, Khanlar, Dashkesan and Shahumian, where the Armenian population is predominant (approximately 90 per cent), were directly incorporated into the Azerbaijan SSR. Thus, the Armenians of Karabagh were cut off from the Armenian SSR and Mountainous Karabakh Autonomous Republic failed to include all Armenian regions. The rights of the autonomous region were gradually curtailed and presently are almost entirely abrogated. The Armenian population of the Azerbaijani SSR has been subjected to chauvinistic policies creating extremely unfavorable conditions of life.}} The Nagorno-Karabakh Armenian intellectuals who wrote to the Kremlin were imprisoned, put under surveillance, or forced to leave Nagorno-Karabakh. All had lost their jobs and were removed from the Communist Party.{{cite AV media |date=May 2020 |title=Parts of a Circle I: The Road to War |type=Documentary |language=English |publisher=Media Initiatives Center, Internews Azerbaijan, and the Humanitarian Research Public Union}} {{quote box|quote=I tried to change the demographics in Karabakh. We encouraged Azeris living in neighbouring regions to move to Karabakh instead of to Baku. I made efforts to ensure that there would be more Azerbaijanis and less Armenians living there.|align=right|width=40% |source= —[[Heydar Aliyev]] in a 2002 interview.}} Throughout the Soviet period, [[Azerbaijan Soviet Socialist Republic|Azerbaijani authorities]] implemented policies aimed at diluting the Armenian majority in Nagorno-Karabakh through various means, including border manipulations,{{harvnb|Saparov|2014|p=116}}: "The solution proposed by the [Karabakh uezdy] conference was similar to previous suggestions, but it also called for the creation of a Karabakh Oblast’, a region that would encompass both mountainous and lowland parts of Karabakh. It offered a way in which the creation of a predominantly Armenian autonomous unit in the mountainous part of the region could be avoided. During 1921, the position of the Azerbaijani leadership regarding Karabakh autonomy had been that suppression of banditry in the region would solve the ethnic conflict, and that there would be no need to grant autonomy to the Armenian-populated part of Karabakh. This tactic was successful in the short term."{{harvnb|Chorbajian|Mutafian|Donabedian|1994|pp=138–140, 154}}: "The borders were to be drawn before 15 August by a mixed commission...but without the participation of either Yerevan or Moscow. All would be presided over by Karaiev. Under such circumstances, the Armenians could expect to be grossly disappointed. On the one hand...they excluded, on the west, the 'corridor' made up of Lachin, Kelbajar, and Kedabek, which had been carefully emptied of its Armenian population to separate Mountainous Karabagh from Armenian Zangezur. On the other hand, in the north, without any justification, they removed the districts of Shamkhor, Khanlar, Dashkesan and Shahumian.. where the Armenian population was predominant (about 90 per cent)... From Shamkhor in the north to Shahumian in the south, Armenian villages in these districts have been systematically emptied...Mountainous Karabagh delimited in this way is only a portion of what had always been Armenian Karabagh, which itself is only a part of what was included in the ancient Armenian provinces of Artsakh and Utik...The spectre of ‘Nakhichevanization’ haunts...Mountainous Karabagh, which had 125,000 inhabitants in 1926 who were 89 percent Armenian. This region has become an ‘enclave’ since the ‘cleansing’ of the Hagaru Valley in order to separate Karabagh from Zangezur by a narrow strip emptied of Armenians...Azerbaijan still contained a large Armenian minority. Aside from the ‘bastion’ of the Autonomous Region of Mountainous Karabagh, Armenians were numerous in Baku and in the region north of the Autonomous Region, up to Shamkhor, where the Armenian villages had been deliberately left outside the frontiers drawn in 1923 and, thereby, subjected to direct Azerbaijani authority. From north to south, these areas had already largely been ‘swept clean,’ with the exception of the area of Shahumian, the northern gateway to the Autonomous Region. The Azerbaijani plan was clearly described in the declaration by the Karabagh Committee on 2 December 1988: 'Exploiting the anarchic situation, the Azerbaijani authorities are about to unleash a monstrous programme: to expel Armenians from their several millennia old homes in Gandzak and the areas north of Artsakh, in preparation for an invasion of Mountainous Karabagh.' Already about 120,000 Armenians have left Azerbaijan, and 50,000 have sought refuge in Armenia and the others in the North Caucasus and Central Asia."{{cite book |author=Walker|first=Christopher|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Armenia&pg=PA |title=Armenia: A Very Brief History |publisher=Rouben Galichian, 2022 |year= |isbn=978-9939-68-926-5 |page=41 |quote=The borders between Armenia and Artsakh were manipulated such that Armenia could have no direct link with Artsakh.}} encouraging the exodus of Armenians, and settling Azerbaijanis in the region.{{Cite journal |last=Hein |first=Patrick |date=7 April 2024 |title=From Stalin to the Aliyev clan: 70 years of hindered autonomy in Nagorno-Karabakh |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/sena.12420 |journal=Studies in Ethnicity and Nationalism |volume=24 |issue=2 |pages=197–215 |language=en |doi=10.1111/sena.12420 |issn=1473-8481|url-access=subscription }}{{cite book |author=Patrick Wilson Gore |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tis_Some_Poor_Fellow_s_Skull&pg=PA |title='Tis Some Poor Fellow's Skull Post-Soviet Warfare in the Southern Caucasus |publisher=iUniverse |year=2008 |isbn=978-0-595-48679-3 |page=xii |quote=So after the ethnic cleansing of Nagorno-Karabakh that the Azeris undertook in 1991, Azeris...were settled in formerly Armenian towns and villages to reduce the oblast's overwhelmingly Armenian complexion.}} This[[Anti-Armenian sentiment in Azerbaijan|discrimination against the Armenian population]] were part of a part of a broader strategy to suppress Armenian culture and identity in Nagorno-Karabakh. Former Azerbaijani [[Heydar Aliyev|President Heydar Aliyev]] later admitted to supporting some of these policies.{{Cite journal |last=Panossian |first=Lisabelle |date=1 April 2024 |title=Protecting Humanity's Cradle of Civilization: Advancing the Right to Self-Determination for Indigenous Peoples in the Middle East & South Caucasus |url=https://scholarlycommons.law.northwestern.edu/njihr/vol22/iss3/1 |journal=Northwestern Journal of Human Rights |volume=22 |issue=3 |pages=149 |quote=Azerbaijan’s former president Heydar Aliyev admitted in a 2002 interview that he had tried to increase the number of Azerbaijanis and reduce the number of Armenians living in the region while he was a Soviet Communist Party Administrator.}}{{harvnb|Saparov|2014|p=164}}: "...confirmation of the deliberate demographic change policy pursued by the Azerbaijani authorities in Karabakh comes from a 2002 interview with Azerbaijani president Heidar Aliyev, who in 1969 became the head of Soviet Azerbaijan: 'I talk about a period when I was the First Secretary [of the Azerbaijani CP] and helped a lot at that time with the development of Nagorno-Karabakh....I tried to change the demographics there. Nagorno-Karabakh petitioned for the opening of an institute of higher education there. [In Azerbaijan] everybody was against it. After deliberations, I decided to open one, but on the condition that there would be three sectors—Azerbaijani, Russian, and Armenian. After [the institute] opened, we no longer sent Azerbaijanis from the neighboring regions to Baku [and] instead sent them there [to Karabakh]. [We also] opened a large shoemaking factory there. In Stepanakert itself, there was no workforce, so we sent Azerbaijanis from the surrounding districts. With these and other measures, I tried to increase the number of Azerbaijanis in Nagorno-Karabakh, and the number of Armenians decreased. Those who worked at that time in Nagorno-Karabakh know about it.'" Teaching Armenian history in Nagorno-Karabakh was banned.{{sfn|Starovoytova|1997|p=24}} Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians were persecuted for choosing to attend college in Armenia instead of Azerbaijan or for supporting the [[FC Ararat Yerevan]] football club instead of [[Neftchi Baku]]. The [[1973 Soviet Top League]] final in which Ararat Yerevan defeated [[FC Dynamo Kyiv]] was not aired in Azerbaijan SSR, and Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians that listened to the match by radio were arrested and questioned by the [[KGB]]. Despite these pressures, Armenians remained the majority in Nagorno-Karabakh when the USSR collapsed.{{cite web |last=Starovoytova |first=Galina |date=November 1997 |title=Sovereignty after Empire: Self-Determinationa Movements in the Former Soviet Union |url=https://www.usip.org/sites/default/files/pwks19.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150905002501/https://www.usip.org/sites/default/files/pwks19.pdf |archive-date=5 September 2015 |access-date=5 September 2015 |website=[[United States Institute of Peace]] |page=24}} Census data from the Soviet period reflects these demographic shifts. According to the [[Soviet Census (1979)|1979 Soviet census]], 160,841 Azerbaijanis lived in Armenia, while 352,410 Armenians resided in Azerbaijan outside of Nagorno-Karabakh.{{cite journal |last1=Henze |first1=Paul B. |date=1 January 1991 |title=The demography of the Caucasus according to 1989 Soviet census data |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/02634939108400741?journalCode=ccas20 |journal=Central Asian Survey |volume=10 |issue=1–2 |pages=147–170 |doi=10.1080/02634939108400741 |issn=0263-4937 |access-date=31 December 2021|url-access=subscription }} By the time of the [[Soviet Census (1989)|1989 Soviet census]], these numbers had declined, with 84,860 Azerbaijanis in Armenia and 245,045 Armenians in Azerbaijan outside of Nagorno-Karabakh. Thomas De Waal notes that in the mid-1980s, there were approximately 350,000 Armenians in Azerbaijan outside of Nagorno-Karabakh and around 200,000 Azerbaijanis in Armenia.{{sfn|De Waal|2003|p=18}} Amid [[Mikhail Gorbachev]]'s reforms the [[dissolution of the Soviet Union]] in 1988–89, a 1988 Nagorno-Karabakh referendum was held to transfer the region to [[Soviet Armenia]], citing self-determination laws in the Soviet constitution. In response, a series of pogroms were committed against Armenians throughout Azerbaijan, leading to the start of the Nagorno-Karabakh War.{{sfn|Starovoytova|1997|p=vi}} Professor Matt Qvortrup considered it hypocritical that Western Europe countries had eagerly recognised the succession of several states from [[Yugoslavia]], ignoring the laws of territorial integrity, but simultaneously did not show the same interest for the Nagorno-Karabakh referendum, noting "the practice of independence referendums seemingly owes more to national interest than to adherence to principles of [[jurisprudence]]".{{cite book |last=Qvortrup |first=Matt |date=2019 |title=The Referendum and Other Essays on Constitutional Politics |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nmyODwAAQBAJ&pg=PA57 |location=Oxford, England |publisher=Hart Publishing |page=57 |isbn=978-1-50992-929-0}} According to Stuart Kaufman, a professor of political science and international relations,{{Cite web |title=Stuart Kaufman {{!}} University of Delaware |url=https://www.udel.edu/faculty-staff/experts/stuart-kaufman/ |access-date=20 October 2022 |website=www.udel.edu |language=en}} and de Waal, the first instance of violence in the conflict occurred in October 1987 when an Azerbaijani official "punished" the Armenian-populated village of [[Çardaqlı, Shamkir|Chardakhly]] with a raid for protesting against the appointment of a new collective-farm director.{{sfn|De Waal|2003|p=18}} During the raid, the village's women, children, and elderly were beaten up.{{Cite book |last=Kaufman |first=Stuart J. |title=Modern hatreds: the symbolic politics of ethnic war |date=2001 |isbn=978-1-5017-0199-3 |location=Ithaca, New York |publisher=Cornell University Press |pages=62–63|oclc=1160511946}} In his 2003 book ''Black Garden'', de Waal speculated that "[p]ossibly in reaction to such incidents", Azerbaijanis in Armenia (specifically in the [[Districts of the Armenian Soviet Socialist Republic|districts]] of [[Ghapan]] and [[Meghri]]) were in November 1987 driven out of their homes, arriving at [[Baku]] in two freight cars.{{sfn|De Waal|2003|pp=18-19}} A number of Armenian scholars and investigative journalists, however, have scrutinized these alleged incidents and argued that, outside the claims made by Azerbaijani officials, there is no evidence, archival or otherwise, to corroborate such instances of mass violence taking place, at least prior to February 1988.{{sfn|Cheterian|2008|pp=104-108}}Hakobyan, Tatul. "[https://www.aniarc.am/2020/02/23/what-happened-in-kapan-1988-karabakh-diary-tatul-hakobyan-book/ Ի՞նչ է կատարվել Կապանում. 1988-ի փետրվար]" [What happened in Kapan? February 1988], ANI Armenian Research Center, 23 February 2020.{{Cite journal |last=Hakobyan |first=Arsen |date=2024 |title="Ղափանի դեպքերի" առասպելը նախասումգայիթյան, սումգայիթյան և հետսումգայիթյան զարգացումներում |trans-title=The myth of the "Ghapan Incidents" in pre-Sumgait, Sumgait and post-Sumgait developments |url=https://arar.sci.am/dlibra/publication/408231/edition/377503 |journal=Լրաբեր հասարակական գիտությունների |issue=2 |pages=64 |doi=10.53548/0320-8117-2024.2-63 }} ==Timeline== ===First Nagorno-Karabakh War (1988–1994)=== {{main|First Nagorno-Karabakh War}} [[File:Gravestones of Azeri soldiers died in Karabakh war.jpg|thumb|Graves of Azerbaijani soldiers]] The First Nagorno-Karabakh War, also known as the ''Artsakh Liberation War'' in Armenia and Nagorno-Karabakh, was an armed conflict that took place in the late 1980s to May 1994, in the [[enclave]] of Nagorno-Karabakh in southwestern Azerbaijan, between the majority ethnic Armenians of Nagorno-Karabakh backed by the Republic of Armenia, and the Republic of Azerbaijan. As the war progressed, Armenia and Azerbaijan, then both former [[Republics of the Soviet Union|Soviet Republics]], entangled themselves in a protracted, undeclared [[mountain warfare|war in the mountainous heights]] of Karabakh as Azerbaijan attempted to curb the [[secession]]ist movement in Nagorno-Karabakh. On 20 February 1988, the [[National Assembly (Artsakh)|enclave's parliament]] voted in favor of uniting with Armenia.{{sfn|de Waal|2013|pp=11–12}} The demand to unify with Armenia, which began anew in 1988, began in a relatively peaceful manner. As the Soviet Union's dissolution neared, the tensions gradually grew into an increasingly violent conflict between ethnic Armenians and ethnic Azerbaijanis. Both sides made claims of [[ethnic cleansing]] and [[pogrom]]s conducted by the other. {{cite journal|last=Rieff|first=David|title=Without Rules or Pity|journal=Foreign Affairs|volume=76|issue=2|url=http://www.cilicia.com/armo19e.html|publisher=[[Council on Foreign Relations]]|date=June 1997|access-date=13 February 2007|archive-url=https://archive.today/20080720114733/http://www.cilicia.com/armo19e.html|archive-date=20 July 2008|url-status=live}} {{cite book|last=Lieberman|first=Benjamin|title=Terrible Fate: Ethnic Cleansing in the Making of Modern Europe|publisher=Ivan R. Dee|year=2006|location=Chicago|pages=284–292|isbn=1566636469}} [[File:Muzeum padlých vojáků, Stěpanakert.jpg|thumb|Photos of fallen Armenian soldiers in [[Stepanakert]], Nagorno Karabakh]] The circumstances of the dissolution of the Soviet Union facilitated an Armenian separatist movement in Soviet Azerbaijan. The declaration of secession from Azerbaijan was the final result of a territorial conflict regarding the land.{{cite book|last=Croissant|first=Michael P.|title=The Armenia-Azerbaijan Conflict: Causes and Implications|publisher=Praeger|year=1998|location=London|isbn=0275962415|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/armeniaazerbaija00croi}} As Azerbaijan declared its independence from the Soviet Union and [[Law on Abolishment of Nagorno-Karabakh Autonomous Oblast|removed the powers held by the enclave's government]], the Armenian majority voted to secede from Azerbaijan. [[1991 Nagorno-Karabakh independence referendum|The referendum]] held in 1991 was boycotted by the Azerbaijani population and had an electorate turnout of 82.1%, of which 99.9% voted in favor of independence. The referendum resulted in the [[List of unrecognized states|unrecognized]] [[Republic of Nagorno-Karabakh]].At the time of the [[dissolution of the USSR]], the United States government [[Stimson Doctrine|recognized as legitimate]] the pre-[[Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact]] 1933 borders of the country (the [[Franklin D. Roosevelt]] government established diplomatic relations with the Kremlin at the end of that year). Because of this, the [[George H. Bush]] administration openly supported the secession of the [[Baltic countries|Baltic SSRs]], but regarded the questions related to the independence and territorial conflicts of [[Georgia (country)|Georgia]], Armenia, [[Azerbaijan]] and the rest of the [[Transcaucasus]] as internal Soviet affairs. Full-scale fighting erupted in the late winter of 1992. International mediation by several groups, including the [[Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe]] (OSCE), failed to bring resolution. In the spring of 1993, Armenian forces captured territory outside the enclave itself, threatening to catalyze the involvement of other countries in the region.Four [[List of United Nations Security Council resolutions on the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict|UN Security Council resolutions]], passed in 1993, called for the withdrawal of Armenian forces from the regions falling outside the borders of the former NKAO. By the end of the war in 1994, the Armenians were in full control of most of the enclave and also held and currently control approximately 9% of Azerbaijan's territory outside the enclave.Using numbers provided by journalist [[Thomas de Waal]] for the area of each rayon, as well as the area of the Nagorno-Karabakh Oblast and the total area of Azerbaijan are (in km2): 1,936, Kelbajar; 1,835, Lachin; 802, Kubatly; 1,050, Jebrail; 707, Zangelan; 842, Aghdam; 462, Fizuli; 75, exclaves; totaling {{convert|7709|km²|0|abbr=on}} or 8.9%: De Waal. ''Black Garden'', p. 286. An estimated 353,000 Armenians from Azerbaijan and 500,000 Azerbaijanis from Armenia and Karabakh have been displaced as a result of the conflict.{{sfn|de Waal|2013|p=327}} A Russian-brokered [[ceasefire]] was signed in May 1994, leading to diplomatic mediation.{{Cite journal|last=Freizer|first=Sabine|date=2015|title=Twenty years after the Nagorny Karabakh ceasefire: an opportunity to move towards more inclusive conflict resolution|journal=Caucasus Survey|language=en|volume=1|issue=2|pages=109–122|doi=10.1080/23761199.2014.11417295|s2cid=128678600|issn=2376-1199|doi-access=free}} ===Border clashes (2008–2020)=== [[File:AZ-qa-location-en.svg|thumb|The situation in the area after the 1994 ceasefire]] The [[2008 Mardakert clashes]] began on 4 March after the [[2008 Armenian election protests]], resulting in several score wounded and killed, with both sides declaring victory. It was the heaviest fighting between [[Nagorno-Karabakh Defense Army|ethnic Armenian]] and [[Military of Azerbaijan|Azerbaijani]] forces since the 1994 ceasefire after the First Nagorno-Karabakh War.{{cite news|title=Karabakh casualty toll disputed |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7278871.stm |work=BBC News |date=5 March 2008 |access-date=5 March 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080309004207/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7278871.stm |archive-date=9 March 2008 |url-status=live}}{{cite news|title=Fatal Armenian-Azeri border clash |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7278483.stm |work=BBC News |date=5 March 2008 |access-date=5 March 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080305231537/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7278483.stm |archive-date=5 March 2008 |url-status=live }} Following the incident, on 14 March the [[United Nations General Assembly]] by a recorded vote of 39 in favour to 7 against adopted [[United Nations General Assembly Resolution 62/243|Resolution 62/243]], demanding the immediate withdrawal of all Armenian forces from the occupied territories of Azerbaijan.{{Cite web|date=14 March 2008|title=A/RES/62/243 |url=https://undocs.org/en/A/RES/62/243|access-date=28 September 2020|website=undocs.org}} The [[2010 Nagorno-Karabakh clashes|2010 Nagorno-Karabakh clash]] was a scattered exchange of gunfire that took place on 18 February on the line of contact dividing Azerbaijani and the Karabakh Armenian military forces. As a result, three Azerbaijani soldiers were killed and one wounded.{{cite news|url=http://www.rferl.org/content/Three_Azerbaijani_Soldiers_Killed_Near_NagornoKarabakh/1962175.html |title=Three Azerbaijani Soldiers Killed Near Nagorno-Karabakh |newspaper=Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty |date=18 February 2010 |publisher=[[RFE/RL]] |access-date=27 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101127072308/http://www.rferl.org/content/Three_Azerbaijani_Soldiers_Killed_Near_NagornoKarabakh/1962175.html |archive-date=27 November 2010 |url-status=live }} The [[2010 Mardakert clashes]] were the deadliest for Armenian forces since the 2008 violence.{{Cite news |date=24 June 2010 |title=OSCE, EU Condemn Karabakh 'Armed Incident' |language=en |work=Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty |url=https://www.rferl.org/a/OSCE_EU_Condemn_Karabakh_Armed_Incident/2079009.html |access-date=23 February 2023}} Between 2008 and 2010, 74 soldiers were killed on both sides.{{cite web|title=Armenia and Azerbaijan: Preventing War |url=https://d2071andvip0wj.cloudfront.net/b60-armenia-and-azerbaijan-preventing-war.pdf |website=Europe Briefing N°60 |publisher=[[International Crisis Group]] |date=8 February 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160520105841/http://www.crisisgroup.org/~/media/Files/europe/caucasus/B60%20Armenia%20and%20Azerbaijan%20---%20Preventing%20War.pdf |archive-date=20 May 2016 }} In late April 2011, border clashes left three Nagorno-Karabakh soldiers dead,{{cite web|url=http://www.armeniansworld.com/azerbaijan-goes-beyond-all-permissible-limits-two-artsakh-servicemen-killed/|title=Azerbaijan goes beyond all permissible limits, two Artsakh servicemen killed|publisher=Armeniansworld.com|access-date=13 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140813200950/http://www.armeniansworld.com/azerbaijan-goes-beyond-all-permissible-limits-two-artsakh-servicemen-killed/|archive-date=13 August 2014|url-status=live}} while on 5 October, two Azerbaijani and one Armenian soldier were killed.{{cite news|url=http://www.azatutyun.am/content/article/24350911.html|title=Armenia, Azerbaijan Report More Deadly Skirmishes|author=Vika Elchyan|work=ArmeniaDiaspora.com|date=7 October 2011 |access-date=13 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923181254/http://www.azatutyun.am/content/article/24350911.html|archive-date=23 September 2015|url-status=live}} In all during the year, 10 Armenian soldiers were killed.{{cite news|url=http://www.rferl.org/content/armenia_army_deaths_down_in_2011/24458025.html|title=Armenian Army Death Toll Down In 2011|work=RadioFreeEurope/RadioLiberty|date=20 January 2012 |access-date=4 July 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150706181622/http://www.rferl.org/content/armenia_army_deaths_down_in_2011/24458025.html|archive-date=6 July 2015|url-status=live}} The following year, [[2012 Armenian–Azerbaijani border clashes|continued border clashes]] between the armed forces of Armenia and Azerbaijan took place from late April through early June. The clashes resulted in the deaths of five Azerbaijani and four Armenian soldiers. In all during 2012, 19 Azerbaijani and 14 Armenian soldiers were killed.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2014/aug/04/nagorno-karabakh-clashes-azerbaijan-armenia|title=Bloody clashes between Azerbaijan and Armenia over disputed territory|work=The Guardian|date=4 August 2014|access-date=13 October 2014}} Another report put the number of Azerbaijani dead at 20.{{cite web |author=Hairenik |date=4 February 2015 |title=Loose Restraints: A Look at the Increasingly Shaky Karabagh Ceasefire |url=http://armenianweekly.com/2015/02/04/karabagh/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151022160959/http://armenianweekly.com/2015/02/04/karabagh/ |archive-date=22 October 2015 |access-date=6 February 2015 |work=Armenian Weekly}} Throughout 2013, 12 Azerbaijani and 7 Armenian soldiers were killed in border clashes. [[File:Caucasus breakaway regions 2008.svg|thumb|The [[List of states with limited recognition|breakaway republics]] of Abkhazia, South Ossetia and Nagorno-Karabakh within the Caucasus region, between 2008 and 2020]] In 2014, [[2014 Armenian–Azerbaijani clashes|several border clashes erupted]].{{cite web |url=http://www.rferl.org/content/armenia-says-two-soldiers-killed-in-fresh-border-skirmishes/25429458.html |title=Armenia Says Two Soldiers Killed In Fresh Border Skirmishes |publisher=Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty |date=20 June 2014 |access-date=2 August 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140731235208/http://www.rferl.org/content/armenia-says-two-soldiers-killed-in-fresh-border-skirmishes/25429458.html |archive-date=31 July 2014 |url-status=live }}{{cite news|url=http://uk.reuters.com/article/uk-armenia-azerbaijan-conflict-idUKKBN0G20EP20140802|title=Five more killed in clashes between Azeris, ethnic Armenians (Reuters, August 2, 2014)|date=2 August 2014|agency=Uk.reuters.com|access-date=13 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141019121229/http://uk.reuters.com/article/2014/08/02/uk-armenia-azerbaijan-conflict-idUKKBN0G20EP20140802|archive-date=19 October 2014|url-status=dead}} By August, 27 Azerbaijani soldiers had died since the start of the year.{{cite web|title=Putin Mediates Azeri–Armenian Talks|url=http://www.eurasiareview.com/13082014-putin-mediates-azeri-armenian-talks/ |access-date=13 August 2014 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140813150917/http://www.eurasiareview.com/13082014-putin-mediates-azeri-armenian-talks/ |archive-date=13 August 2014 }} On 12 November 2014, the [[2014 Armenian Mil Mi-24 shootdown|Azerbaijani armed forces shot down]] a Nagorno-Karabakh Defense Army [[Mil Mi-24]] helicopter over Karabakh's Agdam district.{{cite web|title=Armenia vows 'grave consequences' after helicopter downed|url=https://news.yahoo.com/azerbaijan-shoots-down-armenian-military-helicopter-baku-132139074.html|date=12 November 2014|access-date=12 November 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141112230705/http://news.yahoo.com/azerbaijan-shoots-down-armenian-military-helicopter-baku-132139074.html|archive-date=12 November 2014|url-status=live}} With the crash, 2014 became the deadliest year for Armenian forces since the 1994 ceasefire agreement, with 27 soldiers killed.{{Cite web|url=https://www.reporter.am/go/article/2014-11-12-armenian-helicopter-shot-down-in-karabakh-3-crew-presumed-dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141218170917/http://www.reporter.am/go/article/2014-11-12-armenian-helicopter-shot-down-in-karabakh-3-crew-presumed-dead|url-status=dead|title=Armenian helicopter shot down in Karabakh, 3 crew presumed dead|last=Sanamyan|first=Emil|date=12 November 2014 |archive-date=18 December 2014}} Six Armenian civilians also died in 2014, while by the end of the year the number of Azerbaijanis killed rose to 39 (37 soldiers and 2 civilians). In 2015, 42 Armenian soldiers and 5 civilians were killed as border clashes continued.{{cite web|url=http://armenianweekly.com/2016/01/14/attrition-war-escalates/|title=Armenian-Azerbaijani Attrition War Escalates|work=Armenian Weekly|date=14 January 2016|access-date=14 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160116214426/http://armenianweekly.com/2016/01/14/attrition-war-escalates/|archive-date=16 January 2016|url-status=live}} In addition, at least 64 Azerbaijani soldiers also died, according to Armenian sources.{{cite web|url=http://www.panarmenian.net/eng/news/199881/|title=64 Azeri soldiers killed in 10 months of 2015: survey|work=PanArmenian.Net|access-date=13 May 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160417055317/http://www.panarmenian.net/eng/news/199881/|archive-date=17 April 2016|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://www.yenisafak.com/en/world/azerbaijani-soldier-killed-on-contact-line-with-armenia-ministry-2366778|title=Azerbaijani soldier killed on contact line with Armenia: ministry|author=Piri Medya|date=18 December 2015|work=Yeni Şafak|access-date=15 September 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160404201814/http://www.yenisafak.com/en/world/azerbaijani-soldier-killed-on-contact-line-with-armenia-ministry-2366778|archive-date=4 April 2016|url-status=dead}} Over the years, Azerbaijan had been growing impatient with the status quo. In this regard, propelled by oil and gas windfall, the country embarked in a military build-up. In 2015 alone, Baku spent USD $3 billion on its military, more than Armenia's entire national budget.{{cite web |last1=Vartanyan |first1=Olesya |last2=Grono |first2=Magdalena |date=14 July 2017 |title=Armenia and Azerbaijan collision course over Nagorno-Karabakh |url=https://www.opendemocracy.net/od-russia/olesya-vartanyan-magdalena-grono/armenia-and-azerbaijan-collision-course-over-nagorno-karabakh |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170714115035/https://www.opendemocracy.net/od-russia/olesya-vartanyan-magdalena-grono/armenia-and-azerbaijan-collision-course-over-nagorno-karabakh |archive-date=14 July 2017 |access-date=14 July 2017 |publisher=openDemocracy }} In early 2016, the most serious clashes until the 2020 war occurred (the [[2016 Nagorno-Karabakh conflict]]). Between 1 and 5 April 2016, heavy fighting along the Nagorno-Karabakh frontline left 88 Armenian and 31–92 Azerbaijani soldiers dead. One Armenian and three Azerbaijani soldiers were also missing. In addition, 10 civilians (six Azerbaijani and four Armenian) were also killed.{{cite web|url=https://www.armenianow.com/en/karabakh/2016/04/14/armenia-casualties-update-karabakh-april/951/|title=Karabakh War Casualty Update: Armenia puts April death toll at 92|access-date=15 September 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160418231210/http://www.armenianow.com/en/karabakh/2016/04/14/armenia-casualties-update-karabakh-april/951/|archive-date=18 April 2016|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.meydan.tv/az/site/opinion/13405/|title=Siyahı: 93 hərbçi şəhid olub, 6 mülki şəxs dünyasını dəyişib (YENİLƏNİR)|work=Meydan TV|access-date=15 September 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170728135013/https://www.meydan.tv/az/site/opinion/13405/|archive-date=28 July 2017|url-status=live |last1=Tv |first1=Meydan }} During the clashes, an Azerbaijani military helicopter and 13 unmanned drones were shot down and an Azerbaijani tank was destroyed, while Nagorno-Karabakh lost 14 tanks.{{cite web|url=http://www.panarmenian.net/eng/news/209819/|title=Karabakh army downs 13th Azerbaijani drone|work=PanArmenian.Net|access-date=15 September 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161005010151/http://www.panarmenian.net/eng/news/209819/|archive-date=5 October 2016|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://www.panarmenian.net/eng/news/210323/|title=Azeri surgeon: Number of severely injured soldiers greatly tops the norm|work=PanARMENIAN.Net|access-date=13 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160416095725/http://panarmenian.net/eng/news/210323/|archive-date=16 April 2016|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://haqqin.az/news/67421|title=Минобороны Азербайджана назвало потери в боях|date=5 April 2016 |access-date=26 February 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161004150436/http://haqqin.az/news/67421|archive-date=4 October 2016|url-status=live}} [[2018 Armenian–Azerbaijani clashes|Continued clashes occurred in 2018]].{{Cite news|url=https://www.rferl.org/a/nagornoi-karabakh-armenian-soldier-killed-chilingarian-azerbaijan/28962611.html|title=Military In Nagorno-Karabakh Says Soldier Killed By Azerbaijani Sniper|newspaper=Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty|date=8 January 2018 |language=en|access-date=16 March 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180316115220/https://www.rferl.org/a/nagornoi-karabakh-armenian-soldier-killed-chilingarian-azerbaijan/28962611.html|archive-date=16 March 2018|url-status=live}} Three civilian volunteers were killed in a demining operation in Nagorno-Karabakh on 29 March 2018.{{cite news|url=https://www.rferl.org/a/armenia-azerbaijan-nagorno-karabakh-halo-deminers-killed-car-mine/29132935.html|title=Three Killed In Nagorno-Karabakh Demining Group Operation|website=RadioFreeEurope/RadioLiberty|access-date=16 April 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180416053951/https://www.rferl.org/a/armenia-azerbaijan-nagorno-karabakh-halo-deminers-killed-car-mine/29132935.html|archive-date=16 April 2018|url-status=live}} 2020 saw a number of clashes, particularly heavy in July ([[July 2020 Armenian–Azerbaijani clashes]]).{{Cite news |date=14 July 2020 |title=Azerbaijan general among troops killed in Armenia border clash |website=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-53402476 |access-date=18 July 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200718004331/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-53402476 |archive-date=18 July 2020}}{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=16 July 2020 |title=Armenien und Aserbaidschan bekriegen sich wieder |url=https://www.dw.com/de/armenien-und-aserbaidschan-bekriegen-sich-wieder/a-54198023 |access-date=23 February 2023 |website=Deutsche Welle |language=de-DE}} === Second Nagorno-Karabakh War (2020) === {{main|Second Nagorno-Karabakh War}}[[File:War trophies at 2020 Victory Parade in Baku 25.jpg|thumb|A truck with the slogan "Karabakh is Azerbaijan" at the [[Baku Victory Parade of 2020|Baku Victory Parade]] on 10 December 2020. The parade was held in honor of the Azeri victory in the 2020 conflict.]] Large-scale fighting began on the morning of 27 September, with an Azerbaijani offensive{{cite web |last=Kofman |first=Michael |date=2 October 2020 |title=Armenia–Azerbaijan War: Military Dimensions of the Conflict |url=https://www.russiamatters.org/analysis/armenia-azerbaijan-war-military-dimensions-conflict |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201005003822/https://www.russiamatters.org/analysis/armenia-azerbaijan-war-military-dimensions-conflict |archive-date=5 October 2020 |access-date=3 October 2020 |website=russiamatters.org |publisher=Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs |quote=On 27 September 2020, Azerbaijan launched a military offensive, resulting in fighting that spans much of the line of contact in the breakaway region of Nagorno-Karabakh...}}{{cite web |last=Kucera |first=Joshua |date=29 September 2020 |title=As fighting rages, what is Azerbaijan's goal? |url=https://eurasianet.org/as-fighting-rages-what-is-azerbaijans-goal |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201004115811/https://eurasianet.org/as-fighting-rages-what-is-azerbaijans-goal |archive-date=4 October 2020 |access-date=29 September 2020 |website=eurasianet.org |quote=The Azerbaijani offensive against Armenian forces is its most ambitious since the war between the two sides formally ended in 1994.}} along the [[Line of Contact (Nagorno-Karabakh)|line of contact]] established in the aftermath of the first war. Clashes were particularly intense in the less mountainous districts of southern Nagorno-Karabakh.{{cite news |date=27 September 2020 |title=Armenia and Azerbaijan erupt into fighting over disputed Nagorno-Karabakh |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-54314341 |url-status=live |access-date=27 September 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200928055530/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-54314341 |archive-date=28 September 2020}} On the same day, Azerbaijan's Parliament declared a [[martial law]] and established [[curfew]]s in several cities and regions following the clashes.{{Cite news |date=27 September 2020 |title=Azerbaijan's parliament approves martial law, curfews – president's aide |language=en |work=Reuters |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/uk-armenia-azerbaijan-curfew-idUKKBN26I0OS |access-date=29 September 2020}} Turkey provided military support to Azerbaijan, although the extent of this support has been disputed.{{cite news |last=Jones |first=Dorian |date=28 September 2020 |title=Turkey Vows Support for Azerbaijan in Escalating Nagorno-Karabakh Conflict |publisher=Voice of America |url=https://www.voanews.com/a/south-central-asia_turkey-vows-support-azerbaijan-escalating-nagorno-karabakh-conflict/6196470.html |url-status=bot: unknown |access-date=28 September 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001034250/https://www.voanews.com/south-central-asia/turkey-vows-support-azerbaijan-escalating-nagorno-karabakh-conflict |archive-date=1 October 2020 |quote={{-'}}Turkey is already supporting Azerbaijan militarily, technical assistance and arms sales, providing critical military support, especially in terms of armed drones and technical expertise', said Turkish analyst Ilhan Uzgel.}} The war was marked by the deployment of [[Unmanned aerial vehicle|drones]], sensors, long-range heavy artillery{{cite web |last=Gatopoulos |first=Alex |title=The Nagorno-Karabakh conflict is ushering in a new age of warfare |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/features/2020/10/11/nagorno-karabakh-conflict-ushering-in-new-age-of-warfare |access-date=15 October 2020 |website=aljazeera.com}} and [[missile]] strikes, as well as by state propaganda and the use of official social media accounts in online [[information warfare]].{{cite news |last=Mirovalev |first=Mansur |date=15 October 2020 |title=Armenia, Azerbaijan battle an online war over Nagorno-Karabakh |agency=Al Jazeera |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/features/2020/10/15/karabakh-info-war}} In particular, Azerbaijan's widespread use of drones was seen as crucial in determining the conflict's outcome.{{cite news |last1=Sabbagh |first1=Dan |date=29 December 2020 |title=UK wants new drones in wake of Azerbaijan military success |language=en |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/dec/29/uk-defence-secretary-hails-azerbaijans-use-of-drones-in-conflict |access-date=21 September 2021}} Numerous countries and the [[United Nations]] strongly condemned the fighting and called on both sides to de-escalate tensions and resume meaningful negotiations.{{cite web |date=30 September 2020 |title=UN Security Council calls for immediate end to fighting in Nagorno-Karabakh |url=https://www.france24.com/en/20200930-un-security-council-calls-for-immediate-end-to-fighting-in-nagorno-karabakh |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201009133111/https://www.france24.com/en/20200930-un-security-council-calls-for-immediate-end-to-fighting-in-nagorno-karabakh |archive-date=9 October 2020 |access-date=30 September 2020 |website=france24.com |agency=Associated Press}} Three ceasefires brokered by Russia, France, and the United States failed to stop the conflict.{{cite news |last1=Hovhannisyan |first1=Nvard |last2=Bagirova |first2=Nailia |date=13 October 2020 |title=Nagorno-Karabakh conflict unacceptable: EU |agency=The Canberra Times |url=https://www.canberratimes.com.au/story/6991293/nagorno-karabakh-conflict-unacceptable-eu/ |access-date=17 November 2020}} Following [[Battle of Shusha (2020)|the capture]] of Shusha, the second-largest city in Nagorno-Karabakh, a [[2020 Nagorno-Karabakh ceasefire agreement|ceasefire agreement]] was signed, ending all hostilities in the area from 10 November 2020.{{cite web |date=9 November 2020 |title=Путин выступил с заявлением о прекращении огня в Карабахе |url=https://ria.ru/20201110/karabakh-1583847112.html |access-date=9 November 2020 |language=ru |agency=RIA Novosti}}{{cite web |date=9 November 2020 |title=Пашинян заявил о прекращении боевых действий в Карабахе |url=https://ria.ru/20201110/karabakh-1583845291.html |access-date=9 November 2020 |language=ru |agency=RIA Novosti}}{{cite news |date=10 November 2020 |title=Nagorno-Karabakh: Russia deploys peacekeeping troops to region |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-54885906 |access-date=11 November 2020}} Following the end of the war, an unconfirmed number of [[Armenian POWs during the Second Nagorno-Karabakh War|Armenian prisoners of war]] were captive in Azerbaijan, with reports of mistreatment and charges filed against them,{{cite journal |last1=de Waal |first1=Thomas |date=27 September 2021 |title=The Nagorny Karabakh Conflict in its Fourth Decade |url=https://www.ceps.eu/ceps-publications/the-nagorny-karabakh-conflict-in-its-fourth-decade/ |journal=Centre for European Policy Studies |access-date=28 September 2021}}{{cite web |date=19 March 2021 |title=Azerbaijan: Armenian POWs Abused in Custody |url=https://www.hrw.org/news/2021/03/19/azerbaijan-armenian-pows-abused-custody}}{{cite web |date=2 December 2020 |title=Azerbaijan: Armenian Prisoners of War Badly Mistreated |url=https://www.hrw.org/news/2020/12/02/azerbaijan-armenian-prisoners-war-badly-mistreated |access-date=2 December 2020 |work=Human Rights Watch}}{{cite news |date=1 July 2021 |title=Armenian soldiers on trial in Azerbaijan |work=Eurasianet |url=https://eurasianet.org/armenian-soldiers-on-trial-in-azerbaijan |access-date=11 August 2021}} leading to [[Republic of Armenia v. Republic of Azerbaijan (ICJ case)|a case]] at the [[International Court of Justice]].{{Cite web|url=https://media.un.org/en/asset/k1z/k1zrr42ylv|title=THE HAGUE – The International Court of Justice (ICJ) holds public hearings in the case Armenia v. Azerbaijan | UN Web TV|date=14 October 2021|website=media.un.org|access-date=23 February 2023|archive-date=3 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220503155508/https://media.un.org/en/asset/k1z/k1zrr42ylv|url-status=dead}} [[File:Nagorno-Karabakh war map (2020).svg|thumb|Map of the [[2020 Nagorno-Karabakh ceasefire agreement]]]] Casualties were high,{{Cite web |title=The human cost of the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2020/10/12/the-human-cost-of-the-nagorno-karabakh-conflict |access-date=15 October 2020 |website=www.aljazeera.com |language=en}} officially in the low thousands.{{Cite news |last1=McKernan |first1=Bethan |last2=Zavallis |first2=Achilleas |date=13 October 2020 |title=Trench warfare, drones and cowering civilians: on the ground in Nagorno-Karabakh |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/artanddesign/2020/oct/13/trench-warfare-drones-and-cowering-civilians-on-the-ground-in-nagorno-karabakh |access-date=13 October 2020 |issn=0261-3077}} According to official figures released by the belligerents, Armenia and Artsakh lost 3,825 troops,{{cite web |date=13 April 2022 |title=Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan's speech at the National Assembly during the discussion of the performance report of the Government Action Plan for 2021 |url=https://www.primeminister.am/en/statements-and-messages/item/2022/04/13/Nikol-Pashinyan-Speech/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220414071601/https://www.primeminister.am/en/statements-and-messages/item/2022/04/13/Nikol-Pashinyan-Speech/ |archive-date=14 April 2022 |website=primeminister.am |publisher=The Prime Minister of the Republic of Armenia |quote=The number of victims of the 44-day war is 3825 by today’s data.}} with 187 servicemen [[missing in action]],{{cite web |author= |date=21 March 2022 |title=187 Armenian troops still MIA, 21 civilians missing in 2020 Nagorno Karabakh war |url=https://www.armenpress.am/eng/news/1078446/ |access-date=21 March 2022 |website=armenpress.m |publisher=Armenpress |quote=}} while Azerbaijan claimed 2,906 of their troops were killed, with 6 missing in action.On 21 October 2021, the [[Ministry of Defence (Azerbaijan)|Ministry of Defence of the Republic of Azerbaijan]] published a list of dead servicemen. It said 2,908 people were killed during the war, with another 6 missing in action,[https://mod.gov.az/en/news/list-of-the-servicemen-fallen-shehids-in-the-patriotic-war-38076.html] although at least two of the soldiers named were killed after the conflict ended,[https://www.azernews.az/karabakh/173267.html][https://sozcu.az/azerbaycan-esgeri-susa-yolunda-minaya-duserek-sehid-oldu/] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220408130119/https://sozcu.az/azerbaycan-esgeri-susa-yolunda-minaya-duserek-sehid-oldu/|date=8 April 2022}} leaving a total of 2,906 servicemen confirmed killed in the war. The [[Syrian Observatory for Human Rights]] reported the deaths of 541 Syrian fighters or mercenaries fighting for Azerbaijan.{{cite web |date=3 December 2020 |title=SOHR exclusive {{!}} Death toll of mercenaries in Azerbaijan is higher than that in Libya, while Syrian fighters given varying payments |url=https://www.syriahr.com/en/194516/ |access-date=3 December 2020 |publisher=Syrian Observatory for Human Rights}} However, it was noted that the sides downplayed the number of their own casualties and exaggerated the numbers of enemy casualties and injuries.{{cite web |date=3 October 2020 |title=Military experts from Armenia and Azerbaijan question official casualty figures |url=https://jam-news.net/karabakh-casualties-experts-opinion/ |access-date=18 October 2020 |publisher=JAMnews}} The total number of reported civilian fatalities on both sides was at least 185; the whereabouts of 21 Armenian civilians remain unknown. Civilian areas, including major cities, were hit, particularly [[2020 bombardment of Stepanakert|Stepanakert]], [[2020 bombardment of Martuni|Martuni]], [[Martakert]], [[2020 Ghazanchetsots Cathedral shelling|Shushi]] in the Republic of Artsakh and [[Ganja, Azerbaijan|Ganja]], [[Barda, Azerbaijan|Barda]] and [[Tartar (city)|Tartar]] in Azerbaijan, with many buildings and homes destroyed.{{Cite news |date=5 October 2020 |title=In pictures: Fighting rages in Nagorno-Karabakh conflict |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-54418901 |url-status=live |access-date=5 October 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201005180202/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-54418901 |archive-date=5 October 2020}}{{Cite news |date=4 October 2020 |title=Nagorno-Karabakh conflict: Major cities hit as heavy fighting continues |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-54407436 |url-status=live |access-date=5 October 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201004100202/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-54407436 |archive-date=4 October 2020}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.dailysabah.com/politics/diplomacy/armenia-hits-civilians-visiting-cemetery-in-azerbaijans-tartar|title=Armenia hits civilians visiting cemetery in Azerbaijan's Tartar|first=DAILY SABAH WITH|last=AA|date=15 October 2020|website=Daily Sabah}} === Border tensions (2021–present) === ==== Border crisis (2021–present) ==== {{Main|Armenia–Azerbaijan border crisis (2021–present)}} An ongoing border crisis started on 12 May 2021, when Azerbaijani soldiers crossed several kilometers into Armenia in the provinces of [[Syunik Province|Syunik]] and [[Gegharkunik Province|Gegharkunik]], occupying between 50 and 215 square kilometers.{{cite web |last1=Toal |first1=Gerard |title=Perspectives {{!}} Suddenly a borderland: The new borderization between Armenia and Azerbaijan |url=https://eurasianet.org/perspectives-suddenly-a-borderland-the-new-borderization-between-armenia-and-azerbaijan |access-date=23 May 2023 |date=25 November 2022 |quote=Some open source estimates put it at a total of 145 square kilometers (56 square miles) whereas others indicate 127 square kilometers (49 square miles).}}{{Cite web |last=Khudoyan |first=Knar |date=19 October 2022 |title=Will Armenia and Azerbaijan reach a peace deal? |url=https://www.opendemocracy.net/en/odr/armenia-azerbaijan-peace-deal-nagorno-karabakh-border/ |access-date=22 April 2023 |website=openDemocracy |language=en |quote=Azerbaijan has now occupied roughly 50 square kilometres of Armenian territory since the 2020 conflict.}}{{Cite news |last=Stepanian |first=Ruzanna |date=21 April 2023 |title=Armenian Border Area 'Still Occupied By Azeri Troops' |language=hy |work=«Ազատ Եվրոպա/Ազատություն» ռադիոկայան |url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/32374139.html |access-date=21 April 2023}}{{Cite news |last=Bulghadarian |first=Naira |date=24 October 2022 |title=U.S. Official Visits Armenian-Azeri Border |language=hy |work=«Ազատ Եվրոպա/Ազատություն» ռադիոկայան |url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/32098583.html |access-date=23 April 2023}}{{Cite web |date=14 April 2023 |title=Azerbaijan has occupied at least 215 square kilometers of Armenian territory since 2020 |url=https://www.civilnet.am/en/news/697545/azerbaijan-has-occupied-at-least-215-square-kilometers-of-armenian-territory-since-2020/ |access-date=21 April 2023 |website=CIVILNET |language=en-US}}{{Cite web |title=International politics : Circumventing Russia: Can the South Caucasus connect the West to the East? |url=https://www.freiheit.org/germany/circumventing-russia-can-south-caucasus-connect-west-east |access-date=27 April 2023 |website=www.freiheit.org |date=21 March 2023 |language=en |quote=The latest Azerbaijani attack...resulted in...the occupation of some 60 square kilometers of Armenian territory within two days.}}{{Cite web |title=A child's memories of the never-ending war in Armenia |url=https://www.opendemocracy.net/en/5050/armenia-azerbaijan-war-kapan-syunik-nagorno-karabakh/ |access-date=23 April 2023 |website=openDemocracy |language=en}}{{Cite web |date=31 March 2022 |title=Armenia's Defense Ministry Concealed Azerbaijani Advances Near Syunik Village |url=https://www.civilnet.am/en/news/656133/armenias-defense-ministry-concealed-azerbaijani-advances-near-syunik-village/ |access-date=6 June 2023 |website=CIVILNET |language=en-US |quote=A
