|title=Осетинский батальон в арцахской освободительной войне [Ossetian battalion in the Artsakh liberation war] |url=http://tta.am/осетинский-батальон-в-арцахской-осво/ |website=tta.am |publisher=time to analyze |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170708055134/http://tta.am/%D0%BE%D1%81%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9-%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BE%D0%BD-%D0%B2-%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%86%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B9-%D0%BE%D1%81%D0%B2%D0%BE/ |archive-date=8 July 2017 |language=ru |date=13 March 2013 |quote=36 героев – осетин, навсегда вписали свои имена в одну из ярчайших страниц армянской истории – Арцахскую освободительную войну. В целом, в осетинском батальоне насчитывалось 30 осетин (26 христиан и 4 мусульман), один кабардиниец, татарин, русский и три армянина.}} In May 2011, a ''[[khachkar]]'' was inaugurated in the village of [[Vank, Karabakh|Vank]] in memory of 14 Kuban Cossacks who died in the war.{{cite news |title=В карабахском селе открылся памятник погибшим в войне кубанским казакам [A monument to the Kuban Cossacks who died in the war was opened in the Karabakh village] |url=https://regnum.ru/news/1410060.html |agency=[[REGNUM News Agency]] |date=30 May 2011 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200817160304/https://regnum.ru/news/polit/1410060.html |archive-date=17 August 2020 |language=ru}} Ossetian volunteers reportedly came from both [[South Ossetia]] (Georgia) and [[North Ossetia]] (Russia).According to [[Leonid Tibilov]], President of South Ossetia in 2012–17. {{cite news |title=Президент Южной Осетии: В борьбе за свободу и независимость народу Карабаха помогали волонтеры-осетины [President of South Ossetia: Ossetian volunteers helped the people of Karabakh in the struggle for freedom and independence] |url=http://www.panarmenian.net/rus/news/219928/ |agency=[[PanArmenian.Net]] |date=2 September 2016 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200817140128/http://www.panarmenian.net/rus/news/219928/ |archive-date=17 August 2020 |language=ru |quote=В борьбе за свободу и независимость на помощь народу Арцаха пришли и волонтеры из Южной Осетии.}}{{cite news |title=Осетинские хроники Нагорного Карабаха [Ossetian chronicles of Nagorno-Karabakh] |url=http://osinform.org/55747-osetinskie-hroniki-nagornogo-karabaha.html |work=osinform.org |date=13 April 2016 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200817144907/http://osinform.org/55747-osetinskie-hroniki-nagornogo-karabaha.html |archive-date=17 August 2020 |language=ru |quote=Наибольшей известностью в Арцахе пользовался Мирза Абаев. В 1992 году он прибыл добровольцем в Нагорный Карабах из России.}} No less than 12 [[Armenian diaspora|diaspora Armenian]] volunteers fought and four diaspora fighters died in the war.According to Emil Sanamyan, an analyst at the [[USC Institute of Armenian Studies]]: * {{cite web |last1=Sanamyan |first1=Emil |title=I put together a list, so far I am at 12 names exactly, though I am pretty sure its not an exhaustive list |url=https://twitter.com/emil_sanamyan/status/810560578179702784 |publisher=[[Twitter]] |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200824112916/https://twitter.com/emil_sanamyan/status/810560578179702784 |archive-date=24 August 2020 |date=18 December 2016}} * {{cite web |last1=Sanamyan |first1=Emil |title=whats known is that 4 of Armenian killed in war came from Diaspora; by comparison 40+ were non-Armenians from FSU |url=https://twitter.com/emil_sanamyan/status/810497832293646336 |publisher=[[Twitter]] |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200824112822/https://twitter.com/emil_sanamyan/status/810497832293646336 |url-status=dead |archive-date=24 August 2020 |date=18 December 2016}}{{cite news |last1=Beglaryan |first1=Artak |author-link1=:hy:Արտակ Բեգլարյան |title=The Main Directions of the Artsakh-Diaspora Relations |url=http://theanalyticon.com/?p=933&lang=en |work=theanalyticon.com |date=September 2011 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200824112857/http://theanalyticon.com/?p=933&lang=en |archive-date=24 August 2020 |quote=The contribution of the volunteer-fighters from Diaspora into the military victory of the Artsakh struggle is invaluable.}} According to [[David Rieff]], members of the [[Armenian Revolutionary Federation]] (Dashnaks), "including a substantial number of volunteers from the diaspora, did a great deal of the fighting and dying."{{cite web|last1=Rieff|first1=David|author-link1=David Rieff|title=Case Study in Ethnic Strife|url=http://www.princeton.edu/~amimages/rieff.html|publisher=[[Council on Foreign Relations]]|date=1997|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312161759/http://www.princeton.edu/~amimages/rieff.html|archive-date=12 March 2017}} Former members of the [[Armenian Secret Army for the Liberation of Armenia]] (ASALA) also participated in the war.{{cite journal |last1=Arasli |first1=Jahangir |title=The Rising Wind: Is the Caucasus Emerging as a Hub for Terrorism, Smuggling, and Trafficking? |journal=[[Partnership for Peace Consortium of Defense Academies and Security Studies Institutes#Publications|Connections: The Quarterly Journal]] |date=Spring 2007 |volume=6 |issue=1 |page=22 |url=http://connections-qj.org/article/rising-wind-caucasus-emerging-hub-terrorism-smuggling-and-trafficking |publisher=[[Partnership for Peace Consortium of Defense Academies and Security Studies Institutes]] |doi=10.11610/Connections.06.1.02 |quote=Many members of ASALA fought against Azerbaijan during the war over Karabakh as part of the Armenian and Nagorno-Karabakh militaries.|doi-access=free}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20180721180351/http://connections-qj.org/system/files/06.1.02_arasli.pdf PDF], [https://web.archive.org/web/20191002194600/http://connections-qj.org/article/rising-wind-caucasus-emerging-hub-terrorism-smuggling-and-trafficking archived] ===Diplomatic support=== ====Artsakh and Armenia==== Artsakh (Nagorno-Karabakh Republic) received diplomatic recognition{{cite journal |last1=Blakkisrud |first1=Helge |last2=Kolstø |first2=Pål |title=Dynamics of de facto statehood: the South Caucasian de facto states between secession and sovereignty |journal=Southeast European and Black Sea Studies |date=2012 |volume=12 |issue=2 |page=295 |doi=10.1080/14683857.2012.686013 |s2cid=153522424 |quote=...the three South Caucasian de facto states have mutually recognized each other, as well as being recognized by (unrecognized) Transnistria.}} and diplomatic support, especially during the 2016 clashes, from three partially recognized states: [[Abkhazia]],{{efn|{{Abkhazia-note}}}}{{cite web |title=A telephone conversation between Foreign Ministers of the Nagorno Karabakh Republic and Republic of Abkhazia |url=http://www.nkr.am/en/news/2016-04-07/804 |website=nkr.am |publisher=Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Artsakh |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200824124856/http://www.nkr.am/en/news/2016-04-07/804 |archive-date=24 August 2020 |date=7 April 2016 |quote=Vyacheslav Chirikba asked to convey his condolences to the families of those killed in hostilities and voiced the support of the people and authorities of Abkhazia to Artsakh.}}{{cite web |title=Conversation of Viacheslav Chirikba with Karen Mirzoyan |url=http://old.mfaapsny.org/en/information/?ID=4240 |website=old.mfaapsny.org |publisher=Minister of Foreign Affairs of Abkhazia |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200809130657/http://old.mfaapsny.org/en/information/?ID=4240 |archive-date=9 August 2020 |date=7 April 2016}} [[South Ossetia]],{{efn|{{South Ossetia-note}}}}{{cite web |title=Press release of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of South Ossetia |url=http://www.mfa-rso.su/en/node/1688 |website=mfa-rso.su |publisher=Ministry of Foreign Affairs Republic of South Ossetia |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200724090011/http://www.mfa-rso.su/en/node/1688 |archive-date=24 July 2020 |date=6 April 2016 |quote=The Minister assured his colleague that South Ossetia people follow the development of situation and offered words of support to people of the Nagorno-Karabakh Republic.}} and [[Transnistria]].{{efn|{{Transnistria-note}}}}{{cite web |title=Telephone conversation with NKR Foreign Minister Karen Mirzoyan |url=http://mid.gospmr.org/en/NJh |website=mid.gospmr.org |publisher=Ministry of Foreign Affairs of PMR |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160409172824/http://mid.gospmr.org/en/NJh |archive-date=9 April 2016 |date=4 April 2016 |quote=The head of the Pridnestrovian diplomacy expressed compassion and support to the people of Artsakh in connection with the escalation of tension on the part of the Republic of Azerbaijan.}} During the war, Greece adopted a pro-Armenian position{{cite book |editor1-last=Yiallourides |editor1-first=Christodoulos K. |editor2-last=Tsakonas |editor2-first=Panayotis J. |title=Greece and Turkey after the End of the Cold War |date=2001 |publisher=Aristide D. Caratzas |location=New York and Athens |isbn=0892415649 |page=412 |quote=Greece, on the other hand, had no particular reasons to shun Azerbaijan, but its historical friendship with the Armenian people, and shared concerns over Turkish aggression, naturally induced a pro-Armenian Greek policy.}} and supported it in international forums.Leonidas Chrysanthopoulos, Ambassador of Greece to Armenia in 1993–94: {{cite book |last1=Chrysanthopoulos |first1=Leonidas |title=Caucasus Chronicles: Nation-building and Diplomacy in Armenia, 1993–1994 |date=2002 |publisher=[[Gomidas Institute]] |isbn=978-1884630057}} * p. 66: "I told him that they should be very careful on the Fizuli issue, because if it were to fall into Armenian hands, the international condemnation would be so strong that Greece would no longer be able to support Armenia in international forums and particularly in the European Union. * p. 68: "Greece was doing everything possible in all the other international forums to help Armenia and to bring peace to the troubled area. During the April 2016 and July 2020 clashes, [[Cyprus]] condemned Azerbaijan for violating the ceasefire.{{cite news |title=Cyprus Denounces Civilian Casualties in Artsakh; Urges Turkey Not to Destabilize Situation |url=https://hetq.am/en/article/67014 |access-date=23 July 2020 |work=[[Hetq]] |date=4 April 2016 |quote=The Government of the Republic of Cyprus monitors closely the worrying developments in Nagorno-Karabakh/Artsakh, following the violations of the armistice line from Azerbaijani military forces.}}{{cite web |title=The Minister of Foreign Affairs, Mr Nikos Christodoulides, had a telephone conversation with the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Armenia, Mr Zohrab Mnatsakanyan |url=https://www.pio.gov.cy/en/press-releases-article.html?id=14759#flat |website=pio.gov.cy |publisher=Press and Information Office, Ministry of Interior, Republic of Cyprus |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200715123322/https://www.pio.gov.cy/en/press-releases-article.html?id=14759 |archive-date=15 July 2020 |date=15 July 2020 |quote=Minister Christodoulides expressed to Minister Mnatsakanyan his concern about this development, condemned the ceasefire violation by Azerbaijan...}} Armenian President [[Levon Ter-Petrosyan]] reportedly told the Greek ambassador in 1993 that France and Russia were Armenia's only allies at the time.Leonidas Chrysanthopoulos, Ambassador of Greece to Armenia in 1993–94, wrote: "[[Levon Ter-Petrosyan|Ter-Petrossian]] [...] told me that at the moment Russia and France were the only allies of Armenia. Both countries had reacted in an effective way to Turkey within the United Nations Security Council and the CSCE, and they forced the United States to adopt a more objective position on the Nagorno-Karabakh issue." {{cite book |last1=Chrysanthopoulos |first1=Leonidas |title=Caucasus Chronicles: Nation-building and Diplomacy in Armenia, 1993–1994 |date=2002 |publisher=[[Gomidas Institute]] |isbn=978-1884630057 |pages=67–68}} According to a US State Department cable released in 2020, the French ambassador to the UN, [[Jean-Bernard Mérimée]], succeeded in changing the wording of the [[United Nations Security Council Resolution 822|UNSC Resolution 822]] to state that it was "local Armenian forces", not "Armenian forces" that occupied Kalbajar. He also suggested treating the Armenian capture of Kalbajar not under [[Chapter VII of the United Nations Charter|Chapter VII]] of the [[UN Charter]] (an act of aggression), but [[Chapter VI of the United Nations Charter|Chapter VI]] (a dispute that should be settled peacefully).{{cite web |title=From the Archives: How France Influenced UN's Karabakh Resolution |url=https://armenian.usc.edu/from-the-archives-how-france-influenced-1993-un-resolution-on-karabakh/ |publisher=[[USC Institute of Armenian Studies]] |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200816072652/https://armenian.usc.edu/from-the-archives-how-france-influenced-1993-un-resolution-on-karabakh/ |archive-date=16 August 2020 |date=28 May 2020}} ====Azerbaijan==== Azerbaijan has received explicit diplomatic support in the conflict from several countries and international organizations. Azerbaijan's strongest diplomatic supporters are Turkey and Pakistan,{{cite news |date=15 July 2020 |title=Pakistan condemns Armenian attack on Tovuz district in Azerbaijan |url=http://mofa.gov.pk/pakistan-condemns-armenian-attack-on-tovuz-district-in-azerbaijan/|url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200715193754/http://mofa.gov.pk/pakistan-condemns-armenian-attack-on-tovuz-district-in-azerbaijan/ |archive-date=15 July 2020 |access-date=15 July 2020 |website=mofa.gov.pk |publisher=[[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Pakistan)|Pakistani Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] |language=en}}{{cite news|title=Pakistan will continue supporting Azerbaijan on Nagorno-Karabakh |url=http://www.dailytimes.com.pk/national/14-Mar-2015/pakistan-will-continue-supporting-azerbaijan-on-nagorno-karabakh |work=[[Daily Times (Pakistan)|Daily Times]] |date=14 March 2015 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160123185504/http://www.dailytimes.com.pk/national/14-Mar-2015/pakistan-will-continue-supporting-azerbaijan-on-nagorno-karabakh |archive-date=23 January 2016}} which is the only UN member state not to have recognized Armenia's independence in support for Azerbaijan.{{cite news |last1=Korybko |first1=Andrew |title=Why is Pakistan the only country that does not recognise Armenia? |url=https://tribune.com.pk/article/97102/why-is-pakistan-the-only-country-that-does-not-recognise-armenia |work=[[The Express Tribune]] |date=21 July 2020 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200809082221/https://tribune.com.pk/article/97102/why-is-pakistan-the-only-country-that-does-not-recognise-armenia |archive-date=9 August 2020}} Turkish-backed unrecognized [[Northern Cyprus]] (Turkish Cyprus) also supports Azerbaijan.{{cite web |title=Prime Minister Tatar stressed support of the Turkish Cypriot people for Azerbaijan |url=https://pio.mfa.gov.ct.tr/en/prime-minister-tatar-stressed-support-of-the-turkish-cypriot-people-for-azerbaijan/ |website=pio.mfa.gov.ct.tr |publisher=TRNC Public Information Office |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200811124507/https://pio.mfa.gov.ct.tr/en/prime-minister-tatar-stressed-support-of-the-turkish-cypriot-people-for-azerbaijan/ |archive-date=11 August 2020 |date=15 July 2020}} * {{cite web |last1=A |first1=k. |title=Full text of Minister of Foreign Affairs Emine Çolak's speech at the Annual Coordination Meeting of the Ministers of Foreign Affairs of the Organization of the Islamic Cooperation |url=https://mfa.gov.ct.tr/full-text-new-york-eit/ |website=mfa.gov.ct.tr |publisher=Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus Deputy Prime Ministry and Ministry of Foreign Affairs |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200811124907/https://mfa.gov.ct.tr/full-text-new-york-eit/ |archive-date=11 August 2020 |date=1 October 2015 |quote=...we are deeply concerned that [...] that Karabagh, an indivisible part of Azerbaijan, continues to be occupied by Armenia.}} The [[Organisation of Islamic Cooperation]] (OIC){{cite journal |title=OIC General Secretariat Condemns Armenia's Attack On The Tovuz Region In Azerbaijan |url=https://www.oic-oci.org/topic/?t_id=23655&t_ref=14098&lan=en |website=oic-oci.org |publisher=[[Organisation of Islamic Cooperation]] Nur |title=OIC rejects elections in 'occupied' Nagorno-Karabakh |url=https://www.aa.com.tr/en/politics/oic-rejects-elections-in-occupied-nagorno-karabakh/1788243 |agency=[[Anadolu Agency]] |date=1 the Armenia-Azerbaijan Nagorno-Karabakh Conflict |url=https://www.turkkon.org/en/haberler/turkic-council-secretary-general-on-the-armenia-azerbaijan-nagorno-karabakh-conflict_2032 |website=turkkon.org 2020}} * {{cite news |title=Turkic Council Secretary General strongly condemns Armenian provocation |url=https://azertag.az/en/xeber/Turkic_Council_Secretary_General_strongly_condemns_Armenian_provocation-1537039 |work=azertag.az |agency=[[Azerbaijan State News Agency]] |date=15 July 2020 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200807104650/https://azertag.az/en/xeber/Turkic_Council_Secretary_General_strongly_condemns_Armenian_provocation-1537039 |archive-date=7 August 2020}} have repeatedly supported the Azerbaijani position. Some member states of these organizations, namely [[Uzbekistan]]{{cite news |last1=Kangarli |first1=Gulu |title=President Mirziyoyev: Uzbekistan supports Azerbaijan's fair stance on Nagorno-Karabakh conflict |url=https://azertag.az/en/xeber/President_Mirziyoyev_Uzbekistan_supports_Azerbaijan_039s_fair_stance_on_Nagorno_Karabakh_conflict-1104674 |work=azertag.az |agency=[[Azerbaijan State News Agency]] |date=20 October 2017 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200809104246/https://azertag.az/en/xeber/President_Mirziyoyev_Uzbekistan_supports_Azerbaijan_039s_fair_stance_on_Nagorno_Karabakh_conflict-1104674 |archive-date=9 August 2020}} * {{cite web |title=Узбекистан и Азербайджан наращивают сотрудничество [Uzbekistan and Azerbaijan are increasing cooperation] |url=http://old.president.uz/ru/document/4824/ |website=old.president.uz |publisher=Press Service of the President of the Republic of Uzbekistan |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200809091426/http://old.president.uz/ru/document/4824/ |archive-date=9 August 2020 |language=ru |date=25 September 2010|quote=В этой связи позиция Республики Узбекистан по решению проблемы Нагорного Карабаха остается твердой и неизменной. Узбекистан открыто ее подтверждал при голосовании инициированных Азербайджаном соответствующих резолюций Генеральной Ассамблеи ООН в 2008 году. Узбекистан последовательно выступал и продолжает выступать за мирное, политическое решение нагорно-карабахского конфликта и при этом главным условием урегулирования считает обеспечение территориальной целостности и суверенитета Азербайджана.}} * {{cite web |title=Joint press statements of Presidents of Azerbaijan and Uzbekistan |url=https://en.president.az/articles/792/print |website=en.president.az |publisher=President of the Republic of Azerbaijan |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171123050850/https://en.president.az/articles/792/ |archive-date=23 November 2017 |date=27 September 2010 |quote=While describing Uzbekistan's position on the Armenian-Azerbaijani conflict over Nagorno-Karabakh, Islam Karimov said: [...] Uzbekistan considers the territorial integrity of Azerbaijan as one of the key preconditions for its settlement. I believe that this position is absolutely consistent with international standards, meets historical parallels. Uzbekistan's position on this issue remains unchanged: the territorial integrity of Azerbaijan is a sacred concept, and it must be followed in all solution options of this problem.}} * {{cite news |title=Uzbekistan supports Azerbaijan's fair stance on Nagorno-Karabakh conflict, Uzbek Envoy Ergashev |url=https://azertag.az/en/xeber/UZBEKISTAN_SUPPORTS_AZERBAIJANS_FAIR_STANCE_ON_NAGORNO_KARABAKH_UZBEK_ENVOY_ERGASHEV-568829 |work=azertag.az |agency=[[Azerbaijan State News Agency]] |date=5 September 2007 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200809104843/https://azertag.az/en/xeber/UZBEKISTAN_SUPPORTS_AZERBAIJANS_FAIR_STANCE_ON_NAGORNO_KARABAKH_UZBEK_ENVOY_ERGASHEV-568829 |archive-date=9 August 2020}} and [[Saudi Arabia]]{{cite web |last1=Alrmizan |first1=Mohammed |title=Azerbaijan and Saudi Arabia: Bilateral Opportunities in a Changing Middle East |url=http://kfcris.com/pdf/91ba14ab13aea57468aee2d0cd58c0bd5d89e0ee3e6cf.pdf |website=kfcris.com |publisher=[[King Faisal Foundation#King Faisal Center for Research and Islamic Studies|King Faisal Center for Research and Islamic Studies]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200809105610/http://kfcris.com/pdf/91ba14ab13aea57468aee2d0cd58c0bd5d89e0ee3e6cf.pdf |archive-date=9 August 2020 |page=5 |date=September 2019 |quote=In this matter, the Saudis have backed the right of Azerbaijan in the United Nations General Assembly meetings and in the OIC, asserting its internationally recognized authority over Nagorno-Karabakh. Furthermore, Saudi Arabia does not have yet any level of official or even unofficial ties with Armenia. This is because the Saudis have tended to side with Azerbaijan, especially on this particular issue.}} * {{cite news |last1=Pashkin |first1=Zumrud |title=Ambassador of Saudi Arabia: Our position on Palestinian and Nagorno-Karabakh conflicts is identical |url=https://ona.az/en/politics/ambassador-of-saudi-arabia-our-position-on-palestinian-and-nagorno-karabakh-conflicts-is-identical-7180 |work=ona.az |date=14 August 2019 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200809134215/https://ona.az/en/politics/ambassador-of-saudi-arabia-our-position-on-palestinian-and-nagorno-karabakh-conflicts-is-identical-7180 |archive-date=9 August 2020 |quote=The Kingdom fully and resolutely supports Azerbaijan's position on the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict and the territorial integrity of your country.}} * {{cite news |title=Sauid Arabia Will Not Change Its Position on Nagorno-Karabakh Problem |url=https://azertag.az/en/xeber/SAUDI_ARABIA_WILL_NOT_CHANGE_ITS_POSITION_ON_NAGORNO_KARABAKH_PROBLEM-592303 |work=azertag.az |agency=[[Azerbaijan State News Agency]] |date=14 July 2010 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200809110337/https://azertag.az/en/xeber/SAUDI_ARABIA_WILL_NOT_CHANGE_ITS_POSITION_ON_NAGORNO_KARABAKH_PROBLEM-592303 |archive-date=9 August 2020 |quote=...Speaker of the Saudi Arabia's Shura Council Abdullah Bin Mohammed Bin Ibrahim Al-Sheikh told journalists here. He underlined Saudi Arabia supports all international organizations' decisions and resolutions supporting resolution of the conflict based on territorial integrity of Azerbaijan.}} * {{cite news |title=Azerbaijan Report: February 28, 2003 |url=https://www.rferl.org/a/1340690.html |work=rferl.org |agency=[[RFE/RL]] |date=28 February 2003 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200809110155/https://www.rferl.org/a/1340690.html |archive-date=9 August 2020 |quote=Ali Hasan Jafarin, Saudi Arabia's ambassadors to Baku, said in an interview with the independent newspaper "525" that his country unanimously supports the Azerbaijani position on Nagorno-Karabakh in all international organizations and forums.}} have voiced support for Azerbaijan's position on their own repeatedly. Lebanon, on the other hand, has not supported OIC's pro-Azerbaijani resolutions.{{cite news |last1=Stepanian |first1=Ruzanna |title=Yerevan Decries Azeri Push For Muslim Support On Karabakh |url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/24417280.html |work=azatutyun.am |agency=[[RFE/RL]] |date=9 December 2011 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200825113850/https://www.azatutyun.am/a/24417280.html |archive-date=25 August 2020}} Lebanon's President [[Michel Suleiman]] "stressed that Lebanon has never supported OIC statements on Karabakh." The foreign minister said Palestine wants peaceful resolution of the conflict and liberation of occupied lands of Azerbaijan. “We call on the international community to exert pressure on Armenia in order to achieve peace”, Al-Malki stressed.}} and Israel{{cite journal |last1=Cornell |first1=Svante E. |author-link1=Svante E. Cornell |title=Geopolitics and Strategic Alignments in the Caucasus and Central Asia |journal=Perceptions: Journal of International Affairs |date=August 1999 |volume=IV |issue=2 |page=9 |url=http://sam.gov.tr/wp-content/uploads/2012/01/SVANTE-E.-CORNELL.pdf |publisher=Center for Strategic Research of the [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Turkey]] |issn=1300-8641 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200825103958/http://sam.gov.tr/wp-content/uploads/2012/01/SVANTE-E.-CORNELL.pdf |archive-date=25 August 2020 |quote=...Israel from the start took on an overtly pro-Azerbaijani stance in the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict.}}{{cite journal |last1=Khalifa-zadeh |first1=Mahir |title=Israel and Azerbaijan: To Counteract Iran |journal=Central Asia and the Caucasus |date=2012 |volume=13 |issue=3 |page=76 |url=https://www.ca-c.org/online/2012/journal_eng/cac-03/cacE-03-12.pdf |publisher=Institute for Central Asian and Caucasian Studies |quote=Israel has repeatedly declared that Tel Aviv supports Azerbaijan's territorial integrity. |archive-date=28 September 2021 |access-date=25 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210928233442/https://www.ca-c.org/online/2012/journal_eng/cac-03/cacE-03-12.pdf |url-status=dead }} have voiced support for Azerbaijan. ====2008 UN vote==== On 14 March 2008, the United Nations General Assembly adopted a resolution which "reaffirmed Azerbaijan's territorial integrity, expressing support for that country's internationally recognized borders and demanding the immediate withdrawal of all Armenian forces from all occupied territories there." It was adopted by a vote of 39 in favor to 7 against, while most countries either abstained or were absent. It was backed mostly by Muslim states{{cite news |title=Azerbaijan Withdraws Draft Karabakh Resolution From UN |url=https://www.rferl.org/a/Azerbaijan_Withdraws_Draft_Karabakh_Resolution_From_UN/2154542.html |work=rferl.org |agency=[[RFE/RL]] |date=10 September 2010 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200812165016/https://www.rferl.org/a/Azerbaijan_Withdraws_Draft_Karabakh_Resolution_From_UN/2154542.html |archive-date=12 August 2020 |quote=The U.S., Russia, and France had opposed a similar resolution which Baku managed to push through the UN assembly in March 2008. It was backed by 39 countries, most of them Islamic.}}{{cite news |last1=Mir – Ismail |first1=Alman |title=Azerbaijan, Trapped Between Palestinians and Israel, Takes a Pragmatic Position |url=https://jamestown.org/program/azerbaijan-trapped-between-palestinians-and-israel-takes-a-pragmatic-position/ |newspaper=Jamestown |publisher=[[Jamestown Foundation]] |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200812164715/https://jamestown.org/program/azerbaijan-trapped-between-palestinians-and-israel-takes-a-pragmatic-position/ |archive-date=12 August 2020 |date=21 January 2009 |quote=As a result, on March 14, 2008, it was mainly the Muslim nations that supported Azerbaijan's resolution on the Karabakh conflict at the UN General Assembly.}} (31 were [[Member states of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation|members of the OIC]]).{{efn|Afghanistan, Azerbaijan, Bahrain, Bangladesh, Brunei, Comoros, Djibouti, Gambia, Indonesia, Iraq, Jordan, Kuwait, Libya, Malaysia, Maldives, Morocco, Niger, Nigeria, Oman, Pakistan, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Senegal, Sierra Leone, Somalia, Sudan, Turkey, Uganda, United Arab Emirates, Uzbekistan, Yemen.}} Non-Muslim states that supported the resolution included three post-Soviet states: Georgia, Moldova, Ukraine, and five other nations: Cambodia, Colombia, Myanmar, Serbia, and Tuvalu. Thus, it was supported by seven OSCE members;{{efn|Azerbaijan, Georgia, Moldova, Serbia, Turkey, Ukraine, Uzbekistan}} one [[NATO]] member (Turkey) and no EU member state. It was opposed by [[Angola]], Armenia, [[France]], [[India]], Russia, the United States, and [[Vanuatu]].{{cite news |title=General Assembly Adopts Resolution Reaffirming Territorial Integrity of Azerbaijan, Demanding Withdrawal of All Armenian Forces |url=https://www.un.org/press/en/2008/ga10693.doc.htm |access-date=7 August 2020 |work=un.org |publisher=[[United Nations]] |date=14 March 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200601021639/https://www.un.org/press/en/2008/ga10693.doc.htm |archive-date=1 June 2020}} The OSCE Minsk Group co-chair countries (France, US, Russia) voted against the resolution. They argued that it "selectively propagates only certain of [the basic] principles to the exclusion of others, without considering the Co-Chairs' proposal in its balanced entirety." The co-chair countries called it a unilateral resolution, which "threatens to undermine the peace process," but reaffirmed their "support for the territorial integrity of Azerbaijan, and thus do not recognize the independence of NK."{{cite web |title=Statement of the Co-Chairs of the OSCE Minsk Group |url=https://www.osce.org/mg/49564 |website=[[osce.org]] |archive-url=https://archive.today/20200812171034/https://www.osce.org/mg/49564 |archive-date=12 August 2020 |date=17 March 2008}} ==Azerbaijani laundromat== In 2017, an [[Azerbaijani laundromat]] [[Money laundering|money-laundering]] scheme organized by Azerbaijan was revealed by the [[Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project|OCCRP]]. The report revealed that between 2012 and 2014, Azerbaijan created a [[slush fund]] of USD $2.9 billion used to bribe European and American politicians, journalists, lawmakers, and academics to lobby for Azerbaijani interests abroad, including promoting a pro-Azerbaijan agenda for the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict. This form of bribery has been referred to as "[[caviar diplomacy]]".{{cite web |url=https://www.occrp.org/en/azerbaijanilaundromat/bakus-man-in-america |title=Baku's Man in America |last=Wrate |first=Jonny |date=23 May 2018 |website=[[Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project]] |access-date=23 May 2018 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180523214159/https://www.occrp.org/en/azerbaijanilaundromat/bakus-man-in-america |archive-date=23 May 2018}}{{cite web |last=Canbäck |first=Rasmus |date=9 May 2022 |title=Svante Cornell's dealings in Azerbaijan despite funding from MFA of Sweden |url=https://blankspot.se/svante-cornells-dealings-in-azerbaijan-despite-funding-from-mfa-of-sweden/ |access-date=9 May 2022 |website=[[Blankspot]]}}{{cite web |url=https://www.rai.it/dl/docs/1481650085978caviar_democracyOKOK.pdf |title=Caviar Democracy |last1=Mondani |first1=Paolo |last2=Ciccolella |first2=Cataldo |date=21 November 2016 |website=Network Rai |access-date=13 January 2017 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170113024840/https://www.rai.it/dl/docs/1481650085978caviar_democracyOKOK.pdf |archive-date=13 January 2017}} The laundering scheme has operated by wiring millions of euros into the private bank accounts of influential Western figures and by providing them with luxurious trips to Azerbaijan. The [[European Azerbaijani Society]] (TEAS) lobbying group has played a large role in this by hiring European PR professionals, pariliment members, and former ministers.{{cite news |last=Barseghyan |first=Anna |date=16 November 2020 |title=The difference between Armenian and Azerbaijani lobbying activities in Europe |url=https://neweasterneurope.eu/2020/11/16/the-difference-between-armenian-and-azerbaijani-lobbying-activities-in-europe/ |work=[[New Eastern Europe]] |access-date=26 November 2020 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201126204938/https://neweasterneurope.eu/2020/11/16/the-difference-between-armenian-and-azerbaijani-lobbying-activities-in-europe/ |archive-date=26 November 2020}} Azerbaijani-American businessman Adil Baguirov had been lobbying in Washington through secret funding from Azerbaijan’s state oil company since 2013. Baguirov runs the non-profit Houston-based US Azeris Network, which received a $253,150 transfer after organizing and hosting a conference in Baku attended by 10 American members of Congress. In 2003, Baguirov began working as Special Advisor on Russia and the former Soviet Union to Congressman [[Curt Weldon]]. Weldon and another Congressman, [[Solomon Ortiz]], both founded the Congressional Azerbaijan Caucus in 2004. The Congressional Azerbaijan Caucus was a frequent recipient of Azerbaijani laundromat funds. From 2008 to 2016, Baguirov was invited almost annually by the [[United States House Appropriations Subcommittee on State, Foreign Operations, and Related Programs]] to suggest economic and military aid budgets for Azerbaijan and Armenia. In 2008, Baguirov lobbied for greater aid to be given to Azerbaijan, citing equity and neutrality. But by 2012, Baguirov lobbied for aid to Armenia to be reduced to zero, while requesting that Azerbaijan be granted $26 million from [[United States Agency for International Development|USAID]] and $3.9 million in military aid. The 2012 laundromat investigation revealed several bank transfers in 2012, totalling more than USD $9 million, made to the Hungarian [[MKB Bank]] account in [[Budapest]] right around the time when, amid international controversy, the Hungarian government extradited the convicted Azerbaijani murderer [[Ramil Safarov]] to Azerbaijan.{{Cite web|title=Hungarian businessman connected to the Azerbaijani Laundromat makes a fortune in the ventilator business|url=https://english.atlatszo.hu/2020/11/05/hungarian-businessman-connected-to-the-azerbaijani-laundromat-makes-a-fortune-in-the-ventilator-business/|access-date=6 March 2021|website=English|date=5 November 2020 }} Several media outlets suggested a connection between [[Viktor Orbán]]'s visit to Baku in June and the first instalment of $7.6 million transferred to the bank account in July, since by the end of August Safarov was handed over to Azerbaijan.{{Cite web|last=Novak|first=-Benjamin|date=16 November 2017|title=Big push in Hungary and EP for comprehensive inquiry into Azeri Laundromat|url=https://budapestbeacon.com/big-push-hungary-ep-comprehensive-inquiry-azeri-laundromat/|access-date=6 March 2021|website=The Budapest Beacon|language=en-US|archive-date=12 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210612140019/https://budapestbeacon.com/big-push-hungary-ep-comprehensive-inquiry-azeri-laundromat/|url-status=dead}}{{Cite web|date=5 September 2017|title=Azeri guruló dollárok Budapesten a baltás gyilkos kiadása idején {{!}} atlatszo.hu|url=https://atlatszo.hu/2017/09/05/azeri-gurulo-dollarok-budapesten-a-baltas-gyilkos-kiadasa-idejen/|access-date=6 March 2021|language=hu}}{{Cite web|last=Tbg|date=5 September 2017|title=Akkor érkeztek azeri dollármilliók egy budapesti számlára, amikor az ország kiadta a baltás gyilkost|url=https://444.hu/2017/09/05/akkor-erkeztek-azeri-dollarmilliok-egy-budapesti-szamlara-amikor-az-orszag-kiadta-a-baltas-gyilkost|access-date=6 March 2021|website=444}}{{Cite web|last=Balogh|first=Eva S.|date=6 September 2017|title=The "Azerbaijan Laundromat" and Orbán's Hungary|url=https://hungarianspectrum.org/2017/09/06/the-azerbaijan-laundromat-and-orbans-hungary/|access-date=6 March 2021|website=Hungarian Spectrum|language=en-US|archive-date=11 July 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210711101751/https://hungarianspectrum.org/2017/09/06/the-azerbaijan-laundromat-and-orbans-hungary/|url-status=usurped}}{{Cite web|title=Report: $7 Million Payment Tied to Hungary's Extradition of Azerbaijani Axe-Murderer {{!}} Eurasianet|url=https://eurasianet.org/report-7-million-payment-tied-to-hungarys-extradition-of-azerbaijani-axe-murderer|access-date=6 March 2021|website=eurasianet.org|language=en}}{{Cite web|title=Atlatszo is joining forces with Hungarian MEP to urge a comprehensive inquiry into the Azerbaijani Laundromat|url=https://english.atlatszo.hu/2017/11/17/atlatszo-is-joining-forces-with-hungarian-mep-to-urge-a-comprehensive-inquiry-into-the-azerbaijani-laundromat/|access-date=14 March 2021|website=English|date=17 November 2017 }} In 2017, following a series of critical reports and concern expressed by many members of the [[Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe]] (PACE), the Assembly's Bureau decided to set up an independent, external body to investigate allegations of corruption in PACE.{{Cite web |title=PACE Independent External Investigation Body released a report on the allegations of corruption within the Assembly {{!}} Civic Solidarity |url=https://www.civicsolidarity.org/article/1544/pace-independent-external-investigation-body-released-report-allegations-corruption |access-date=21 February 2021 |website=www.civicsolidarity.org}}{{Cite web |date=22 April 2018 |title=Council of Europe members suspected of corruption, inquiry reveals |url=http://www.theguardian.com/law/2018/apr/22/council-of-europe-members-suspected-of-corruption-inquiry-reveals |access-date=21 February 2021 |website=the Guardian |language=en}} The investigation body's final report was published in April 2018,{{Cite web |last=PACE |date=15 April 2018 |title=REPORT of the Independent Investigation Body on the allegations of corruption within the Parliamentary Assembly |url=http://assembly.coe.int/Communication/IBAC/IBAC-GIAC-Report-EN.pdf |url-status=live |access-date=7 March 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180423035154/http://assembly.coe.int/Communication/IBAC/IBAC-GIAC-Report-EN.pdf |archive-date=23 April 2018}} finding "strong suspicions of corruptive conduct involving members of the Assembly" and naming a number of members and former members as having breached the Assembly's Code of Conduct. Many of the members or former members mentioned in the report were sanctioned: four members{{Cite web |last=PACE |date=16 May 2018 |title=Allegations of corruption within the Assembly: committee deprives four members of certain rights |url=https://pace.coe.int/en/news/7066 |url-status=live |access-date=7 March 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200926180010/https://pace.coe.int/en/news/7066 |archive-date=26 September 2020}} were deprived of certain rights and 14 members,{{cite web |last=PACE |date=27 June 2018 |title=Committee deprives fourteen former members of the right to access Council of Europe premises |url=https://pace.coe.int/en/news/7142|url-status=live |access-date=7 March 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200929054029/https://pace.coe.int/en/news/7142 |archive-date=29 September 2020}} accused of accepting gifts and bribes from the government of Azerbaijan, were expelled from the Assembly's premises for life.{{cite web|title=Council of Europe expels 14 members accused of corruption in Azerbaijan case – EU-OCS – European Observatory of Crimes and Security|url=https://eu-ocs.com/council-of-europe-expels-14-members-accused-of-corruption-in-azerbaijan-case/|access-date=21 February 2021|website=eu-ocs.com}} One named individual was German politician and former [[Parliamentary State Secretary]] [[Eduard Lintner]]. It was revealed Lintner had lobbied on behalf of the Azerbaijani government since as early as 2009 when he founded the Society for Promoting German-Azerbaijani Relations.{{cite news |last=Winter |first=Chase |date=6 September 2017 |title=Azerbaijan slush fund ensnares German ex-lawmaker |url=https://www.dw.com/en/azerbaijani-laundromat-brings-german-ex-politician-into-spotlight/a-40379377 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170906163721/https://www.dw.com/en/azerbaijani-laundromat-brings-german-ex-politician-into-spotlight/a-40379377 |archive-date=6 September 2017 |access-date=6 September 2017 |work=[[Deutsche Welle]]}} == Major ceasefire agreements and international mediation == {{Further information|Madrid Principles|Tehran Communiqué|Zheleznovodsk Communiqué}} Two major armistices occurred upon following the First and Second Nagorno-Karabakh War. Both ceasefires were brokered by Russia, with the first also being mediated by the [[OSCE Minsk Group]] (Russia, US, France). === Bishkek Protocol === {{Main|Bishkek Protocol}} A Russian-brokered ceasefire was signed in May 1994 and peace talks, mediated by the OSCE Minsk Group have been held ever since by Armenia and Azerbaijan. The 1994 Bishkek Protocol called for both sides to cease hostilities and engage in dialogue aimed at demilitarization of the region, return of refugees, and the creation of a [[Commonwealth of Independent States|CIS]] peacekeeping force.{{cite report |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/resrep07026.5 |title=Cis Collective Peacekeeping Forces |last=Allison |first=Roy |date=1994 |publisher=European Union Institute for Security Studies (EUISS)}}{{cite web |title=The Bishkek Protocol: Bishkek, 5 May 1994 |url=https://www.peaceagreements.org/viewmasterdocument/310}}{{Cite web |title=Bishkek Protocol {{!}} UN Peacemaker |url=https://peacemaker.un.org/armeniaazerbaijan-bishkekprotocol94 |access-date=28 July 2023 |website=peacemaker.un.org}} Azerbaijan has repeatedly accused the Minsk Group (Russia, US, France) of being pro-Armenian.{{cite news|last1=Rahimov|first1=Rahim|date=22 July 2020|title=Armenian-Azerbaijani Border Clashes: The Russian Dimension and Beyond|work=jamestown.org|agency=[[Jamestown Foundation]]|url=https://jamestown.org/program/armenian-azerbaijani-border-clashes-the-russian-dimension-and-beyond/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20200811141329/https://jamestown.org/program/armenian-azerbaijani-border-clashes-the-russian-dimension-and-beyond/|archive-date=11 August 2020|quote=Azerbaijani President Ilham Aliyev had lambasted the Minsk Group co-chairs (Russia, France and the United States) in an unusually explicit manner for what he described as their ineffectiveness and alleged pro-Armenian bias (President.az, July 6).}}{{cite news|date=21 March 2016|title=Aliyev Again Lambastes 'Pro-Armenian' Mediators|work=azatutyun.am|agency=[[RFE/RL]]|url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/27626496.html|archive-url=https://archive.today/20200811141711/https://www.azatutyun.am/a/27626496.html|archive-date=11 August 2020}}{{cite news|last1=Greene|first1=Richard|date=25 March 2002|title=Armenia/Azerbaijan: As Minsk Group Marks 10 Years, Karabakh Peace Appears More Elusive Than Ever|work=rferl.org|agency=[[RFE/RL]]|url=https://www.rferl.org/a/1099187.html|archive-url=https://archive.today/20200811141937/https://www.rferl.org/a/1099187.html|archive-date=11 August 2020|quote=....Azerbaijani Foreign Minister Vilayat Guliev publicly accused the body of pro-Armenian bias.}} In 1996, when France was chosen by the OSCE to co-chair the Minsk Group, Azerbaijan asked the OSCE to reconsider the decision because France was perceived by Azerbaijan as pro-Armenian.{{cite book|last1=Cornell|first1=Svante|title=Small Nations and Great Powers: A Study of Ethnopolitical Conflict in the Caucasus|date=2005|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1135796693|page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=G_qQAgAAQBAJ&dq=osce%27s+choice+of+france+as+finland%27s+successor+perceived+france+pro-armenian&pg=PA102 102]|author-link1=Svante Cornell}} Svante Cornell argued in 1997 that France, the US and Russia are "more or less biased towards Armenia in the conflict."{{cite journal|last1=Cornell|first1=Svante E.|date=Summer 1997|title=Undeclared War: The Nagorno-Karabakh Conflict Reconsidered|url=https://isdp.eu/content/uploads/images/stories/isdp-main-pdf/1997_cornell_undeclared-war.pdf|url-status=dead|journal=Journal of South Asian and Middle Eastern Studies|volume=XX|issue=4|page=23|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200811141036/https://isdp.eu/content/uploads/images/stories/isdp-main-pdf/1997_cornell_undeclared-war.pdf|archive-date=11 August 2020|quote=As a result, three of the permanent members of the U.N. Security Council are more or less biased towards Armenia in the conflict (including France, where a substantial Armenian minority exists, which has always been politically active.)|author-link1=Svante Cornell}}{{cite web |url= https://www.dn.se/arkiv/nyheter/chef-for-ud-finansierat-institut-har-nara-koppling-till-diktatur/ |title= Chef för UD-finansierat institut har nära koppling till diktatur |language=Swedish |trans-title=The head of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs-funded institute is closely linked to dictatorship |last= |first= |date= 19 December 2017 |website= Dagens Nyheter |access-date= 8 July 2021 |quote=}} In 2018 Azerbaijan accused the US and France of bias for allowing [[Bako Sahakyan]], the then president of Artsakh, to visit their countries.{{cite news|last1=Kucera|first1=Joshua|date=15 March 2018|title=Nagorno Karabakh Leader Makes Unprecedented Visit to Washington|agency=[[EurasiaNet]]|url=https://eurasianet.org/nagorno-karabakh-leader-makes-unprecedented-visit-to-washington|archive-url=https://archive.today/20200816085800/https://eurasianet.org/nagorno-karabakh-leader-makes-unprecedented-visit-to-washington|archive-date=16 August 2020}}{{cite news|date=19 November 2018|title=Azerbaijan Protests Against Karabakh Leader's Visits To U.S., France|work=azatutyun.am|agency=[[RFE/RL]]|url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/29609466.html|archive-url=https://archive.today/20200816090157/https://www.azatutyun.am/a/29609466.html|archive-date=16 August 2020}} === 2020 ceasefire agreement === {{Main|2020 Nagorno-Karabakh ceasefire agreement}} On 9 November 2020, Armenia and Azerbaijan signed a ceasefire under mediation from Russia, with the Republic of Artsakh also agreeing to end hostilities.{{Cite web |date=9 November 2020 |title=Президент непризнанной НКР дал согласие закончить войну |url=https://ria.ru/20201110/karabakh-1583847338.html |access-date=9 November 2020 |work=[[RIA Novosti]] |language=ru}} According to the agreement, both sides retained control of the positions they held at midnight. Armenia returned the territories surrounding Nagorno-Karabakh that it had occupied since the 1990s. Azerbaijan also retained control over one-third of Nagorno-Karabakh proper that it had captured during the war: including [[Shusha District (NKAO)|Shusha]] and [[Hadrut District (NKAO)|Hadrut]].{{Cite web |title=Violence and Politics in Armenia-Azerbaijan Relations |url=https://bakuresearchinstitute.org/en/violence-and-politics-in-armenia-azerbaijan-relations/ |access-date=27 June 2023 |website=Baku Research Institute |date=14 October 2022 |language=en-US |quote=As a result of the 2020 war, however, Azerbaijan received all territories around Soviet-era Nagorno Karabakh that were occupied by Armenian forces during the first Karabakh war, plus the two regions of Nagorno Karabakh proper: Shushi/Shusha and Hadrut.}}{{Cite journal |last=Cheterian |first=Vicken |date=20 October 2022 |title=Technological determinism or strategic advantage? Comparing the two Karabakh Wars between Armenia and Azerbaijan |journal=Journal of Strategic Studies |volume=47 |issue=2 |language=en |pages=214–237 |doi=10.1080/01402390.2022.2127093 |s2cid=253061240 |issn=0140-2390 |quote=The Armenian side also lost territories within the former NKAO, namely the district of Hadrut and the strategic town of Shusha/Shushi, areas that were not even considered for handover to Azerbaijan during the long years of diplomatic negotiations between the two wars.|doi-access=free }}{{Cite news |last=Lynch |first=Ian J. |date=18 November 2020 |title=A cold winter for peace in Nagorno-Karabakh? |language=en |work=Ahval |url=https://ahvalnews.com/nagorno-karabakh/cold-winter-peace-nagorno-karabakh |access-date=27 June 2023 |quote=The new agreement allows Azerbaijan to keep the territory it took by force, including Shusha and Hadrut, within the historic boundaries of Nagorno-Karabakh. It also requires Armenian forces to turn over other territories they have occupied for the last 26 years, including the so-called Lachin corridor, which is Nagorno-Karabakh’s primary link to Armenia proper.}} In total, the Armenian side lost roughly 75% of the territories in and around Nagorno-Karabakh that it controlled prior to the war.{{Cite web |last=Hauer |first=Neil |title=Armenia Is Still Grieving |url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2021/04/24/armenia-azerbaijan-war-nagorno-karabakh-aftermath/ |access-date=23 December 2022 |website=Foreign Policy |date=24 April 2021 |language=en-US}} Until 2020, an independent Azerbaijan had never exercised de facto control over the region.{{Cite journal |last=BARSEGHYAN |first=Angela |date=2022 |title=State Regulation of the Construction Sector of the Republic of Artsakh |journal=Alternative |pages=42–47 |doi=10.55528/18292828-2022.4-42 |doi-broken-date=6 July 2025 |s2cid=255255166 |issn=1829-2828|doi-access=free }}{{Citation |last=Gardner |first=Anne-Marie |title=Nagorno-Karabakh: Balancing Standards? |date=2011 |url=https://doi.org/10.1057/9780230117600_4 |work=Democratic Governance and Non-State Actors |pages=71–103 |editor-last=Gardner |editor-first=Anne-Marie |access-date=28 July 2023 |place=New York |publisher=Palgrave Macmillan US |language=en |doi=10.1057/9780230117600_4 |isbn=978-0-230-11760-0|url-access=subscription }}{{Citation |title=Territorial Disputes |date=1 November 2006 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.14217/9781848598799-9-en |work=A Manual of International Dispute Resolution |pages=35–51 |access-date=28 July 2023 |publisher=Commonwealth |doi=10.14217/9781848598799-9-en |doi-broken-date=6 July 2025 |isbn=978-1-84859-879-9|url-access=subscription }}{{Cite web |date=5 December 2012 |title=A Monument to Former President of Azerbaijan Now Source of Conflict in Mexico City |url=https://www.mic.com/articles/20180/a-monument-to-former-president-of-azerbaijan-now-source-of-conflict-in-mexico-city |access-date=28 July 2023 |website=Mic |language=en}}{{cite web |last=Ambartsumian |first=Yelena |date=28 February 2021 |title=Why Armenian Cultural Heritage Threatens Azerbaijan's Claims to Nagorno-Karabakh |url=http://hyperallergic.com/614619/why-armenian-cultural-heritage-threatens-azerbaijans-claims-to-nagorno-karabakh/ |access-date=28 July 2023 |website=Hyperallergic |language=en-US}}{{Cite book|last1=Krüger |first1=Heiko |title=Self-Determination and Secession in International Law |chapter=Nagorno-Karabakh |date=2014 |pages=214–232 |url=https://academic.oup.com/book/3811/chapter-abstract/145283746?login=false |doi=10.1093/acprof:oso/9780198702375.003.0011 |isbn=978-0-19-870237-5 }} Approximately 2,000 Russian soldiers were deployed as peacekeeping forces around Nagorno-Karabakh with a mandate of at least five years. The peacekeepers were also given mandate over the Lachin corridor which following remains the only passage between Armenia and Nagorno-Karabakh. The alternative [[Vardenis]]–Martakert route has been closed by Azerbaijan since it took control of the [[Dadivank]]-[[Sotk]] section (in [[Kalbajar]]) during the Second Nagorno-Karabakh War (2020).{{cite web |date=8 December 2020 |title=Millions of Dollars of Infrastructure Lost in the Karabakh War |url=https://www.civilnet.am/en/news/474786/millions-of-dollars-of-infrastructure-lost-in-the-karabakh-war/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210423120035/https://www.civilnet.am/news/474786/millions-of-dollars-of-infrastructure-lost-in-the-karabakh-war/?lang=en |archive-date=23 April 2021 |access-date=23 December 2022 |website=Civilnet |language=en-US}}{{cite web |first= |date=November–December 2020 |title=Protection Monitoring Armenia Report #1 |url=https://www.unhcr.org/am/wp-content/uploads/sites/123/2022/02/2020-11-12_Armenia-Protection-Monitoring-Report.pdf |access-date=5 January 2023 |website=United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees |publisher=The UN Refugee Agency |quote=On 24th November 2020 the Martakert-Karvachar-Vardenis highway was closed; thus, the only operational route between NK and Armenia now is via the Lachin corridor.}} As per the agreement, Armenia returned all territory it had occupied around Nagorno-Karabakh and Azerbaijan retained control over one-third of Nagorno-Karabakh proper that it captured. The 2020 agreement gave [[Peacekeeping operations in Nagorno-Karabakh|Russian peacekeepers]] a temporary but renewable mandate to the region. Following the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh War, both Russia and the European Union have increased their presence in Armenia along the border with Azerbaijan in order to improve stability of the border and deter offensives from Azerbaijan.{{Cite web |date=25 April 2023 |title=Treading a Tightrope on the Armenian Border: Reviewing the First Two Months of the EU's New Mission in the South Caucasus |url=https://caucasuswatch.de/en/insights/treading-a-tightrope-on-the-armenian-border-reviewing-the-first-two-months-of-the-eus-new-mission-in-the-south-caucasus.html |access-date=19 June 2023 |website=Caucasus Watch |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=Armenia: Growing fears of another war with Azerbaijan – DW – 03/28/2023 |url=https://www.dw.com/en/armenia-growing-fears-of-another-war-with-azerbaijan/a-65156669 |access-date=28 July 2023 |website=dw.com |language=en}} Upon Armenia's request, Russia's Federal Security Service expanded its patrols within Armenia and the EU contributed a civilian monitoring mission. However, while both entities have deterred the possibility of full-scale warfare, they have been unable to fully prevent Azerbaijan's goals.{{Cite web |date=25 April 2023 |title=Treading a Tightrope on the Armenian Border: Reviewing the First Two Months of the EU's New Mission in the South Caucasus |url=https://caucasuswatch.de/en/insights/treading-a-tightrope-on-the-armenian-border-reviewing-the-first-two-months-of-the-eus-new-mission-in-the-south-caucasus.html |access-date=19 June 2023 |website=Caucasus Watch |language=en |quote=In an ironic twist, the unarmed European observers in Armenia and the armed Russian peacekeepers have begun to look alike, both preventing full-scale warfare but unable to fully contain Baku's ambitions. The 'Russian peacekeepers,' political scientist Nerses Kopalyan commented sharply, 'function more like an impotent observation mission than an armed contingent.'}} Russia and the European Union have criticized each other's presence within Armenia. The European Union encouraged Armenia to seek alternative security alliances given "Russia's alleged readiness to guarantee the security of Armenia has proven to be non-existent."{{Cite web |last=Kovatchev |first=Andrey |title=Report on EU-Armenia relations {{!}} A9-0036/2023 {{!}} European Parliament |url=https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/A-9-2023-0036_EN.html |access-date=18 March 2023 |website=www.europarl.europa.eu |language=en |quote="Whereas Russia's alleged readiness to guarantee the security of Armenia has proven to be non-existent, as demonstrated by its lack of response to Azerbaijan's continuous attacks even in the light of Armenia's attempt to resort to the Collective Security Treaty Organization (CSTO); [The European Parliament] Recalls that the clauses of the CSTO were not activated either during the 2020 war or in September 2022, despite Armenian requests; encourages Armenia, in order to ensure better protection of its sovereignty and territorial integrity, to consider the possibility of diversifying its partnerships and potential security alliances with its regional and Euro-Atlantic partners, as its long-standing reliance on Russia and its allies in the CSTO has proved insufficient."}} Russia, in turn, criticized the EU and Armenia for implementing the civilian monitoring in Armenia and claims it is an effort by the West to diminish Russia's power in the region.{{Cite news |last=Buniatian |first=Heghine |date=18 January 2023 |title=Moscow Unhappy With EU's New Monitoring Mission In Armenia |language=hy |work=«Ազատ Եվրոպա/Ազատություն» ռադիոկայան |url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/32229358.html |access-date=19 June 2023}}{{Cite web |date=18 January 2023 |title=EU moves onto Putin's turf with new Armenia monitoring mission |url=https://www.politico.eu/article/eropean-union-putins-turf-new-armenia-monitoring-mission/ |access-date=28 July 2023 |website=POLITICO |language=en}} === 2023 ceasefire agreement === {{Main|2023 Nagorno-Karabakh ceasefire agreement}} A day after the resuming of hostilities, a ceasefire agreement was announced with the government of the Republic of Artsakh agreed to disarm and to enter into talks with the government of Azerbaijan regarding the reintegration of the territory.{{cite news |date=20 September 2023 |title=Nagorno-Karabakh forces agree Azerbaijan demand to disarm |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-66863702 |url-status=live |access-date=20 September 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230920104937/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-66863702 |archive-date=20 September 2023}} === 2024 Russian peacekeepers withdrawal === On 12 June 2024, the Russian peacekeepers completed their withdrawal from the Nagorno-Karabakh region which had started in April that year, following an agreement between President Putin and his Azerbaijani counterpart Aliyev.{{cite web |date=12 June 2024 |title=Azerbaijan Says Russian Peacekeepers Have Completed Withdrawal |url=https://www.thedefensepost.com/2024/06/12/azerbaijan-russian-peacekeepers-withdrawal/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241106195620/https://thedefensepost.com/2024/06/12/azerbaijan-russian-peacekeepers-withdrawal/ |archive-date=6 Nov 2024 |publisher=The Defense Post}} === 2025 Armenia–Azerbaijan peace agreement === [[File:P20250808DT-1377 President Donald Trump signs a trilateral joint declaration with President Ilham Aliyev of Azerbaijan and Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan of Armenia.jpg|thumb|Azerbaijani President [[Ilham Aliyev]], US President [[Donald Trump]] and Armenian Prime Minister [[Nikol Pashinyan]] signing a trilateral joint declaration in Washington, D.C., 8 August 2025]] On 8 August 2025, Armenian Prime Minister [[Nikol Pashinyan]] and Azerbaijani President [[Ilham Aliyev]] signed [[Armenia–Azerbaijan peace agreement|a peace agreement]] in a ceremony hosted by US President [[Donald Trump]] in the [[White House]],{{cite news |title=Leaders of Armenia and Azerbaijan shake hands and sign deal at White House peace summit |url=https://apnews.com/article/donald-trump-white-house-armenia-azerbaijan-069379e9c4a058c96af38afbf4684829 |work=AP News |date=9 August 2025 |language=en }} laying groundwork to end the more than 35-year conflict between Armenia and Azerbaijan.{{Cite news |last=Cancryn |first=Alejandra Jaramillo, Adam |date=8 August 2025 |title=Trump touts his favorite type of foreign deal — promoting peace in Armenia-Azerbaijan with some personal branding {{!}} CNN Politics |url=https://www.cnn.com/2025/08/08/politics/strategic-armenia-azerbaijan-corridor-named-after-trump |access-date=8 August 2025 |work=[[CNN]] |language=en}} == Political status == {{Main|Political status of Nagorno-Karabakh}} The political status of Nagorno-Karabakh remained unresolved from its declaration of independence from the Soviet Union in 1991 until its dissolution in 2024.{{cite web |date=4 March 2023 |title=Nagorno-Karabakh: The Volatile Core of the South Caucasus |url=https://geohistory.today/nagorno-karabakh/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230525021247/https://geohistory.today/nagorno-karabakh/ |archive-date=25 May 2023 |access-date=9 June 2023 |language=en-US |quote=The Republic of Nagorno-Karabakh is one of four frozen conflicts that emerged after the collapse of the Soviet Union. Legally recognized as a part of Azerbaijan, it declared independence in 1991 and then defended that independence in a war with Azerbaijan that lasted until 1994. While it enjoys no international recognition of sovereignty, Nagorno-Karabakh has been de facto independent since its declaration and has been supported militarily and economically by neighboring Armenia.}}{{Cite web |title=Conflicts in Transcaucasia |url=https://assembly.coe.int/nw/xml/XRef/X2H-Xref-ViewHTML.asp?FileID=7753&lang=EN |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210511145258/http://assembly.coe.int/nw/xml/XRef/X2H-Xref-ViewHTML.asp?FileID=7753&lang=en |archive-date=11 May 2021 |access-date=9 June 2023 |website=assembly.coe.int |quote=At the end of 1991 the parties were in stark confrontation after Azerbaijan abolished Nagorno-Karabakh's autonomous status and the latter declared independence after the plebiscite-referendum of 10 December 1991.}} Within the Soviet Union, the region was an ethnic Armenian [[autonomous oblast]] of the Azerbaijan Soviet Socialist Republic. From 1994 to 2024, the United Nations Security Council, OSCE Minsk Group, and other bodies made various statements and proposed dialogue initiatives; none of them successful. The Republic of Artsakh has not been recognized by any country, including Armenia, although international mediators and human rights organizations have emphasized self-determination for the local Armenian population, both as an internationally recognized right but also as a form of genocide prevention.{{Cite news |last=Bedevian |first=Astghik |date=2 March 2023 |title=German Leader Backs Karabakh's Right To Self-Determination |language=hy |work=«Ազատ Եվրոպա/Ազատություն» ռադիոկայան |url=https://www.azatutyun.am/a/32296669.html |access-date=18 July 2023}}{{Cite web |date=30 May 2023 |title=A Peace to End All Peace? Statement on the International Actors Sponsoring So-Called Peace Negotiations Between Armenia and Azerbaijan |url=https://www.lemkininstitute.com/statements-new-page/a-peace-to-end-all-peace%3F--statement-on-the-international-actors-sponsoring-so-called-peace-negotiations-between-armenia-and-azerbaijan- |website=The Lemkin Institute for Genocide Prevention}}{{Cite web |title=Kennan Cable No. 81: What's at Stake in Nagorno-Karabakh: U.S. Interests and the Risk of Ethnic Cleansing |url=https://www.wilsoncenter.org/publication/kennan-cable-no-81-whats-stake-nagorno-karabakh-us-interests-and-risk-ethnic-cleansing |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230404191938/https://www.wilsoncenter.org/publication/kennan-cable-no-81-whats-stake-nagorno-karabakh-us-interests-and-risk-ethnic-cleansing |archive-date=4 April 2023 |access-date=4 April 2023 |website=www.wilsoncenter.org |date=4 April 2023 |language=en |quote=For nearly 30 years, they built a self-proclaimed independent republic with democratic elections, a free press, and a range of public institutions. Officially, it remained within the territorial boundaries of Azerbaijan, unrecognized by any foreign country, though international mediators made reference to the right of self-determination for local Armenians as part of ongoing peace talks.}}{{Cite web |date=23 May 2023 |title=A Peace to End All Peace? Statement on the International Actors Sponsoring So-Called Peace Negotiations Between Armenia and Azerbaijan |url=https://www.lemkininstitute.com/statements-new-page/a-peace-to-end-all-peace%3F--statement-on-the-international-actors-sponsoring-so-called-peace-negotiations-between-armenia-and-azerbaijan- |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230531140324/https://www.lemkininstitute.com/statements-new-page/a-peace-to-end-all-peace%3F--statement-on-the-international-actors-sponsoring-so-called-peace-negotiations-between-armenia-and-azerbaijan- |archive-date=31 May 2023 |access-date=9 June 2023 |website=Lemkin Institute for Genocide Prevention |quote=The Lemkin Institute believes that, given the circumstances, the self-determination of the people of Artsakh is a form of genocide prevention in addition to a right recognized by the Charter of the United Nations and several human rights treaties and declarations, which has become part of international jus cogens. Self-determination is further a recognized right of all peoples under oppressive colonial regimes.}} Following the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh War, Azerbaijan has rescinded its offer of special status or autonomy to its ethnic Armenian residents and instead insisted on their "integration" into Azerbaijan.{{Cite web |last=Isayev |first=Heydar |date=20 April 2022 |title=Azerbaijan quiet as Karabakh negotiations progress |url=https://eurasianet.org/azerbaijan-quiet-as-karabakh-negotiations-progress |access-date=2 January 2023 |website=eurasianet.org |language=en}}{{Cite web |last=Vock |first=Ido |date=8 June 2023 |title=Fear and loathing in Armenia |url=https://www.newstatesman.com/world/europe/2023/06/fear-loathing-in-armenia-azerbaijan |access-date=9 June 2023 |website=New Statesman |language=en-US |quote=President Aliyev told Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians to 'obey the laws of Azerbaijan [and] be a loyal and normal citizen of Azerbaijan'. He threatened that if the territory’s separatist institutions were not dissolved, Azerbaijan would dissolve them by force and rejected the prospect of international protections for ethnic Armenians.}} In 2023, Azerbaijani President Aliyev said that Armenian residents of Nagorno-Karabakh must be "reintegrated" as "normal citizen[s] of Azerbaijan" and that "the [special] status [for Armenians] went to hell. It failed; it was shattered to smithereens. It is not and will not be there. As long as I am president, there will be no status."{{cite news |last1=Isayev |first1=Heydar |last2=Shahverdyan |first2=Lilit |date=4 August 2023 |title=More false starts in Azerbaijan-Karabakh talks |work=Eurasianet |url=https://eurasianet.org/more-false-starts-in-azerbaijan-karabakh-talks}} Aliyev also threatened military action.{{cite news |date=30 May 2023 |title=Azerbaijani president gives ultimatum to Karabakh authorities |work=Eurasianet |url=https://eurasianet.org/azerbaijani-president-gives-ultimatum-to-karabakh-authorities |url-status=live |access-date=5 July 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230629140145/https://eurasianet.org/azerbaijani-president-gives-ultimatum-to-karabakh-authorities |archive-date=29 June 2023 |quote=The president of Azerbaijan has delivered an ultimatum to de facto authorities in Nagorno Karabakh: disband your government or prepare for the consequences. He suggested Baku could easily end Armenian administration of the region through military action. |ref=test}}{{cite web |last1=Aghayev |first1=Ismi |last2=Avetisyan |first2=Ani |date=7 March 2023 |title=Nagorno-Karabakh claims Azerbaijan offered 'integration' or 'tougher measures' |url=https://oc-media.org/nagorno-karabakh-claims-azerbaijan-offered-integration-or-tougher-measures/ |access-date=7 March 2023 |website=OC Media |language=en-US}} Despite being offered Azerbaijani citizenship, Artsakh residents did not trust Azerbaijan's guarantees of security due to the [[Human rights in Azerbaijan|country's history of human rights abuses]], [[Anti-Armenian sentiment in Azerbaijan|Armenophobia]] and lack of rights to ethnic minorities.{{Cite web |title=Nagorno-Karabakh blockade: Azerbaijani journalist speaks out |url=https://www.opendemocracy.net/en/odr/nagorno-karabakh-blockade-azerbaijan-armenia-citizenship/ |access-date=26 January 2023 |website=openDemocracy |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=Kennan Cable No. 81: What's at Stake in Nagorno-Karabakh: U.S. Interests and the Risk of Ethnic Cleansing |url=https://www.wilsoncenter.org/publication/kennan-cable-no-81-whats-stake-nagorno-karabakh-us-interests-and-risk-ethnic-cleansing |access-date=4 April 2023 |website=www.wilsoncenter.org |date=4 April 2023 |language=en |quote=The history of violence against Armenians in Azerbaijan, including the recent execution of Armenian prisoners of war and the sexual mutilation of female soldiers, give Armenians in Nagorno-Karabakh little confidence in their safety...According to Freedom House, Azerbaijan's government "has worked to stifle public expressions of Talysh and Lezgin identity, among other targeted groups."}}{{Cite web |last=Vock |first=Ido |date=8 June 2023 |title=Fear and loathing in Armenia |url=https://www.newstatesman.com/world/europe/2023/06/fear-loathing-in-armenia-azerbaijan |access-date=9 June 2023 |website=New Statesman |language=en-US |quote=Azerbaijan says the territory’s inhabitants can live as Azerbaijani citizens under its sovereignty, but Armenians treat these claims with derision. 'Azerbaijani dissidents say even Azerbaijanis do not enjoy their constitutional rights,' Tigran Grigoryan, head of the Regional Centre for Democracy and Security, an Armenian think tank, told me. 'So how can Armenians expect to?'}} Various human rights observers, scholars specializing in genocide studies, and politicians considered the blockade of Artsakh a form of ethnic cleansing and warned of the risk of genocide. Many international observers also did not consider Azerbaijan's claim that Artsakh Armenians could live safely under Aliyev's regime to be credible.{{Cite web |date=31 July 2023 |title=Karabakh blockade reaches critical point as food supplies run low |url=https://www.intellinews.com/karabakh-blockade-reaches-critical-point-as-food-supplies-run-low-286173/ |access-date=1 August 2023 |website=www.intellinews.com |language=en}}{{cite news |last=Boy |first=Ann-Dorit |date=18 January 2023 |title=Blockade in the Southern Caucasus: "There Is Every Reason to Expect More Violence This Year" |language=en |work=Der Spiegel |url=https://www.spiegel.de/international/world/blockade-in-the-southern-caucasus-there-is-every-reason-to-expect-more-violence-this-year-a-639a972e-cc4e-477d-99f2-766beb2fcbea |access-date=19 January 2023}}{{Cite web |date=31 July 2023 |title=In Other News: Reaching the Breaking Point |url=https://www.civilnet.am/en/news/746065/in-other-news-reaching-the-breaking-point/ |access-date=31 July 2023 |website=CIVILNET |language=en-US}}{{Cite web |last=Hauer |first=Neil |date=31 July 2023 |title=Karabakh blockade reaches critical point as food supplies run low |url=https://www.intellinews.com/karabakh-blockade-reaches-critical-point-as-food-supplies-run-low-286173/ |access-date=1 August 2023 |website=www.intellinews.com |language=en |quote=Khachatryan’s detention confirms the fears of many Karabakh Armenians that, if Azerbaijan assumes control over Karabakh, it will detain (and torture) them arbitrarily, using their participation in one or more of the wars as justification. These criteria extend to nearly every male resident of the small enclave. “Arrests with linkages to the past wars, local army or the [Karabakh] government …would quality almost all local men for detentions,” wrote Olesya Vartanyan, International Crisis Group’s senior analyst for the South Caucasus. The detainees can expect torture or worse, as the Armenian prisoners of war following the 2020 war conflict experienced.}} Political analyst Eric Hacopian has said that "Expecting Armenians to submit to the rule of a nationalistic and undemocratic Azeri government] is the equivalent of asking 100,000 Israelis to live under Hamas."{{Cite news |title=It's Bad to Be a Sheep |work=Tablet Magazine |url=https://www.tabletmag.com/sections/israel-middle-east/articles/armenia-and-israel-armin-rosen}} Caucasus expert Laurence Broers wrote "the blockade [of Nagorno-Karabakh] renders irrelevant any talk of the civil integration of Karabakh Armenians. It vindicates the worst fears of the Karabakh Armenian population." Political analysts predict that Azerbaijan would arbitrarily detain and torture civilians, under the pretext of their association with the Artsakh government or with previous wars, if it took control over the region. At least two incidents of Azerbaijani forces detaining Armenian residents around Azerbaijan's military checkpoint have been confirmed.{{Cite news |last1=Shahverdyan |first1=Lilit |last2=Isayev |first2=Heydar |date=31 July 2023 |title=Azerbaijan arrests Nagorno-Karabakh resident during medical evacuation for "war crime" |work=Eurasianet |url=https://eurasianet.org/azerbaijan-arrests-nagorno-karabakh-resident-during-medical-evacuation-for-war-crime}}{{Cite web |date=30 July 2023 |title=Azerbaijan arrests Nagorno-Karabakh resident for 'war crimes' |url=https://oc-media.org/azerbaijan-arrests-nagorno-karabakh-resident-for-war-crimes/ |access-date=1 August 2023 |website=OC Media |language=en-US}} ==See also== * [[Armenia–Azerbaijan border]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|refs= {{cite journal |last1=Dinu |first1=Mihai-Ștefan |title=The ethnical-religious context of the armed conflicts from the Caucasus between 1990 and 2002 |journal=Strategic Impact |date=2010 |issue=4 |page=35 |url=http://cssas.unap.ro/en/pdf_periodicals/si37.swf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130921055051/http://cssas.unap.ro/en/pdf_periodicals/si37.swf |url-status=dead |archive-date=21 September 2013 |publisher=[[Carol I National Defence University|Romanian National Defence University "Carol I"]] Centre for Defence and Security Strategic Studies |location=Bucharest |quote=However, Russia was not the only ally of Armenia, but also Greece and Iran, both with a long history of tense relations with Turkey. Greece supported Armenia both by delivering military and economic assistance and diplomatic representation by promoting Armenia's interests in the EU and NATO. Iran provided trade opportunities and an opening to the maritime space.}} {{cite book |last1=Panossian |first1=Razmik |author-link1=Razmik Panossian |editor1-last=Hughes |editor1-first=James |editor2-last=Sasse |editor2-first=Gwendolyn |editor1-link=Jim Hughes (academic) |editor2-link=Gwendolyn Sasse |title=Ethnicity and Territory in the Former Soviet Union: Regions in Conflict |date=2002 |publisher=[[Routledge]] |isbn=978-1136342042 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=QBWgBAAAQBAJ&dq=soviet+sided+azerbaijan+karabakh&pg=PA145 145] |chapter=The Irony of Nagorno-Karabakh: Formal Institutions versus Informal Politics}} {{cite book |last1=Matveeva |first1=Anna |title=The South Caucasus: Nationalism, Conflict and Minorities |url=https://www.refworld.org/pdfid/469cbfd90.pdf |publisher=[[Minority Rights Group International]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200823144616/https://www.refworld.org/pdfid/469cbfd90.pdf |archive-date=23 August 2020 |page=11 |date=2002|isbn= 189769344-3}} {{harvnb|Chorbajian|Mutafian|Donabedian|1994|pp=13–18}} Unless otherwise stated, the statistics cited by the authors is from data compiled by the [[International Institute for Strategic Studies]] in its annual ''The Military Balance'', published in 1993. Reference to these statistics can be found on pages 68–69 and 71–73 of the report. {{cite web |title=SIPRI Yearbook 1994 |url=https://www.sipri.org/sites/default/files/SIPRI%20Yearbook%201994.pdf |website=sipri.org |publisher=[[Stockholm International Peace Research Institute]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200826153836/https://www.sipri.org/yearbook/1994 |archive-date=26 August 2020 |date=1994 |page=88}} Table of conflict locations with at least one major armed conflict in 1993 }} ==Bibliography== * {{Cite book |last=Četerjan |first=Viken |author-link=Vicken Cheterian |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BYdNEAAAQBAJ |title=War and peace in the Caucasus: Russia's troubled frontier |publisher=[[Hurst Publishers|Hurst]] |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-85065-929-7 |edition=1. |location=London}} * {{cite book|last1=Chorbajian|first1=Levon|last2=Mutafian|first2=Claude|last3=Donabedian|first3=Patrick|author-link1=Levon Chorbajian|author-link2=Claude Mutafian|title=The Caucasian Knot: The History and Geopolitics of Nagorno-Karabagh|date=1994|location=Zed Books|isbn=978-1-85649-288-1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OUlnYdOHJ3wC}} * {{Cite book |last=De Waal |first=Thomas |author-link=Thomas de Waal |title=Black garden: Armenia and Azerbaijan through peace and war |title-link=Black Garden |publisher=[[New York University Press]] |year=2003 |isbn=978-0-8147-1945-9 |edition=1st |location=New York}} * Heß, Michael Reinhard (2016). ''Panzer im Paradies. Der Berg-Karabach-Konflikt zwischen Armenien und Aserbaidschan'' [Tanks in paradise. The Nagorno-Karabakh conflict between Armenia and Azerbaijan]. Berlin: Dr. Köster, {{ISBN|978-3-89574-906-3}}. * {{Cite book |url=https://www.hrw.org/sites/default/files/reports/AZER%20Conflict%20in%20N-K%20Dec94.pdf |title=Azerbaijan: Seven years of conflict in Nagorno-Karabakh |publisher=[[Human Rights Watch]] |year=1994 |isbn=978-1-56432-142-8 |ref={{harvid|HRW|1994}} |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200628191526/https://www.hrw.org/sites/default/files/reports/AZER%20Conflict%20in%20N-K%20Dec94.pdf |archive-date=28 June 2020 |url-status=live}} * {{Cite book |last=Saparov |first=Arsène |title=From Conflict to Autonomy in the Caucasus: The Soviet Union and the Making of Abkhazia, South Ossetia and Nagorno Karabakh |date=2014 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-75899-2 |language=en |doi=10.4324/9781315758992 }} * {{Cite journal |last=Taarnby |first=Michael |date=2008 |title=The Mujahedin in Nagorno-Karabakh: A Case Study in the Evolution of Global Jihad |url=https://www.realinstitutoelcano.org/en/work-document/the-mujahedin-in-nagorno-karabakh-a-case-study-in-the-evolution-of-global-jihad-wp/ |url-status=live |journal=Working Paper |location=Madrid |publisher=[[Elcano Royal Institute]] |pages=1–12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200819082551/http://www.realinstitutoelcano.org/wps/wcm/connect/a2aa12004f018b88b8d1fc3170baead1/WP20-2008_Taarnby_Mujahedin_Nagorno-Karabakh_Global_Jihad.pdf?MOD=AJPERES&CACHEID=a2aa12004f018b88b8d1fc3170baead1 |archive-date=19 August 2020}} * {{Cite journal |last=Cornell |first=Svante E. |author-link=Svante Cornell |date=1998 |title=Turkey and the conflict in Nagorno Karabakh: a delicate balance |journal=Middle Eastern Studies |language=en |volume=34 |issue=1 |pages=51–72 |doi=10.1080/00263209808701209 |issn=0026-3206 |jstor=4283917}} {{Nagorno-Karabakh Conflict}} {{Post-Cold War Asian
