4 | [[2020 Toyota 200|DAR]] | [[2020 Alsco 300 (Charlotte)|CLT]] | [[2020 Cheddar's 300|BRI]] | [[2020 EchoPark 250|ATL]] | [[2020 Hooters 250|HOM]] | [[2020 Contender Boats 250|HOM]] | [[2020 Unhinged 300|TAL]] | [[2020 Pocono Green 225|POC]] | [[2020 Pennzoil 150|IRC]] | [[2020 Shady Rays 200|KEN]] | [[2020 Alsco 300 (Kentucky)|KEN]] | [[2020 My Bariatric Solutions 300|TEX]] | [[2020 Kansas Lottery 250|KAN]] | [[2020 Henry 180|ROA]] | [[2020 UNOH 188|DRC]] | [[2020 Drydene 200 (Saturday)|DOV]] | [[2020 Drydene 200 (Sunday)|DOV]] | [[2020 Wawa 250|DAY]] | [[2020 Sport Clips Haircuts VFW 200|DAR]] | [[2020 Go Bowling 250|RCH]] | [[2020 Virginia is for Racing Lovers 250|RCH]] | [[2020 Food City 300|BRI]] | [[2020 Alsco 300 (Las Vegas)|LVS]] | [[2020 Ag-Pro 300|TAL]] | [[2020 Drive for the Cure 250|ROV]] | [[2020 Kansas Lottery 300|KAN]] | [[2020 O'Reilly Auto Parts 300|TEX]] | [[2020 Draft Top 250|MAR]] | [[2020 Desert Diamond Casino West Valley 200|PHO]] | colspan=2| ! ! |- |} ===Car No. 22 history=== ;Part-time (2009) [[File:Brad Keselowski NAPA 200.jpg|thumb|left|[[Brad Keselowski]] won his first Nationwide Championship in 2010.]] In 2009, Penske developmental driver [[Parker Kligerman]] made his debut at [[Kansas Speedway]], winning the pole, leading seven laps, and finishing a respectable sixteenth. Kligerman also attempted the season finale at Homestead, but failed to qualify, running the No. 42 car instead. ;Brad Keselowski (2010–2011) For 2010, Penske Racing ran two full-time Nationwide series cars with [[Discount Tire]] and [[Ruby Tuesday (restaurant)|Ruby Tuesday]] coming on board to sponsor [[Brad Keselowski]] in the No. 22. They continued to use Dodge engines, despite Dodge cutting their Nationwide support. On November 6, 2010, Brad Keselowski and the No. 22 Discount Tire/[[Ruby Tuesday (restaurant)|Ruby Tuesday]] Nationwide team secured the NASCAR Nationwide driver championship by finishing 3rd at Texas Motor Speedway. By holding an insurmountable 465-point lead over Carl Edwards with two races left in the season, the No. 22 team delivered Roger Penske's first NASCAR title of any kind. ;Multiple Drivers (2011–2018) For the [[2011 NASCAR Nationwide Series|2011 season]], Penske continued to run the No. 22 full-time with Brad Keselowski. In August, Keselowski suffered a hard crash while testing at [[Road Atlanta]]. He was replaced in the No. 22 by Hornish Jr., [[Kurt Busch]], and [[Parker Kligerman]]. [[Formula 1]] Champion [[Jacques Villeneuve]] drove the No. 22 at the road courses. The No. 22 team scored five wins with Keselowski and another with Busch at Watkins Glen. In 2012, Keselowski was scheduled to split the 22 ride, with [[Parker Kligerman]] running between five and seven races. However, after only running three races with the team, Kligerman was replaced in both the Nationwide Series and his [[NASCAR Camping World Truck Series|Truck Series]] ride at [[Brad Keselowski Racing|BKR]] with fellow up-and-coming driver [[Ryan Blaney]], who ran the standalone oval races.{{cite web|last1=Moody|first1=Dave|title=Blaney Replaces Kligerman in Penske Nationwide Entry|url=http://motorsports-soapbox.blogspot.com/2012/07/blaney-replaces-kligerman-in-penske.html|website=godfathermotorsports.com|access-date=September 20, 2014|date=July 17, 2012}} Villeneuve was named to drive at [[Road America]] and Montreal for the team.{{cite web|author1=Penske Racing|title=Penske Racing names Villeneuve for Road America and Montreal events|url=http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/penske-racing-names-villeneuve-for-road-america-and-montreal-events/?v=2&s=1&q=Parker+Kligerman+2012|archive-url=https://archive.today/20140920011633/http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/penske-racing-names-villeneuve-for-road-america-and-montreal-events/?v=2&s=1&q=Parker+Kligerman+2012|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 20, 2014|website=motorsport.com|access-date=September 20, 2014|location=[[Mooresville, North Carolina]]|date=May 17, 2012}} [[File:Road America 2013 Nationwide 22 AJ Allmendinger.jpg|thumb|right|The No. 22 won the 2013 owner's championship. Pictured is [[A. J. Allmendinger]]'s winning car at [[Road America]].]] In 2013, [[Brad Keselowski]] and [[Ryan Blaney]] were scheduled to share the No. 22, joined by new Penske driver [[Joey Logano]]. In June, former Penske Cup driver [[A. J. Allmendinger]] signed on to run two races in the 22, at the road courses [[Road America]] and [[Mid-Ohio Sports Car Course|Mid-Ohio]].{{cite web|author1=Penske Racing|title=AJ Allmendinger to race in NNS events at Road America and Mid-Ohio for Penske Racing|url=http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/aj-allmendinger-to-race-in-nns-events-at-road-america-and-mid-ohio-for-penske-racing/?v=2&s=1&q=A.J.+Allmendinger|website=motorsport.com|access-date=September 20, 2014|location=[[Mooresville, North Carolina]]|date=June 1, 2013|archive-date=December 20, 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141220143709/http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/aj-allmendinger-to-race-in-nns-events-at-road-america-and-mid-ohio-for-penske-racing/?v=2&s=1&q=A.J.+Allmendinger|url-status=dead}} Allmendinger won the pole at Road America,{{cite web|author1=Staff Report|title=ALLMENDINGER WINS COORS LIGHT POLE AT ROAD AMERICA|url=http://www.nascar.com/en_us/news-media/articles/2013/06/22/nascar-nationwide-qualifying-results-coors-light-pole.html|website=[[NASCAR]].com|publisher=[[NASCAR]]|access-date=September 20, 2014|location=[[Elkhart Lake, Wisconsin]]|date=June 22, 2013}} then proceeded to win the race, his first career Nationwide win, after leading 29 laps.{{cite web|author1=Staff Report|title=ALLMENDINGER WINS AT ROAD AMERICA|url=http://www.nascar.com/en_us/news-media/articles/2013/06/22/nascar-nationwide-results-road-america-johnsonville-sausage-200.html|website=[[NASCAR]].com|publisher=[[NASCAR]]|access-date=September 20, 2014|location=[[Elkhart Lake, Wisconsin]]|date=June 22, 2013}} Allmendinger then won at Mid-Ohio after starting second and leading 73 of 94 laps.{{cite web|last1=Vincent|first1=Amanda|title=Allmendinger dominates Mid-Ohio to earn the victory|url=http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/allmendinger-dominates-mid-ohio-to-earn-the-victory/?v=2&s=1&q=allmendinger+2013|website=motorsport.com|access-date=September 20, 2014|date=August 18, 2013|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141220141613/http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/allmendinger-dominates-mid-ohio-to-earn-the-victory/?v=2&s=1&q=allmendinger+2013|archive-date=December 20, 2014}} Ryan Blaney then won his first career race at [[Kentucky Speedway]], after leading 96 of the final 100 laps of the race.{{cite web|last1=Vincent|first1=Amanda|title=Ryan Blaney scores his first NNS win at Kentucky|url=http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/ryan-blaney-scores-his-first-nns-win-at-kentucky/?v=2&s=1&q=A.J.+Allmendinger|website=motorsport.com|access-date=September 20, 2014|date=September 22, 2013|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141220143918/http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/ryan-blaney-scores-his-first-nns-win-at-kentucky/?v=2&s=1&q=A.J.+Allmendinger|archive-date=December 20, 2014}} The team won the Nationwide Owners' Championship on the strength of twelve total race victories among the four drivers. This was the first Nationwide Owners title for Team Penske. In 2014, the No. 22 car was shared by [[Joey Logano]], [[Brad Keselowski]], [[Ryan Blaney]], and [[Alex Tagliani]] in hopes of defending the Nationwide Owners' Championship. [[Michael McDowell (racing driver)|Michael McDowell]] ran the car at Kentucky in September, the fifth driver to run the car in 2014.{{cite web|author1=[[NASCAR]] Wire Service|title=Familiar faces Hornish and McDowell return to action with owner points title implications|url=http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/familiar-faces-hornish-and-mcdowell-return-to-action-with-owner-points-title-implications/?v=2&s=1&q=michael+mcdowell|website=motorsport.com|access-date=September 20, 2014|date=September 18, 2014}} The No. 22 team beat the No. 54 JGR team once again for the owner's title. They again beat the No. 54 team for the owner's title in 2015, before going winless in 2016. In 2017 Brad Keselowski brought the 22 back to victory lane at Pocono after a last-lap pass on [[Kyle Larson]]. Keselowski won another race at the fall Richmond race and [[Ryan Blaney]] won the fall Dover race. The 22 won the owners championship again at Homestead with [[Sam Hornish Jr.]] driving the car to a second-place finish. The No. 22 team is known for its competition for the Nationwide Owner's Championship with the equally strong [[Joe Gibbs Racing]] and their No. 18 and No. 20 teams. [[File:Brad Keselowski New Hampshire Xfinity 2018.jpg|thumb|[[Brad Keselowski]]'s [[2018 NASCAR Xfinity Series|2018 Xfinity]] [[Ford Mustang (sixth generation)|Mustang]] at [[New Hampshire Motor Speedway]].]] ;Austin Cindric (2019–2021) [[File:22 Austin Cindric 2017 Johnsonville 180.jpg|thumb|left|Cindric's No. 22 Xfinity Series car at [[Road America]]]] For the 2019 season, [[Austin Cindric]] was announced as the full-time driver of the No. 22 with sponsorship from MoneyLion. On July 11, 2019, crew chief Brian Wilson was suspended for one race after the car scheduled to race at [[2019 Alsco 300 (Kentucky)|Kentucky]] was discovered to have an illegal body modification. The L1-level penalty also resulted in a deduction of ten points for the team and Cindric, and a $10,000 fine.{{cite web |url=https://www.nascar.com/news-media/2019/07/11/xfinity-series-penalty-austin-cindric-team-penske-kentucky/ |title=No. 22 Xfinity Series team issued L1 penalty; crew chief escorted from garage |website=NASCAR.com |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC. |date=July 11, 2019 |access-date=July 12, 2019}} Cindric scored two wins and fourteen top-fives, but had a poor race at Kansas and could not advance to the Championship 4. In 2020, Cindric became the ninth different driver to win three consecutive races in series history by winning back-to-back races at Kentucky as well as Texas where he had initially finished in second place until race-winner [[Kyle Busch]] was disqualified for failing post-race inspection. Cindric went on to make the ''Championship 4'' and win his first Xfinity Championship while at the same time winning the race at [[Phoenix Raceway]] in Overtime.{{cite web|url=https://nascar.nbcsports.com/2020/11/07/austin-cindric-wins-xfinity-championship/|title=Austin Cindric charges to Xfinity championship in overtime|website=nascar.nbcsports.com|publisher=NBC Sports|date=November 7, 2020|access-date=November 8, 2020}} Cindric would go on to win five races in 2021 and finished runner-up to [[Daniel Hemric]] in the season finale and final standings before taking over the famous No. 2 in the [[2022 NASCAR Cup Series]], replacing [[Brad Keselowski]] as he went to [[RFK Racing]]. ;Hiatus On January 13, 2022, Team Penske shut down its Xfinity program to focus on its Cup Series efforts and also the NASCAR Xfinity Series program was diverted to [[International Motor Sports Association|IMSA]] [[WeatherTech SportsCar Championship]] and [[FIA]] [[FIA World Endurance Championship|World Endurance Championship]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jayski.com/2022/01/13/team-penske-has-no-plans-to-field-xfinity-series-car-in-2022/ |title=Team Penske Has No Plans to Field Xfinity Series Car in 2022 |website=[[Jayski's Silly Season Site]] |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |date=January 13, 2022 |access-date=January 14, 2022}} {{clear}} ====Car No. 22 results==== {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center; font-size:75%" |- ! 2* | colspan=2| ! 1st ! 4035 |- |} ===Car No. 26 history=== [[File:Parker Kligerman NAPA 200 - 2.jpg|thumb|[[Parker Kligerman]] in 2010]] In an alliance with [[Brian Keselowski Motorsports|K-Automotive Racing]] (owned by [[Brad Keselowski]]'s brother [[Brian Keselowski|Brian]]), Penske fielded the 26 car in select races in 2010, primarily [[Car of Tomorrow]] races. Nineteen-year-old [[Parker Kligerman]] debuted in the car at [[Daytona International Speedway|Daytona]] with [[Discount Tire]], starting on the outside pole and finishing thirteenth.{{cite web|author1=Penske Racing|title=Daytona II: Parker Kligerman race report|url=http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/daytona-ii-parker-kligerman-race-report/?v=2&s=1&q=Parker+Kligerman+2010|archive-url=https://archive.today/20140920011157/http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/daytona-ii-parker-kligerman-race-report/?v=2&s=1&q=Parker+Kligerman+2010|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 20, 2014|website=motorsport.com|access-date=September 19, 2014|location=[[Daytona Beach, Florida]]|date=July 3, 2010}} His next race was [[Circuit Gilles Villeneuve|Montreal]], where he scored a strong 8th-place finish. He then finished fifteenth at Richmond. At [[Charlotte Motor Speedway|Charlotte]] in October, Kligerman qualified eighth, but crashed after only three laps and finished last.{{cite web|author1=[[Dodge]] Motorsports|title=Charlotte II: Dodge teams race quotes|url=http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/charlotte-ii-dodge-teams-race-quotes-2010-10-17/?v=2&s=1&q=Parker+Kligerman+2010|archive-url=https://archive.today/20140920011201/http://www.motorsport.com/nascar-ns/news/charlotte-ii-dodge-teams-race-quotes-2010-10-17/?v=2&s=1&q=Parker+Kligerman+2010|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 20, 2014|website=motorsport.com|access-date=September 19, 2014|date=October 17, 2010}} [[Sam Hornish Jr.]] ran the season finale in the 26, finishing 21st. ====Car No. 26 results==== {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center; font-size:75%" |- ! 26 |- |} ===Car No. 39 history=== For 2005, [[Ryan Newman (racing driver)|Ryan Newman]] raced in nine races. In the last seven he competed in, he won the first five, came in sixteenth at Texas, then won the season finale at Homestead finishing thirty-fourth in the points standings. He also got a pair of third-place finishes before those six wins. In 2006, [[Kurt Busch]] and Newman drove a limited schedule in a [[Penske]] No. 39 for Busch, and an [[Alltel]] No. 39 for Newman. Though Newman got a highest finish of second at [[Auto Club]], Busch ended up notching two wins at Texas and Watkins Glen. In the other five races he competed in, he came in the top five in all but one, finishing twenty-first at Michigan before finishing thirty-ninth in points. IndyCar Champion [[Sam Hornish Jr.]] began racing the No. 39 in the last two races of the year, crashing out of both races. The only race for the 39 in 2007 was at [[Watkins Glen International]], where Kurt Busch started on the pole and finished third. ====Car No. 39 results==== {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center; font-size:75%" |- ! [[Joey Logano]] ran the car at [[Watkins Glen International|Watkins Glen]] with [[Discount Tire]] as the sponsor,{{cite web|last1=Gluck|first1=Jeff|title=Why will Joey Logano drive No. 48 car at Watkins Glen?|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/nascar/2013/07/31/joey-logano-penske-racing-watkins-glen-nationwide-series/2603657/|website=[[USA Today]]|access-date=December 20, 2014|date=July 31, 2013}} starting third and finishing 21st. [[Ryan Blaney]] then ran the car at Phoenix with [[AutoZone]], finishing tenth.{{cite web|title=Blaney Driving The No. 48 AutoZone Ford In Nationwide Series|url=http://ryanblaney.com/blaney-driving-the-no-48-autozone-ford-in-nationwide-series/#.VJVnwV4AAA|website=[[Ryan Blaney|ryanblaney.com]]|access-date=December 20, 2014|date=November 6, 2013|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141208055352/http://ryanblaney.com/blaney-driving-the-no-48-autozone-ford-in-nationwide-series/#.VJVnwV4AAA|archive-date=December 8, 2014}} [[Brad Keselowski]] ran the car at Homestead with Discount Tire, winning the race.{{cite web|last1=Spencer|first1=Reid|title=AUSTIN DILLON CLAIMS TITLE; KESELOWSKI TAKES WIN|url=http://www.nascar.com/en_us/news-media/articles/2013/11/16/nascar-nationwide-series-homestead-miami-speedway-winner-recap.html|website=[[NASCAR]].com|publisher=[[NASCAR]]|access-date=December 20, 2014|location=[[Homestead, Florida]]|date=November 16, 2013}} ====Car No. 48 results==== {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center; font-size:75%" |- ! 1 |- |} ==Truck Series== ===Truck No. 22 history=== Penske fielded the No. 22 truck in [[1996 NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series|1996]] in ten races, with [[Ricky Johnson]] driving three, [[Kenny Wallace]] six, and [[Rusty Wallace]] one. Johnson failed to qualify for another race at [[Colorado National Speedway]]. The team scored a best finish of fourth at [[North Wilkesboro Speedway]] with Kenny Wallace driving.{{Cite web|url=https://www.racing-reference.info/misc-stats/?id=penskro01&trk=t0&series=C&o=1?id=penskro01&trk=t0&series=C&o=1|title=Misc Stats}} ====Truck No. 22 results==== {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center; font-size:75%" |- ! {{Short description|Quarter of the Neukölln borough in Berlin, Germany}} {{About|the quarter of Berlin|the administrative borough|Neukölln|the constituency|Berlin-Neukölln (electoral district)|the railway and U-Bahn station|Berlin-Neukölln station|Neu-Cölln, the historical district of Berlin|Cölln|the David Bowie instrumental work|Neuköln|the neighborhood Neuköln in North Rhine-Westphalia|Oberhausen|the mine Neu-Cöln|Borbeck-Mitte}} {{redirect|Rixdorf|the manor in Schleswig-Holstein|Lebrade|the ship|Neukölln (locality)#Ferry}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2025}} {{very long|words=24,000|date=October 2025}}
{{Infobox German place | name = Neukölln | name_local = | image_photo = Berlin Johannes-Basilika asv2022-06 img2.jpg | image_caption = Hasenheide on the western outskirts of Neukölln with St. John's Basilica | type = Quarter | City = Berlin | image_flag = Flagge Bezirk Neukölln.svg | image_coa = Coat_of_arms_of_borough_Neukoelln.svg | coordinates = {{coord|52|28|39.3|N|13|26|31.5|E|format=dms|display=inline,title}} | state = Berlin | district = | borough = Neukölln | divisions = [[#Neighborhoods|9 neighborhoods]] or [[#Urban planning|5 regions]] with 21 planning areas | Bürgermeistertitel = Borough mayor | mayor = Martin Hikel | party = SPD | elevation = 43.6 | area = 11.7 |pop_ref =<ref>{{Population Germany|key=11|datref=QUELLE}}</ref> |population = {{Population Germany|key=110801}} |population_as_of = {{Population Germany|key=11|datref=STAND}} | postal_code = 12043, 12045, 12047, 12049, 12051, 12053, 12055, 12057, 12059 | area_code = | licence = B | year = 26 June 1360 (official), 1190–1210 (unofficial) | plantext = Location of Neukölln in Neukölln borough and Berlin | image_plan = Berlin Neukoelln Neukoelln.png | website = https://www.berlin.de/ba-neukoelln/ }}
'''Neukölln''' ({{IPA|de|nɔʏˈkœln|lang|De-Neukölln.ogg}}), formerly '''Rixdorf''' ({{IPA|de|rˈɪksdɔɾf|lang}}), from 1899 to 1920 an independent city, is a large inner-city quarter of [[Berlin]] in the homonymous [[Boroughs and quarters of Berlin|borough]] of [[Neukölln]], and evolved around the historic village of Rixdorf. With 162,548 inhabitants (2025) the quarter has the second-largest population of Berlin after [[Prenzlauer Berg]]. Since the early 13th century, the local settlements, villages and cities down to the present day have always been a popular destination for colonists and immigrants. In modern times, it was originally shaped by the working class and ''[[gastarbeiter]]s'', but western immigration since the turn of the millennium has led to [[gentrification]] and a rejuvenation of the quarter's culture and nightlife.
== Geography == [[File:Hasenheide rixdorfer-hoehe 2021-06-06.png|thumb|left|Rixdorfer Höhe, Hasenheide]]
Neukölln is on the [[North European Plain]], which is typically characterized by low-lying marshy woodlands with a [[Geography of Berlin#Topography|mainly flat topography]]. The quarter lies on the geological border between the shallow [[Weichselian glaciation|Weichselian]] [[:de:Warschau-Berliner Urstromtal|Warsaw-Berlin]] ''[[Urstromtal]]'' glacial valley and the northernmost edge of the [[Teltow (region)|Teltow]] [[Old and Young Drift|young drift]] [[Moraine#Ground moraines|ground moraine]] plateau, specifically the ''Rollberge'',<ref group="note">Lit. ''rollende Berge'', i.e. ''sanft geschwungene Berge'' ("rolling hills") in the figurative meaning of ''rollen'' as ''fließen'' ("to flow") or ''wogen'' ("to wave"); one of Neukölln's neighborhoods was named after the ''Rollberge'' range (''see below''). ''See also'' the ''[[Kreuzberg (Tempelhofer Berge)|Kreuzberg]]'', Berlin's most prominent glacial (and highest natural inner-city) hill which, together with the ''Rollberge'' and the slopes near the former ''Mühlenberg'' in [[Schöneberg]], forms the larger northern Teltow range called ''Tempelhofer Berge''.</ref> a small range of glacial hills rising to the south of [[Hermannplatz (Berlin U-Bahn)|Hermannplatz]] and the street [[:de:Hasenheide (Straße)|Hasenheide]], and to the west of Rixdorf and the street [[:de:Karl-Marx-Straße (Berlin)|Karl-Marx-Straße]]. Neukölln's average natural elevation is {{convert|43.6|m|ft|abbr=on}} above [[Normalhöhennull|NHN]], ranging from {{convert|32.3|m|ft|abbr=on}} to {{convert|54.8|m|ft|abbr=on}},<ref group="note">Neukölln's highest point is on the Teltow in the central Schillerkiez approximately at Schillerpromenade 27/28; the quarter's lowest point is in the glacial valley near the eastern border to [[Plänterwald]] along the Heidekampgraben near Kiefholzstraße (Köllnische Heide); cf. i.a. [https://gdi.berlin.de/viewer/main/# "Geoportal Berlin"] s.v. "Karten → Fachkarten → INSPIRE-transformiert → II 01 Höhe → Höhenlage im INSPIRE-Datenmodell (Digitales Geländemodell)", ''Senatsverwaltung für Stadtentwicklung, Bauen und Wohnen'', Berlin.</ref> with the highest man-made elevation at {{convert|67.9|m|ft|abbr=on}} achieved by the ''[[:de:Rixdorfer Höhe|Rixdorfer Höhe]]'', a ''[[schuttberg]]'' in the [[Volkspark Hasenheide]].<ref group="note">The ''Rixdorfer Höhe'' has the highest elevation in the southern part of Berlin's inner city within the ''[[Berlin Ringbahn|Ringbahn]]'', surpassed only by a few ''schuttberg'' deposits in the north near the [[Barnim Plateau|Barnim]] like the {{convert|86.5|m|ft|abbr=on}} ''[[:de:Humboldthöhe|Humboldthöhe]]'' in the [[:de:Volkspark Humboldthain|Volkspark Humboldthain]] ([[Gesundbrunnen (Berlin)|Gesundbrunnen]]); ''see also'' [[:de:Liste von Erhebungen in Berlin|Liste von Erhebungen in Berlin]]. Neukölln's tallest building structure, on the other hand, is the [[Estrel Tower]] at {{convert|176|m|ft|abbr=on}}, which is Berlin's tallest skyscraper and Germany's tallest hotel tower; ''see also'' [[List of tallest buildings in Berlin]].</ref> Neukölln's [[Geographical centre|geographical center]], based on a [[Minimum bounding box|minimum bounding plane]], is located east of Richardstraße 101 near Kirchgasse at a linear distance of approximately {{convert|2.3|km|mi|abbr=on}} to the river [[Spree (river)|Spree]] with an elevation of {{convert|34.8|m|ft|abbr=on}}.
[[File:Kottbusserstr Landwehrkanal E.jpg|thumb|left|Landwehr Canal with Neukölln and the quayside bar ''Ankerklause'' on the right]]
=== Location === Neukölln forms the lower east side of Berlin's city center within the [[Berlin Ringbahn|Ringbahn]], and is the northernmost quarter in the homonymous borough of [[Neukölln]], with the borough stretching all the way to Berlin's southern border with [[Brandenburg]]. The quarter, administratively abbreviated '''Neukö''', is officially an ''Ortsteil'', and the borough a ''Verwaltungsbezirk'' (administrative district), in Berlin designated ''Bezirk'' (district).<ref group="note">The use of the shortened term ''Bezirk'' has legal reasons, because Berlin's boroughs are only ''partially'' administrative districts: Berlin is a single municipality (''Einheitsgemeinde''), so its boroughs have limited power, acting only as agencies of Berlin's state and city governments, as laid out in the 1920 [[Greater Berlin Act]]; the boroughs are financially dependent on state donations, as they neither possess any taxation power nor own any property. (This is in contrast to municipalities and counties in other German states, which are territorial corporations, so-called ''Gebietskörperschaften'', with autonomous functions and property.) Furthermore, a borough's district office, while in charge of most administrative matters affecting its borough's residents, can be overruled by the [[Berlin Senate]], which is the agent of Berlin's ''Hauptverwaltung'' (central administration); ''see also'' [[Boroughs and quarters of Berlin#Administration and politics|Boroughs and quarters of Berlin § Administration and politics]].</ref> Different from the borough, the smaller ''quarter'' of Neukölln has no mayor or representative assembly of its own. To distinguish the quarter from the borough, the latter is sometimes informally called ''Groß-Neukölln'' ("Greater Neukölln"), while the quarter is also called ''Berlin-Neukölln''<ref group="note">Over the years there have been several petitions to reestablish the historical name ''Rixdorf''; Kai Ritzmann, [https://www.bz-berlin.de/berlin/neukoelln/aus-nord-neukoelln-soll-wieder-rixdorf-werden "Aus (Nord-)Neukölln soll wieder Rixdorf werden"], 1 July 2019, ''B.Z.''.</ref> or ''Nord-Neukölln''.<ref group="note">Other alternative toponyms for the quarter are ''Neukölln-Nord'', ''Nordneukölln'' and ''Neukölln 44''. The number 44 had been part of the quarter's [[:de:Postleitzahl (Deutschland)#Deutsche Bundespost|old postal code]] (1000 Berlin 44, ''Neukölln 1'') and is still in informal cultural use after the introduction of the new [[postal codes in Germany]] on 1 July 1993. (Neukölln's modern postal codes, which range from 12043 to 12059, therefore still include the local number 44; however, only the codes with odd terminating numbers are currently in use.) Aside from its use to distinguish the quarter from the borough, the old postal code 44 has i.a. become part of local music and youth culture, adopted e.g. by clubs like the venue ''Hole<sup>44</sup>'', and by native [[hip hop music]]ians such as [[:de:Nashi44|Nashi44]], Doni44, 44Grad or [[:de:Kalazh44|Kalazh44]].</ref>
In the south, the quarter Neukölln is adjacent to the quarter [[Britz]], which is also part of greater Neukölln, and to [[:de:Berlin-Späthsfelde|Späthsfelde]] in the quarter [[Baumschulenweg]] east of Britz. In the north and north-west, it borders on the quarter [[Kreuzberg]] in the [[Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg]] borough, namely the southern part of the historical [[Luisenstadt]] district in the [[:de:Berlin SO 36|SO 36]] neighborhood, and the Graefekiez and [[:de:Bergmannkiez|Bergmannkiez]] in the [[:de:Kreuzberg 61|Kreuzberg 61]] neighborhood, which were once part of the historical [[:de:Tempelhofer Vorstadt|Tempelhofer Vorstadt]] district. Neukölln's northernmost tip is also adjacent to Kreuzberg's neighborhood [[:de:Wassertorplatz|Wassertorplatz]] in the southern part of the historical [[Friedrichstadt (Berlin)|Friedrichstadt]] district. In the west and south-west, Neukölln borders on the quarter [[Tempelhof]] in the [[Tempelhof-Schöneberg]] borough. In the north-east to south-east, Neukölln borders on the quarters [[Alt-Treptow]], [[Plänterwald]] and western [[Baumschulenweg]], which, together with Späthsfelde, are all part of the [[Treptow-Köpenick]] borough in former [[East Berlin]].
Neukölln is separated from Kreuzberg by the park [[Volkspark Hasenheide]], the [[Landwehr Canal]], and the streets [[:de:Kottbusser Damm|Kottbusser Damm]] and [[:de:Hasenheide (Straße)|Hasenheide]] as far as the city square [[:de:Südstern (Berlin)|Südstern]], which conforms to Berlin's historical ''[[Purlieu|Weichbildgrenze]]'' (1861–1919). With [[Tempelhof]], Neukölln shares the [[Tempelhofer Feld]], the vast field of the former [[Berlin Tempelhof Airport|Tempelhof Airport]], now a popular recreation area. The [[Britz Canal]], the green corridor ''Heidekamppark'' with the trench [[:de:Heidekampgraben|Heidekampgraben]], the Kiefholzstraße and several urban streets in the Harzer Kiez separate Neukölln from the quarters of Treptow-Köpenick in former [[East Berlin]]. Finally, the [[Bundesautobahn 100|Stadtring]] motorway with the Carl-Weder-Park, streets like Britzkestraße, Juliushof and Grenzallee, as well as the southern end of the [[Neukölln Ship Canal]], form the administrative border with the Britz quarter.
The [[Teltow Canal]], on the other hand, forms the geographical and demographic border within the borough Neukölln. The canal separates the dense urban areas of Neukölln and northern Britz with their higher share of immigrants and lower-income citizenry from the borough's southern residential areas, which, with the exception of [[Gropiusstadt]], are mainly characterized by a larger number of family homes and middle-class households.<ref group="note">The continuing growth of Neukölln's urbanized and gentrified sphere, however, is slowly expanding the northern demographics southward, for example into Britz' neighborhood [[Hufeisensiedlung]], or into the Buschkrugkiez, e.g. some city blocks north of Blaschkoallee like the Ideal-Siedlung.</ref>
=== Subdivisions === ==== Neighborhoods ==== [[File:Berlin-Neukölln Karte.png|thumb|left|Primary neighborhoods]] [[File:Berlin-Neukölln LORs 2025.png|thumb|left|Lifeworld-oriented regions]]
Neukölln has nine primary neighborhoods (''[[Kiez]]e''), administratively called ''Ortslagen'',<ref>Cf. i.a. [https://www.berlin.de/special/immobilien-und-wohnen/stadtteile/neukoelln/908298-5170841-neukoelln.html "Neukölln"], ''Berlin.de – Das offizielle Stadtportal'', 5 September 2023.</ref> among them the historical sites of Neukölln's foundation south-east of the quarters's geographical center, * '''Richardplatz-Süd'''<ref group="note">Also known as ''Richardkiez''; the original ''Richarsdorp'', later ''Ricksdorf'', ''Rixdorf'' and ''Deutsch-Rixdorf''; named after an unknown historical ''Richard'', probably in the 13th century, and possibly after a local knight or administrator, or after a prominent historical person like [[Richard of Chichester]], [[Richard of Cornwall]] or [[Richard de Bures]] (''see below''); in Berlin's LOR framework (''see below''), Richardplatz-Süd is part of both the planning areas Alt-Rixdorf and Braunschweiger Straße.</ref> to the south and south-east of the central plaza Richardplatz, and * '''[[:de:Böhmisch-Rixdorf|Böhmisch-Rixdorf]]'''<ref group="note">Also known as ''Böhmisches Dorf'' (Bohemian Village); among the Bohemian settlers, it was called ''Český Rixdorf'' ([[Czechs|Czech]] Rixdorf) until the early 20th century; in Berlin's LOR framework, Böhmisch-Rixdorf is part of the planning area Alt-Rixdorf.</ref> to the north and north-west, which together are commonly referred to as '''''Rixdorf''''' or ''Alt-Rixdorf'' ("Old Rixdorf").<ref group="note">In Berlin's LOR framework, the Rixdorf region is much larger and comprises many additional planning areas to the north, east and south of the historical village center Alt-Rixdorf.</ref> The other primary neighborhoods are (from north to south): * '''[[:de:Reuterkiez|Reuterkiez]]''',<ref group="note">Also known as ''Reuterquartier''; named after the central localities ''Reuterstraße'' and ''Reuterplatz'', themselves named after novelist [[Fritz Reuter]]; in Berlin's LOR framework, the Reuterkiez is part of a larger region called ''Reuterstraße'', which also includes the secondary neighborhoods ''Donaukiez'' and ''Harzer Straße''.</ref> * '''Flughafenkiez''',<ref group="note">Often called ''Flughafenstraße'', seldomly ''Flughafenstraßenkiez''; the name derives from the central street Flughafenstraße, which refers to the former Tempelhof airport nearby.</ref> * '''Schillerkiez''',<ref group="note">Also called ''Schillerpromenade''; named after the neighborhood's main street, the [[:de:Schillerpromenade|Schillerpromenade]], itself named after [[Friedrich Schiller]]; in Berlin's LOR framework, ''Schillerpromenade'' is the name of a much larger region that also includes three additional secondary neighborhoods to the north and south of the actual Schillerkiez.</ref> * '''[[:de:Rollbergsiedlung|Rollberg]]''',<ref group="note">Also called ''Rollbergkiez'', ''Rollbergviertel'' or ''Rollbergsiedlung''; named after the ''Rollberge'', a range of glacial hills (''see above''); not to be confused with the ''Rollberge'' estates, which is the alternative name for the [[:de:Schwarzwaldsiedlung|Schwarzwaldsiedlung]] in Berlin [[Reinickendorf (locality)|Reinickendorf]].</ref> * '''Weiße Siedlung''',<ref group="note">Lit. "white estates"; named after the white faces of most of the neighborhood's high-rise buildings; not to be confused with the [[World Heritage Site]] ''[[:de:Weiße Stadt (Berlin)|Weiße Stadt]]'' in Berlin Reinickendorf; the Weiße Siedlung is often lumped together with (and even called) the '''Dammwegsiedlung''', even though the latter is a distinct 1920s settlement further south (''see below''); in Berlin's LOR framework, the two neighborhoods are combined as the planning area ''Weiße Siedlung'', which additionally comprises the eastern Neukölln docklands, the Treptow freight yards, which, despite their name, are part of Neukölln (''see below''), and major recreational spaces at the eastern outskirts, mostly a large area of privately leased [[Allotment (gardening)|garden allotments]] at the site of the former Cölln Heath.</ref> * '''Körnerpark''',<ref group="note">Sometimes referred to as ''Körnerkiez''; named after the [[Körnerpark]], itself named after [[:de:Franz Körner|Franz Körner]], the former owner of the gravel quarry, in which the park was created; Körner gifted the quarry to the city of Rixdorf under the condition that the planned park would bear his name.</ref> and * '''[[:de:High-Deck-Siedlung|High-Deck-Siedlung]]'''.<ref group="note">Named after the architectural concept of using a close network of pedestrian [[skyway]]s between the estate buildings; also called ''Sängerviertel'' ("Singers' Quarter") due to several street names, e.g. the [[Michael Bohnen]] beltway.</ref>
==== Other sites ==== Several commonly recognized secondary neighborhoods exist in Neukölln. The most important and oldest central urban community is the '''Donaukiez''' along Donaustraße between [[Sonnenallee (Berlin)|Sonnenallee]] and Karl-Marx-Straße, including [[Hermannplatz]] at its western end.<ref group="note">South of the Reuterkiez and part of the LOR region ''Reuterstraße''; named after the river ''Donau'', the German name for the [[Danube]].</ref> The oldest suburban community is the historically important '''Dammwegsiedlung''' just south of the Weiße Siedlung, an early modern housing estate from the 1920s.<ref group="note">Also called ''Siedlung am Dammweg''; named after an old ''Dammweg'' (path on a dam) across the historical ''[[:de:Köllnische Heide|Cöllnische Heide]]'' (Cölln Heath) to the former landing stages at the river [[Spree (river)|Spree]]; administratively part of the planning area ''Weiße Siedlung'', itself part of the LOR region ''Köllnische Heide''.</ref> A recently evolved neighborship of cultural importance is the '''Weserkiez''' with its famous party mile around Weserstraße north of Sonnenallee.<ref group="note">Part of both Reuterkiez and Rixdorf, with the Wildenbruchstraße forming the official border; named after the river [[Weser]].</ref> Due to urban development and expansion, many other ''kiez'' communities have formed over the decades, sometimes distinguished in the city's official LOR framework (''see below'') or the focus of current or former [[:de:Quartiersmanagement|neighborhood management]],<ref group="note">Examples are: * '''Ganghoferstraße''' around the border between Donaukiez and Böhmisch-Rixdorf south of Sonnenallee; named after German author [[Ludwig Ganghofer]]; in Berlin's LOR framework, the Ganghoferstraße neighborhood belongs solely to the ''Rixdorf'' region and is a fairly large area stretching all the way to Thiemannstraße; * '''Warthekiez''' around Wartheplatz; part of Schillerpromenade, and including the cemetery park ''Neuer St. Jacobi Friedhof''; named after the river ''Warthe'', the German name for the [[Warta]]; * '''Harzer Straße''', a residential area north of the [[Neukölln Ship Canal]], also called ''Harzer Kiez'', '''Bouchéstraße''' or ''Bouchékiez''; nominally part of both Reuterkiez and Rixdorf, with the Wildenbruchstraße forming the official border, but also closely connected with the ''Kungerkiez'' in Alt-Treptow nearby; named after the local streets Harzer Straße and Bouchéstraße respectively, themselves named after the [[Harz|Harz region]] and German politician Johann Bouché (1759–1846); in Berlin's LOR framework, the Harzer Kiez is split into two areas, the small residential area ''Bouchéstraße'' in the west and ''Treptower Straße Nord'', a larger partially industrial area in the east; * areas south of the [[Berlin Hermannstraße station|Berlin Hermannstraße]] and [[Berlin Neukölln station]]s like '''Glasower Straße''', also called ''Glasower Kiez'' (south of Körnerpark and named after the Glasow neighborhood in the town of [[Blankenfelde-Mahlow|Mahlow]]), and '''Silbersteinstraße''', also called ''Silbersteinkiez'' (part of Schillerpromenade, and named after German politician [[:de:Raphael Silberstein|Raphael Silberstein]]); the Glasower Kiez also includes the westernmost parts of Neukölln's industrial park Lahnstraße; * '''Hasenheide''', a western secondary neighborhood north of the Schillerkiez with fewer residential infrastructure, but large recreational spaces and parks; it evolved from the old ''Hasenheide'' forest of Rixdorf, now a park, literally "hares' heath"; in Berlin's LOR framework, Hasenheide is part of the larger region ''Schillerpromenade''; * '''Schulenburgpark''' south of Dammwegsiedlung, a small neighborship close to High-Deck-Siedlung; named after the [[:de:Schulenburg (Adelsgeschlecht)|Schulenburg dynasty]]; in Berlin's LOR framework, ''Schulenburgpark'' is the name of a large planning area, which is part of ''Köllnische Heide'', Neukölln's largest region, and comprises the actual Schulenburgpark neighborship, the High-Deck-Siedlung, and many of the industrial sites southeast of the [[Berlin Ringbahn]].</ref> while an outlier is '''''Kreuzkölln''''', which is a meta-neighborhood and loosely defined cultural sphere, described by a modern jocular toponym,<ref group="note">[[Portmanteau]] of "''Kreuz''berg" and "Neu''kölln''"; the short hands ''X-Kölln'' and especially ''Xkölln'' are also in use; compare ''Xhain'', short for ''Kreuzhain'', a similar local portmanteau for the borough Friedrichs''hain''-''Kreuz''berg.</ref> which since the mid-2000s has often been used for the northern parts of the quarter and the surrounding regions of Kreuzberg.<ref group="note">[[File:Kreuzkölln Reuterkiez SO 36 Kreuzberg 61.png|thumb|right|Approximate extent of ''Kreuzkölln'' (red: minimum, red dotted: maximum, purple: Reuterkiez wedge)]] The toponym originally stood for the western part of the Reuterkiez, excluding the neighborhoods Donaukiez and Harzer Straße, and comprising the LOR areas Maybachufer, Reuterplatz and Weichselplatz, which are geographically wedged between the adjacent city blocks of the quarter [[Kreuzberg]], whose neighborhoods [[:de:Berlin SO 36|SO 36]] and [[:de:Kreuzberg 61|Kreuzberg 61]] border to the north and west respectively. After many years of gentrification, the toponym ''Kreuzkölln'' today describes the western part of the Reuterkiez together with the adjacent city blocks of Kreuzberg, more precisely the ''Graefekiez'' and the ''Reichenberger Kiez'', also known as ''Wiener Kiez'', which together have now formed an almost fully integrated, albeit gentrified, social and urban sphere (Johannes Schneider, [https://www.tagesspiegel.de/berlin/namen-fuer-berliner-kieze-sagt-endlich-kreuzkoelln/13422316.html "Sagt endlich Kreuzkölln!"], ''Tagesspiegel'', 12 April 2016).</ref>
==== Urban planning ==== Berlin's official [[urban planning]] framework, on the other hand, divides Berlin's boroughs and quarters into so-called ''Lebensweltlich orientierte Räume'' ("lifeworld-oriented regions"). In this LOR framework, the quarter of Neukölln, non-administrative district 10 in borough 08, as of 2025, is divided into five regions, each of them further compartmentalized into a total of 21 LORs, also called ''Planungsräume'' (planning areas).<ref>[https://www.statistik-berlin-brandenburg.de/publikationen#verzeichnisse "Publikationen: Verzeichnisse] s.v. "Berlin: Adressverzeichnis für die Lebensweltlich orientierten Räume", ''Amt für Statistik Berlin-Brandenburg'', Potsdam, June 2025; download: [https://download.statistik-berlin-brandenburg.de/45d50014cd39990c/4d7dbb679b9e/VZ_Adr_VerzeichnisLOR08_2025_BE.pdf "Adressverzeichnis für die lebensweltlich orientierten Räume: Berlin Neukölln"].</ref>

As of 2024, Berlin ranks among the greenest cities in Germany with only 44.5% of [[Impervious surface|sealed ground]], an average of {{convert|4.24|m3/m2|cuyd/sqyd|abbr=on}} of vegetation, and a 50.6% share of surface area with green space (44%) or water bodies (6.6%), which provides a cooler urban climate and many options for natural habitats and urban recreation.<ref group="note">[https://www.duh.de/presse/pressemitteilungen/pressemitteilung/zu-viel-grau-zu-wenig-gruen-viele-deutsche-staedte-fallen-durch-im-ersten-hitze-check-der-deutschen/ "Zu viel Grau, zu wenig Grün: Viele deutsche Städte fallen durch im ersten Hitze-Check der Deutschen Umwelthilfe"], ''Deutsche Umwelthilfe'', 30 July 2024 (with [https://www.duh.de/fileadmin/user_upload/download/Pressemitteilungen/Kommunal/Hitze-Check_2024/Hitze-Check_Staedte-Deutschland_Uebersicht_240729.pdf download]); the remaining 4.9% are (partially vegetated) mixed-use areas or uncategorized land types.</ref> Similarly, the borough of Neukölln has only 45.6% of sealed ground, mainly due to its extensive recreational parks, which, together with other nature areas, have a share of ca. 43%, with green spaces at 35%, of which 19.9% are parks and meadows, and up to 8% of water surfaces. However, due to the absence of large-scale true forests (''see below''), the borough only has an average of {{convert|3.0|m3/m2|cuyd/sqyd|abbr=on}} of vegetation. Nevertheless, from 2019 to 2024 this was still enough to ensure an average maximum summer midday surface temperature of {{convert|36.01|°C|°F|abbr=on}}, more than one degree Celsius (two degrees Fahrenheit) lower than Berlin's hottest borough of [[Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg]], which measured {{convert|37.22|°C|°F|abbr=on}}. Together with [[Pankow]], this makes Neukölln one of the coolest boroughs in Berlin's inner city, and places it slightly below the class of boroughs that is categorized by an above-average risk of heat exposure for its residents.<ref>[https://www.duh.de/presse/pressemitteilungen/pressemitteilung/zweiter-hitze-check-der-deutschen-umwelthilfe-mehr-als-12-millionen-menschen-in-deutschen-staedten-v/ "Zweiter Hitze-Check der Deutschen Umwelthilfe: Mehr als 12 Millionen Menschen in deutschen Städten von extremer Hitze betroffen"], ''Deutsche Umwelthife'', 12 June 2025, s.v. [https://www.duh.de/fileadmin/user_upload/download/Pressemitteilungen/Kommunal/Hitze-Check_2025/Hitze-Check_Staedte-Bundeslaender_2025_Berlin.pdf "Hitze-Check 2.0 in Deutschlands Städten: Berlin"].</ref> However, average estimates for the northern quarter of Neukölln with 72.5% sealed ground and only 15% green areas would result in a much higher heat exposure compared to the borough's southern quarters, even though at approximately 7.5%, the quarter Neukölln (including Kreuzberg's [[Landwehr Canal]]) has a rather high share of water surface area.
=== Parks and forests === The borough of Neukölln, and especially its homonymous quarter, is densely populated and urbanized, and only has 3.1% (2019) of original natural land and true forests left, second to last before the borough Tempelhof-Schöneberg.<ref>''Amt für Statistik Berlin-Brandenburg'' (31 December 2018) in: [https://www.inforadio.de/dossier/2019/wmr---mobilitaet-in-berlin/platz-da-daten.html "Flächenverteilung in Berlin: 15 Prozent sind Verkehrswege "], ''RBB Inforadio'', 2019.</ref> However, the lack of true forests, which in Neukölln are only 0.1% of the overall surface area, is offset by many green plazas, parks and other vegetated recreational areas at an overall share of 35%, not counting active cemeteries,<ref group="note">Still, due to their old age, many of Neukölln's cemeteries, which are counted as mixed-use areas, now double as recreational parks or tourist locations during opening hours; the most famous and oldest ones are the 1751 Moravian cemetery, the small ''[[:de:Böhmischer Gottesacker Rixdorf|Böhmischer Gottesacker]]'' in Rixdorf, and the 1813 ''Friedhof hinter der Hasenheide'', later renamed ''Garnisonfriedhof'' (garrison cemetery), today called ''[[:de:Friedhof Columbiadamm|Friedhof Columbiadamm]]'', while the largest one, situated between the neighborhoods Rollberg and Körnerpark, is a cemeterial ensemble consisting of ''St. Thomas'', ''Neue Luisenstadt'' and ''St. Michael'', of which the former is one of Neukölln's historical garden monuments, originally built in 1872. Four additional active or former cemeteries are now historical monuments, namely the 1888 ''Emmauskirchhof'' in the far south, ''St. Jacobi'' on Karl-Marx-Straße (1852), the 1866 Turkish cemetery on Columbiadamm (''see below''), and the western part of ''St. Thomas'' (1872), present-day Anita-Berber-Park.</ref> which actually make Neukölln one of the greenest of all Berlin boroughs, even taking the city's top spot with 19.9% of parks and meadows.<ref group="note">[https://www.bz-berlin.de/archiv-artikel/so-gruen-sind-die-berliner-bezirke "So grün sind die Berliner Bezirke"], ''B.Z.'', 9 June 2016. Neukölln also has one of Berlin's 76 remaining natural [[Bog|bogland]]s spanning {{convert|740|ha|km2|abbr=on|3}} or {{convert|1828.6|acre|mi2|abbr=on|2}}, primarily the ''Erlenbruch'', which, together with two smaller bogs, has persisted at the [[Stream#Types|brook]] ''[[:de:Rudower Fließ|Rudower Fließ]]'' in the borough's far south; Hildburg Bruns, [https://www.bz-berlin.de/berlin/moor-krimi-versunkener-see "Moor-Krimi in Köpenick! Versunkener See freigelegt"], ''B.Z.'', 29 May 2025. Recultivated boglands and a herd of [[water buffalo]] are found further east in Neukölln's [[:de:Landschaftspark Rudow-Altglienicke|landscape park Rudow-Altglienicke]].</ref>
Green space in the quarter Neukölln has a relatively low share of approximately 15% and is dominated by small to medium-sized parks, but the two major parks in the western part of Neukölln, the [[Volkspark Hasenheide]] and the [[Tempelhofer Feld]], more than make up for the lack of large green areas in other spots. Smaller parks are found in all neighborhoods, many of which are among Neukölln's historical garden monuments,<ref>For the complete list, cf. [https://www.berlin.de/landesdenkmalamt/denkmale/liste-karte-datenbank/denkmalliste/ "Denkmalliste"] with [https://www.berlin.de/landesdenkmalamt/_assets/pdf-und-zip/denkmale/liste-karte-datenbank/dm_liste.pdf?ts=1712155949 download], ''Landesdenkmalamt Berlin'', Berlin 2023.</ref> for example the [[:de:Anita-Berber-Park|Anita-Berber-Park]] (Schillerpromenade), a former cemetery, and the stadium park of the [[:de:Werner-Seelenbinder-Sportpark|Werner Seelenbinder sporting grounds]], both of which connect to the Tempelhofer Feld, the recently decommissioned cemetery ''Neuer St. Jacobi Friedhof'' (Schillerpromenade), now mostly used as a park, with parts under management by the ''[[:de:Prinzessinnengärten|Prinzessinnengärten]]'' gardening project, [[:de:Lessinghöhe|Lessinghöhe and Thomashöhe]] (Körnerpark), the [[Körnerpark]] itself, a former gravel quarry, with the Rübelandpark connecting Thomashöhe and Körnerpark, the [[:de:Comenius-Garten|Comenius Garden]] (Rixdorf), [[:de:Herbert-Krause-Park|Herbert-Krause-Park]] and [[:de:Von-der-Schulenburg-Park|Von-der-Schulenburg-Park]] near High-Deck-Siedlung, and extensive stretches of garden allotments like ''Helmutstal'' and ''Märkische Schweiz'' close to the quarter's eastern border, including the [[:de:Heidekampgraben#Grünzug Heidekampgraben|Heidekamppark]], a long green corridor adjacent to the trench ''Heidekampgraben''. On the southern and south-western borders to the quarters Britz and Tempelhof respectively is the [[:de:Carl-Weder-Park|Carl-Weder-Park]], a stretched park above the underground ''Stadtring'' autobahn west of the Britzer Damm. Immediately adjacent to the north is the ''Emmauswald'', often called ''Emmi'' by Berliners, a former cemetery and Neukölln's only true natural forest, with the ''Emmauskirchhof'', a still active graveyard, connecting to the east. Furthermore, several inner-city squares and building complexes have been designed with green stretches.<ref group="note">Examples are monuments like the Reuterpark on Reuterplatz or the Richardpark on [[:de:Richardplatz|Richardplatz]], smaller parks like Trusepark, green plazas like Hertzbergplatz, dedicated plaza parks like Weichselpark on Weichselplatz and Wildenbruchpark on Wildenbruchplatz, as well as special places like the [[Atrium (architecture)|atrium]] of Neukölln's ''[[:de:Stadtbad Neukölln|Stadtbad]]'' (public bath house), or the neighborhood garden ''Silent Rixdorf'' in Böhmisch-Rixdorf.</ref>
=== Water bodies === In the borough Neukölln, water bodies are up to 8% of the whole surface area, with the northern quarter at approximately 7.5% trailing behind Rudow and especially Britz, two of Neukölln's four other quarters. Like its parks and forests, with the exception of the ''Emmauswald'', all of Neukölln's water bodies are man-made. Several of the quarter's parks contain artificial lakes and ponds, for example the Volkspark Hasenheide (''Rixdorfer Teich''), the Comenius Garden (''Weltenmeer''), the Karma Culture Garden in Rixdorf, and the Von-Der-Schulenburg-Park (High-Deck-Siedlung). Neukölln's prominent waterway is the [[Neukölln Ship Canal]], which connects the [[Teltow Canal|Teltow]] and [[Britz Canal]]s with the [[Landwehr Canal]] and (through Kreuzberg) the river [[Spree (river)|Spree]]. The [[:de:Hafen Neukölln|Neukölln Harbor]], consisting of an upper and lower basin and connected via the ''[[:de:Schleuse Neukölln|Neukölln Watergate]]'', was built in tandem with the Britz Harbor north of the Teltow Canal. Smaller landing stages are located along the Neukölln Ship Canal until Kiehlufer, and these ''Neukölln Docklands'' are currently subject to extensive redevelopment.
=== Wildlife === Like all of inner-city Berlin, Neukölln, despite its high level of urbanization, has a diverse and thriving population of [[urban wildlife]].<ref>Selmar Schülein, [https://www.fluter.de/tiere-stadtnatur-neukoelln "Die Waschbär-Chroniken"], ''fluter'' '''88''', 22 September 2023.</ref> The quarter's sufficient share of vegetation, parks and other green areas (''see above'') not only provides a cooler urban climate, but also promotes the settlement of wildlife. Wild species in Neukölln have usually found their safe retreats along the waterways and in the bigger parks and cemeteries, while using migration routes into the central neighborhoods along train tracks and through the interconnected park and cemetery areas, for example from Tempelhofer Feld to Lessinghöhe. Greener quarters adjacent to Neukölln in the south and east also promote migration into the quarter.
Neukölln's nature and wildlife are primarily managed by [[Park ranger|ranger]]s from Berlin's ''[[:de:Stiftung Naturschutz Berlin|Stiftung Naturschutz]]'' (Charity for Nature Conservation). Political measures over the past years have improved natural habitats and the ecological component of Berlin's path toward a more [[sustainable development]]. Among them are a strict urban tree planting and replacement policy, an emphasis on discreetly controlled rank growth, both in parks and on [[median strip]]s, protected nature areas in larger parks, and more neighborhood-oriented action like [[roof garden]]s, for example the ''Kranichgarten'' at the ''Neukölln Arcaden'', vegetated and partially fenced ''[[:de:Baumscheiben|Baumscheiben]]'' around road trees instead of [[tree grate]]s, as well as vegetated [[parklet]]s. The Tempelhofer Feld is home to several protected and endangered plant and animal species like the [[Calliptamus italicus|Italian locust]] and the [[Leptidea sinapis|wood white butterfly]]. Almost half of the vast park's bird species are on the list of highly [[endangered species]], among them the [[whinchat]] and the [[Northern wheatear|wheatear]].<ref>Juliana Schlaberg, [https://berlin.nabu.de/stadt-und-natur/stadtentwicklung/tempelhofer-feld/geschichte.html "Akte Tempelhof – Einmal Freiheit und zurück"], ''[[Naturschutzbund Deutschland]]'', 8 May 2014</ref>
Generally, Neukölln's wildlife is no different from that of other inner-city quarters of Berlin, so [[red fox]]es, rabbits and smaller [[rodent]] species like the [[red squirrel]] and several [[muroidea]] as well as urban birds like [[dove]]s, [[crow]]s and (on the quarter's canals) [[swan]]s, [[Goose|geese]] and ducks are almost ubiquitous. Less noticeable species include the [[badger]], the [[beech marten]], the [[hedgehog]], [[Microbat|bats]], the [[true toad]] and other species of frog. In recent years, [[Eurasian otter|otter]]s and [[beaver]]s have also made a comeback in Neukölln, for example at the Landwehr Canal.<ref>Victoria Atanasov, [https://www.morgenpost.de/bezirke/neukoelln/article237027035/Biber-nagen-an-Baeumen-am-Landwehrkanal.html "Biber und Baum am Landwehrkanal müssen geschützt werden"], ''Berliner Morgenpost'', 29 November 2022.</ref> To date, no sightings of [[wild boar]] have been recorded for the quarter Neukölln.<ref group="note">[https://pardok.parlament-berlin.de/starweb/adis/citat/VT/18/SchrAnfr/S18-13670.pdf "Schriftliche Anfrage der Abgeordneten Danny Freymark (CDU) und Stephan Schmidt (CDU) vom 01. März 2018 zum Thema 'Wildschweinplage in Berliner Bezirken' und Antwort vom 14. März 2018"], ''Abgeordnetenhaus Berlin'', 14 March 2018. However, an average of 50–100 kills of wild boar are usually registered every year in the ''borough'' of Neukölln. Generally, boars only seldomly migrate into Berlin's inner-city quarters, which would need to be directly interconnected with the large forests in Berlin's periphery, for example like the western parts of [[Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf]]; for sightnings of wild boar on the [[Kurfürstendamm]], see e.g. Cay Dobberke, [https://www.tagesspiegel.de/berlin/schweine-am-kudamm-7033612.html "Wildschweinplage: Schweine am Ku'damm"], ''Tagesspiegel'', 25 March 2011.</ref>
While the plain and unadorned surfaces of modern architecture have all but pushed out traditional urban bird species like the [[common swift]] and the [[Western house martin|house martin]], Berlin, unlike other German cities, is still a safe haven for many others. A prominent example is the [[Old World sparrow|sparrow]], and Berlin is now regarded as the "sparrow capital" of Germany.<ref>Astrid Kinateder in: [https://www.sowohntberlin.de/meine-stadt/wildtiere-in-berlin/ "Wildtiere in Berlin: Artenvielfalt im Hinterhof"], ''So wohnt Berlin'' ([[Gewobag]]), 11 August 2022.</ref> [[Bird of prey|Predatory birds]], though common to Berlin,<ref>[https://www.tip-berlin.de/ausfluege/waelder/greifvoegel-berlin-umland/ "Greifvögel in Berlin und Brandenburg: Da flattert einiges"], ''Tip'', 12 October 2021.</ref> are not native to the quarter of Neukölln, but sometimes intrude from other peripheral areas, for example the [[common kestrel]] from the borough's southern quarters. Rank growth and gardening policies have been the basis for a slight revival of the urban insect population, including endangered or almost extinct species like the [[vine weevil]], which also reattracts bird species to the urbanized areas. Furthermore, Neukölln is now home to 300 species of [[Bee|wild bees]].
Like all cities in today's [[Globalization|globalized]] world, Berlin and Neukölln are also home to several [[invasive species]] like the [[raccoon]] or the [[Impatiens glandulifera|Himalayan balsam]].<ref>For an overview, ''see'' e.g. [https://www.stiftung-naturschutz.de/naturschutz/koordinierungsstelle-fauna/invasive-arten "Invasive Arten"], ''Stiftung Naturschutz Berlin'', with [https://www.stiftung-naturschutz.de/fileadmin/user_upload/pdf/Faunenschutz/Invasive_Arten_Berlin_201904.pdf "Invasive Arten in Berlin"], April 2019.</ref> Neukölln in particular has a fairly large population of [[nutria]] and [[muskrat]]. Several foreign species of fish and [[crustacean]]s have settled in Berlin's waterways, and have markedly shifted the balance of indigenous species. Especially the population of [[Procambarus clarkii|red swamp crayfish]] has risen sharply in the past decade, including in the Landwehr Canal, and the reintroduction of eels into Berlin's waterways is planned as a countermeasure.<ref>Jörn Hasselmann, [https://www.tagesspiegel.de/berlin/aale-sollen-invasive-art-in-berlin-bandigen-us-sumpfkrebse-gibts-jetzt-auch-im-teltowkanal-9526823.html "Aale sollen invasive Art in Berlin bändigen – US-Sumpfkrebse gibt's jetzt auch im Teltowkanal"], ''Der Tagesspiegel'', 19 March 2023.</ref>
== Etymologies == {{Gallery
|width=149 |align=center
|File:Tempelritter-Schild.jpg|Knights Templar cross, [[:de:Dorfkirche Tempelhof|Templar village church]], Tempelhof
|File:RichardusCornwall.jpg |[[Richard of Cornwall]]
|File:Blason famille fr de Bures.svg |[[Richard de Bures]], family coat of arms
|File:Saint_Richard.jpg |[[Richard of Chichester|St. Richard of Chichester]]
|Coat of arms de-be neu-cölln.png |[[Cölln|Neu-Cölln am Wasser]], coat of arms
}}
=== Rixdorf: Richard's village === At the time of its official foundation in 1360 as a [[Knights Hospitaller]] ''[[angerdorf]]'', Rixdorf was called ''Richarsdorp'' (''Richardsdorf'', "Richard's Village"), while a proposed earlier toponym for the preceding [[Knights Templar]] [[Hamlet (place)|hamlet]] is ''*Richardshof'' ("Richard's Court").<ref group="note">Heike Schroll, ''Das Landesarchiv Berlin und seine Bestände: Übersicht der Bestände aus der Zeit bis 1945 (Tektonik-Gruppe A)'', Berliner Wissenschaftsverlag, Berlin, 2003, p. 134; in [[Low German]] the toponym would have been ''Richarshove''; compare [[Tempelhof]] as ''Tempelhove''. A potential date for the emergence of this hypothetical toponym is the rededication of the military stronghold as an access yard (ca. 1245); however, the Templar yard or court could as well have been called ''Richarsdorp'' from the beginning, as that was already its toponym at the time of the village's official foundation in 1360. The foundational charter emphasizes the legal elevation to village status, and does not mention any renaming, earlier toponyms or planned extensions; furthermore, even as a yard, Richardsdorf had for a long time had several facilities and buildings (Eugen Brode, ''Geschichte Rixdorfs'', Rixdorf 1899, p. 4 sq.), so the designation as a ''Dorf'' ("village") is more likely even for its Templar era before the legal status change in 1360.</ref> Two alternate [[Low German]] spellings, ''Richarstorp'' and ''Richardstorff'', are already present in the foundational charter. The village's name was usually pronounced with mostly [[Elision|elided]] or [[High German consonant shift|shifted]] consonants ({{IPA|de|rˈɪkasdˌɔɾp|lang}}). Alternate spellings of the 14th century were ''Richardsdorp'' and ''Richardstorpp'', while the 15th century introduced ''Reicherstorff'', ''Richerstorp'' and ''Rigerstorp'' (1435).
From the 16th century onward, vernacularly [[Contraction (grammar)|contracted]] forms took hold.<ref group="note">Compare e.g. engl. ''Richard'' or ''Rickard'' → ''Rick''.</ref> ''Ricksdorf'' (1525) became a widely accepted spelling for two centuries, with many alternate forms appearing in historical records, for example ''Reichstorff'' (1541), ''Richstorff'', ''Rigstorff'' (1542) and ''Richsdorf'' (1543), and in the 17th and 18th century ''Rechsdorff'', ''Rechsdorp'', ''Risdorf'', ''Reichsdorp'', ''Rieksdorf'', ''Ryksdorf'', ''Riecksdorff'' (1693) and ''Riechsdorf'' (1737). The earliest known source for ''Rixdorf'' is from the year 1709, and it became the official modern [[High German languages|High German]] spelling in 1797. Still, the central historical localities ''[[:de:Richardplatz|Richardplatz]]'' and ''[[:de:Richardstraße (Berlin)|Richardstraße]]'' have retained the town's original etymology, and were never renamed to ''Rixplatz'' ("Rix Plaza") or ''Rixstraße'' ("Rix Road").<ref group="note">This is because the ''x'' in ''Rix-'' also originated from the original toponym's [[Genitive case#German|genitive ''s'']] (''Richard'''s'''dorf'', "Richard''''s''' Village"), which was never part of the plaza's and street's toponyms.</ref>
The mainstream theory on the [[etymology]] of ''Richardsdorf'', and therefore of ''Rixdorf'', assumes an [[eponym]]ous individual called ''Richard'',<ref group="note">Main contemporary spellings were e.g. ''Richart'' and ''Rikard'' or latinized variants ''Ricardus'' and ''Richardus''. Generally, the name ''[[Richard]]'' means "strong ruler" and stems from the [[Proto-Germanic language|Proto-Germanic]] roots ''*rīk-'' ("ruler", "leader") and ''*hardu-'' ("hardy", "strong"), originally from [[Proto-Indo-European language|PIE]] ''*reg-'' ("to move in a straight line", "to lead", "to rule") and ''*kar-'' ("hard"). The syllable ''-dorp'' (''Dorf'', [[Old English]] ''thorp'', "village") stems from the Proto-Germanic root ''*thurpa-'' ("pen", "compound", "fenced area"), and possily from PIE ''*treb-'' ("dwelling"). Taking into account the proposed older toponym ''*Richardshof'', the word ''Hof'' (''hove'') is a [[cognate]] of ''Hufe'' ("[[oxgang]]") from Proto-Germanic ''*hōfaz'' and the root ''*hōfa-'' ("hoof"), possibly originating from PIE ''*kop-'' ("to beat", "to strike").</ref> allegedly a local Knight Templar, [[bailiff]] or commander of the [[:de:Komturei Tempelhof|Tempelhof commandery]], or even the original administrator or chief settler (''[[:de:Lokator|Lokator]]'') of *Richardshof in the early 13th to 14th century. However, no ''Richard'' is mentioned in historical sources in connection with the Templar villages of the [[Teltow (region)|Teltow]], let alone an actual [[List of Knights Templar#Germany|Knight Templar]] ''per Alemanniam et Slaviam'', so alternate etymologies were eventually propagated,<ref group="note">A prominent contemporary Richard with at least indirect connections to the Templar Order was [[Richard of Cornwall]] (died 1272), who in 1241 during the [[Barons' Crusade]] negotiated a proper burial for the Templars killed at the [[Battle at Gaza (1239)|Battle at Gaza]], and who also transferred sovereignty over [[Ashkelon|Ascalon castle]] to the German king [[Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor|Frederick II]], who had already laid the groundwork for the German colonization of the Teltow region after the [[Northern Crusades]] (''see below''); Richard became [[King of the Romans]] (and therefore of Germany) in 1257, however against the vote by Brandenburg's Margrave [[John I, Margrave of Brandenburg|John I]]. The only prominent contemporary Knight Templar called Richard was Richart (Richardus) de Bures ([[Richard de Bures]]), who, at the time when Rixdorf's original Templar stronghold was rededicated as an access yard (ca. 1245, ''see below''), served as [[List of grand masters of the Knights Templar|Templar grand master]] after the death or capture of his predecessor [[Armand de Périgord]] in 1244, and who himself died a Templar martyr on 9 May 1247 during the battle at [[Sea of Galilee|Lake Tiberias]], which was followed by the destruction of [[Tiberias]]. However, only the former of these two Richards has ever been proposed in ''Richardsdorf'' etymologies.</ref> while exaggerating [[Folk etymology|folk etymologies]] also emerged and tried to connect the toponym to important historical or [[Canonization|canonized]] Richards.<ref group="note">Among them were nobleman and Christian saint [[Richard the Pilgrim]] (died 720), famous crusader king [[Richard I of England|Richard the Lionheart]] (died 1199), and primarily bishop [[Richard of Chichester]] (died 1253), who was canonized in 1262 and eventually became a popular saint in Europe, but possibly too late to have influenced the toponym ''Richarsdorp''.</ref> In modern times, alternate spellings like ''Reichsdorp'' spawned secondary folk etymologies different from ''Richard'', namely from ''Reich'' ("empire") or the surname ''Reich'' or ''Ryk'', but without taking the documented earlier variants and vernacular contractions into account.<ref group="note">The latter hypothesis was presented in [[Christoph Friedrich Nicolai|Friedrich Nicolai]], ''Beschreibung der königlichen Residenzstädte Berlin und Potsdam'', Berlin 1779–86, Vol. 3 (1786), p. 1047, s.v. "I. Anhang: Umgebung von Berlin – Ryksdorf, Britz – IV. Vor dem Kottbusserthore"; Nicolai propagated an origin from the ''Ryke'' (''Reiche'') family, one of [[Cölln]]'s and [[Alt-Berlin|Berlin]]'s old patrician dynasties (''see also'' [[:de:Rykestraße|Rykestraße]]), who allegedly had founded the hamlet in the 13th century. Apart from the toponym's known etymological progression from ''Richardsdorf'', the theory created chronological problems, because the Ryke history in Tempelhof only began in the 14th century: in 1344, Johannes Ryke, a patrician citizen of Cölln, had been given the ''[[schultheiß]]'' court of [[Marienfelde]] south of [[Tempelhof]] by ''Herrenmeister'' (Lord of the Knights) [[:de:Hermann von Werberg|Hermann von Werberg]], bailiff and ''Statthalter'' (Governor) of Brandenburg, who in turn would play an important role in the official foundation of Richardsdorf (''see below''), which might have been the impetus for Nicolai's alternate theory; this, however, demanded that the Ryke family's local political influence needed to be backdated by at least a century (Eugen Brode, ''Geschichte Rixdorfs'', Rixdorf 1899, p. 6). Generally, for the theories on Neukölln's ''Rixdorf'', no primary sources exist that would refute the ''Richard'' etymology and corroborate a clear distinction between the later contracted form ''Rixdorf'' and its sufficiently documented toponymic origin as ''Richarsdorp''.</ref> Ideas of a [[Proto-Slavic language|Slavic]] origin were non-academic and anecdotal at best, and are disregarded in modern literature.<ref group="note">One etymological hypothesis surmised an origin from Slavic ''*rъžь'' ("rye"), leading to ''Rixdorf'' as ''Roggendorf'' ("rye village"); alternatively, the Slavic ''*rykati'' (''ricas'', "to growl") was said to be a possible etymological root. However, Slavic origin theories had already been rejected in the 19th century; cf. Eugen Brode, ''Geschichte Rixdorfs'', Rixdorf 1899, p. 6; Gundakkar Klaussen, "Rixdorf, Preußens jüngste Stadt", in: Adolf Kröner (ed.), ''Die Gartenlaube'' '''8''', Leipzig 1899, p. 246 sq.</ref> Instead, ''Rixdorf'' is regarded as a classic example of a toponym based on a founder's or important leader's name, comparable to other localities of Berlin like [[Heinersdorf]] (from ''Hinrick'', [[Heinrich (given name)|Heinrich]]) and [[Reinickendorf (locality)|Reinickendorf]] (from ''Reginhard'', ''Reinicke'', [[Reinhard]]).
=== Neukölln: the new colony === In the 12th and early 13th centuries, during the time of [[Albert the Bear]]'s and his successors' foundational advances into the region of modern-day Brandenburg and Berlin, [[Latin]] had been the administrative ''[[lingua franca]]'' of the [[Middle Ages|Mediaeval]] [[Holy Roman Empire]]. The [[Ostsiedlung|immigrating colonists]] mostly spoke [[German dialects|dialects]] of the [[German language|German common language]],<ref group="note">''See also'' [[Theodiscus]]; ''Deutsche'', unlike the English or French terms ''Germans'' or ''Allemands'' seem to suggest, was never an ethnic term, and instead denoted the (common) people who spoke ''theodiscus'' (''deutsch''), the language "of the people"; to this day, the individual term for a German is therefore ''Deutscher'' ("common person", "person who speaks the [common] ''deutsche'' language"), derived from the original adjective ''deutsch'' ([[Old High German|O.H.G.]] ''diutisc''), not from the later noun and toponym ''Deutschland'' as ''Deutschländer'' ("citizen of ''Deutschland''"), which is only (and only occasionally) used as a [[Neologism|neologistical]] term for [[Turks in Germany]] or as a sometimes pejorative ''pars pro toto'' for all German citizens or residents with a [[migration background]].</ref> while the [[Hevelli]] and [[Sprevane]], the [[Slavs|Slavic]] tribes who had replaced the original [[Germanic peoples|Germanic natives]] of the region, spoke [[West Slavic languages]], for example [[Polabian language|Old-Polabian]]. For this reason, the toponyms of many of Berlin's localities have a Slavic origin, including Berlin itself, whose name, in spite of its earliest Latin form as ''Berolinum'',<ref group="note">See also the city's female [[personification]] as ''[[Berolina]]''.</ref> possibly stems from [[Proto-Slavic language|Proto-Slavic]] ''*berl-/*brl-'', an obscure root which is usually interpreted as "bog", "moor" or "swamp".<ref group="note">The Old-Polabian toponym ''Berlin'' or ''Birlin'' ("swamp area") was also used with a definite article as ''der Berlin'' ("the Berlin"), which strongly suggests a Slavic plot name, e.g. "the swamp", instead of a preceding Slavic settlement; cf. Arnt Cobbers, ''Kleine Berlin-Geschichte – Vom Mittelalter bis zur Gegenwart'', Berlin 2008, p. 14; ''see also'' [[History of Berlin#Etymology|etymology of ''Berlin'']].</ref>
The name ''Neukölln'', however, is in many ways an exception to Berlin's toponymic rules. When Rixdorf was rechristened ''Neukölln'' in 1912, the city's new name was a catch-all term. It logically referenced several places in the vicinity, namely the [[:de:Köllnische Heide|Cölln Heath]] to the east, as well as [[Cölln]] itself, [[Alt-Berlin]]'s historical twin city, which had been Rixdorf's feudal parent city for several generations (''see below''). The primary reference, however, was to the ''Neucöllner Siedlungen'' (Neucölln Estates), which had been constructed on the ''Berlinische Wiesen'' north of the old Rixdorf in the decades before the renaming. The estates' name recalled the meadows' old name ''Cöllnische Wiesen'' (Cölln Meadows), and thereby, whether intentionally or not, imitated [[:de:Neu-Kölln|Neu-Cölln]], an old district south of the medieval part of Berlin and Cölln proper. This historical Neu-Cölln, sometimes written ''Neu-Cöln'' or ''neu Cölln'', was at first also called ''Neu-Cölln am Wasser'' ("New Cölln by the water").<ref group="note">The "water" referenced both Neu-Cölln's southern military moat, which was later filled, and the historical ''Cöllnischer Stadtgraben'' ("Cölln Moat") between Cölln and Neu-Cölln, which later became the [[:de:Spreekanal|Spree Canal]] west and south of [[:de:Spreeinsel|Spree Island]]; ''see also'' [[Fischerinsel]], the southern neighborhood on Spree Island.</ref> It was built in 1662 as the southern military extension of the city of Cölln, and remained a small district of Berlin until the [[Greater Berlin Act]] of 1920, when it was dissolved in the new [[Mitte (locality)|Mitte]] quarter of the [[Mitte|homonymous borough]].<ref group="note">This means that from 1912 to 1920, the region had two localities that bore virtually the same name, Berlin's small district ''Neu-Kölln'' and vis-à-vis the city ''Neukölln''. Today, the area of the historical Neu-Cölln is part of the Luisenstadt neighborhood (named after the [[Luisenstadt|historical Luisenstadt]]), and it is almost never called by its historical name, but places like the [[Köllnischer Park]], originally a military bastion in Neu-Cölln (''Bastion VII''), still point to the origin of the toponym ''Neukölln''.</ref>
The etymology of ''Cölln'', and therefore of ''Neukölln'' ("New Cölln"), is from imperial Latin ''colonia'' ("colony", "settlement", "colonial town"),<ref group="note">From Latin ''colonus'' (i.a. "settler in a foreign land") and ''colere'' (i.a. "to cultivate", "to inhabit"), originally from the [[Proto-Indo-European language|PIE]] root ''*kwel-'' (i.a. "to dwell").</ref> and the colonial town in Brandenburg was at first called ''Colonia'' (1237)<ref group="note">In a reference to a priest as ''Symeon plebanus de Colonia'' (28 October 1237).</ref> and ''Colonia juxta Berlin'' (1247, "colony near Berlin"). In the same manner as the toponym of modern-day ''Köln'' ([[Cologne]]), the former [[Roman Empire|Roman]] [[Colonia Claudia Ara Agrippinensium]] (50 [[Common Era|CE]]) and ''Colonia Agrippina'' (322), was gradually [[Germanization|germanized]] from post-Roman ''Colonia'' (since 450) to its Mediaeval names ''Colne'', ''Coellen'', and ''Cölln'', so was Brandenburg's [[Holy Roman Empire|Holy Roman]] ''Colonia'', first partially to ''Colne prope Berlin'' (1344, "Colne near Berlin"), then to ''Collen'' (1440), before settling on ''Cölln'' in later centuries.<ref group="note">Manfred Niemeyer (ed.), ''Deutsches Ortsnamenbuch'', Berlin/Boston 2012, p. 60 s.v. "Berlin: II, III"; id., p. 323 s.v. "Köln: III, IV"; cf. Winfried Schich, "Das mittelalterliche Berlin (1237–1411)", in: Wolfgang Ribbe, ''Geschichte Berlins'' '''1''', München 1988, p. 146–206. A direct adoption of Cologne's Latin name (Joachim Herrmann [1937] in: id. ''et al.'', ''Berlin. Ergebnisse der heimatkundlichen Bestandsaufnahme, Reihe "Werte unserer Heimat"'' '''49/50''', Berlin 1987, p. 143 sq.) is therefore less likely than a new Holy Roman colony next to (the) Berlin receiving its toponym ''Colonia'' independently, which was then gradually germanized like its counterpart on the [[Rhine]]. An even older hypothesis, which mirrored Berlin's etymology and tried to postulate a Slavic origin of the new colony's name, surmised an obscure base form ''*Kol'no'' from ''*kol'' ("stake, spigot"); cf. Emil Stutzer, ''Die Deutschen Großstädte – Einst und Jetzt'', Berlin/Braunschweig/Hamburg 1917; this is an unlikely etymology, especially given the fact that no remnants of a Slavic settlement preceding the new ''colonia'' were ever found, but still cannot be completely ruled out; cf. Manfred Niemeyer (ed.), ''Deutsches Ortsnamenbuch'', Berlin/Boston 2012, p. 60 s.v. "Berlin: II, III". Older treatises had disregarded a potential Slavic in favor of a Latin origin as a ''[[sui generis]]'' name for a ''colonia nova'' (new colony), but left open the possibility that the colonists had been inspired by Cologne's Latin name; cf. i.a. Jan Hendrik Regenbogen, ''Commentatio de Bello Sacro'', Leiden 1819, p. 245 sq.: "Coloniam ad Spream quoque ea aetate conditam fuisse, ita nominatum vel a Colonia nova, vel ab advenis, e Colonia Agrippina oriundis, admodum est verisimile […]." It was a common, but never verified belief among the people until the early 18th century that many colonists had migrated to Brandenburg's ''Colonia'' directly from Cologne on the Rhine, which spawned the secondary hypothesis that at least the later Germanized spelling ''Cölln'' was inspired by Rhenish ''Cölln'' (Cologne); Ernst Fidicin (ed.), ''Historisch-diplomatische Beiträge zur Geschichte der Stadt Berlin'', Vol. 3, Berlin 1837, [https://archive.org/details/historischdiplom03fidiuoft/page/3/mode/1up p. 3 sq., n. 3].</ref> Latin literature kept referring to Cölln as ''Colonia''. To distinguish it from Cologne, toponym extensions were often applied, for example ''Marchiae'' ("of the [[March (territory)|March]] [Brandenburg]"), or ''ad Spream'', ''ad Spreeam'' or ''ad Spreham'' ("on the [river] [[Spree (river)|Spree]]"), or ''Brandenburgica'' ("[[Margraviate of Brandenburg|Brandenburgian]]").<ref>Johann Georg Theodor Graesse, Friedrich Benedict, Helmut Piechl (ed.), Sophie-Charlotte Piechl (ed.), ''Orbis Latinus – Lexikon lateinischer geographischer Namen des Mittelalters und der Neuzeit'' '''1''', Braunschweig 1972, p. 550 s.v. [https://daten.digitale-sammlungen.de/bsb00050912/image_552 "Colonia Brandenburgica"].</ref> Even though neither ''Neukölln'' nor the historical ''Neu-Cölln'' were ever officially called ''[[:la:Nova Colonia|Nova Colonia]]'',<ref group="note">Similar to ''[[Berolina#Name|Berolina]]'', the name of Berlin's historical personification, the ahistorical toponym ''Nova Colonia'' is used occasionally by associations and companies in the Neukölln quarter and borough, for example the [[chamber choir]] ''Capella Nova Colonia'', while the variant ''Colonia Nova'' is the name of an event venue and congress center in northern Rixdorf, situated very close to the area of the historical ''Neucölln Estates''.</ref> etymologically ''Neukölln'' still translates as "New Colony", which was a fitting new name for a city that since the first German colonists of the early 13th century until today has always been a prominent destination for settlers and immigrants.

=== Prehistory === [[File:-510 Reitergrab von Neukölln anagoria.JPG|thumb|right|Germanic equestrian tomb of Neukölln]]
Archeological finds on the Rixdorf lot point to a [[Germanic peoples|Germanic]] [[prehistory]] of Neukölln,<ref group="note">Prehistorical overview and historical chronology, settlement history and monuments i.a. in ''Berlin-Neukölln, seine Geschichte und Denkmale: Rixdorf'', Bezirksamt Neukölln, Berlin 1999 ([https://www.neukoelln-online.de/denkmale/rixdorf/inhalt.htm online copy]); for more detailed annals of Rixdorf and Neukölln from its inception to 1999, cf. Manfred Motel, ''Chronik von Rixdorf'', Berlin 1999 ([http://www.christa-bahr.de/rixdorf.html online copy]).</ref> with evidence of a settlement since the late [[Neolithic]] age, like early [[flint]] tools, potsherds from the [[Bronze Age]] on Richardplatz, or [[Iron Age Europe|Iron Age]] burial urns in the ''Hasenheide''.<ref>Gundakkar Klaussen, "Rixdorf, Preußens jüngste Stadt", in: Adolf Kröner (ed.), ''Die Gartenlaube'' '''8''', Leipzig 1899, p. 246 sq.</ref> Finds from the era of the [[Roman Empire]] were ubiquitous in Berlin, which includes West-Germanic [[terp]]s and ceramics on the Richardplatz, a [[Gordian I|Gordianic]] bronze coin on the ''Rollberge'',<ref group="note">Originally minted in [[Singidunum]], it was either a coeval monetary import or reused in more recent times as a pendant.</ref> and nearby the important ''[[Körnerpark#Equestrian tomb of Neukölln|Reitergrab von Neukölln]]'' (equestrian tomb) south-west of Richardplatz at the Körnerpark, which stems from the onset of the [[Merovingian dynasty|Merowingian era]] in the first half of the 6th century.<ref group="note">I.a. Albert Kiekebusch, "Ein germanisches Reitergrab aus der späten Völkerwanderungszeit von Neukölln (Rixdorf) bei Berlin", ''Praehistorische Zeitschrift'' '''IV 3/4''', 1912. Today, the ''Reitergrab'' is on display at the [[Märkisches Museum]].</ref>
The original tribes that lived in the Berlin region belonged to the [[Elbe Germanic peoples|Elbe-Germanic]] [[Suebi|Suevian]] [[Semnones]]. They eventually migrated southwestward during the era of the [[Migration Period|Barbarian Invasions]]<ref group="note">Starting in the third century CE, almost all of the Semnonic tribes migrated from ''[[Germania|Germania Magna]]'' across the ''[[Limes (Roman Empire)|limes]]'' to the [[Upper Rhine]] region and became part of the wider and partially [[Roman Empire|Romanized]] [[Alemanni]]c culture; ''see also'' [[Swabia]] and [[Alamannia]].
